TITLE: Vulnerability and adaptation options to climate change for rural livelihoods â€“ A country-wide analysis for Uganda
AUTHORS: Jannike Wichern, Katrien Descheemaeker, Ken E. Giller, Peter Ebanyat, Godfrey Taulya, Mark T. van Wijk
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Uganda
METHODS: Developed a multi-level vulnerability framework linking crop suitability models (Ecocrop) with household food availability data (from LSMS-ISA) to assess vulnerability to climate scenarios across Uganda. Analysis included eight key crops and examined impacts of temperature and rainfall changes. Also tested eight adaptation options based on suitability and household dependence.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Applies multiple simplified climate scenarios (e.g., +3Â°C and âˆ’10% rainfall) to identify sensitivity and adaptive potential, particularly for key crops and regions across Uganda.
THEMES/FINDINGS: Four vulnerability hotspots were identified (West Nile, Central North, Southwest, Central Southwest). â€¨â€¨Climate impacts vary by crop and region; adaptation options such as crop substitution and improved varieties show potential but vary in household-level effectiveness.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Excludes non-crop income activities like livestock and off-farm work in modeling; doesn't incorporate extreme events; limited climate variable resolution; focuses only on a pessimistic climate scenario.
NOTES: Framework is scalable and adaptable, allowing for integration with broader data (e.g., RHoMIS) and future socioeconomic or climate data. Highlights need for context-specific, targeted policy and investment in adaptation.
Abstract: Rural households in sub-Saharan Africa earn a substantial part of their living from rain-fed smallholder agriculture, which is highly sensitive to climate change. The framework combines crop suitability maps with a household food availability analysis to quantify household vulnerability to climate-related impacts on crop production and effects of adaptation options. The framework was tested for Uganda, identifying four hotspots of household vulnerability across the country. About 30% of households in the (central) southwest hotspots were vulnerable to a 3°C temperature increase and 10% rainfall decline through declining suitability for several key crops including highland banana, cassava, maize and sorghum. Options for adaptation to increasing temperatures were most beneficial in northern Uganda, while drought-related adaptation options were more beneficial in the southwest.
Keywords: Climate change, Adaptation, Vulnerability, Food security, Uganda, Smallholder agriculture

TITLE: Spodoptera eridania: Current and emerging crop threats from another invasive, pesticide-resistant moth
AUTHORS: Jessica Weinberg, Noboru Ota, Georg Goergen, Johannes Romuald Fagbohoun, Ghislain T. Tepa-Yotto, Darren J. Kriticos
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Global, with emphasis on Sub-Saharan Africa, Americas, and Asia
METHODS: Developed a CLIMEX bioclimatic niche model for Spodoptera eridania based on climate, host crop distribution, and potential future climate conditions (RCP 8.5 for 2050). Overlayed global distribution of major host crops (beans, soybeans, tomato, cotton, sweet potato, etc.) to identify high-risk zones for pest establishment and migration.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Applies a 2050 RCP 8.5 scenario to predict future pest range and sensitivity; adaptation needs and risks under warming climates are central.
THEMES/FINDINGS: S. eridania threatens major global crops and is likely to expand poleward by ~200â€“250 km under climate change. â€¨â€¨Risk increases where irrigation creates suitable habitat. Pesticide resistance and lack of control infrastructure exacerbate the threat.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Limited African presence data to validate model; assumes species biology consistent across continents; uncertainties in climate projections and crop distribution layers.
NOTES: Recommends coordinated global monitoring and phytosanitary controls; notes the additive threat with other pests like Tuta absoluta on tomatoes.
Abstract: Spodoptera eridania (Stoll), a polyphagous lepidopteran pest from the Americas, has recently invaded western and central Africa. Like its congeners, S. eridania has developed pesticide resistance. The researchers fit a CLIMEX niche model for S. eridania and applied a climate change scenario for 2050 to investigate the sensitivity of the pest threat. The study found that S. eridania can potentially expand its range throughout the tropics and into the sub-tropics, threatening a range of important commercial and subsistence crops. Modelled climatic changes will mostly expand this species potential range poleward by around 200 km by 2050. These areas of emerging potential expansion are mostly into subtropical climates, supporting diverse cropping systems, including at risk crops beans, groundnut, potato, soybeans, tomato and sweet potato.
Keywords: Spodoptera eridania, invasive species, pesticide resistance, CLIMEX modeling, climate change, crop threats, Africa

TITLE: Temporal Changes in Minimum and Maximum Temperatures at Selected Locations of Southern Africa
AUTHORS: Walter Mupangwa, Lovemore Chipindu, Bongani Ncube, Siyabusa Mkuhlani, Nascimento Nhantumbo, Esther Masvaya, Amos Ngwira, Mokhele Moeletsi, Isaiah Nyagumbo, Feyera Liben
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Southern Africa â€” Malawi, Mozambique, South Africa, Zimbabwe
METHODS: Daily minimum and maximum temperature data from 23 meteorological stations across four countries were analyzed for trends using the Modified Mannâ€“Kendall and Theilâ€“Sen slope estimators. Data were tested for homogeneity and corrected for serial correlation. The study spanned 33â€“79 years depending on the location.
IMPACT AREAS:
Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Focuses heavily on observed and projected warming trends. Stresses the urgent need to integrate temperature trends into adaptation planning, particularly for crops, livestock, and water systems.
THEMES/FINDINGS: Minimum temperature increased more variably and strongly than maximum temperature. â€¨â€¨Semi-arid zones saw greatest change. Impacts span from early senescence in crops to pest proliferation and livestock stress. â€¨â€¨Temperature changes require urgent inclusion in climate resilience strategies.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Data gaps in certain stations; did not account for rainfall interaction in trend analysis; limited exploration of specific agricultural productivity outcomes.
NOTES: Study provides a strong empirical foundation for informing national climate-smart agricultural policies and adaptation measures in southern Africa.
Abstract: The study analyzed minimum and maximum temperatures' variability and trends using daily time series data from 23 meteorological stations across Malawi, Mozambique, South Africa and Zimbabwe, employing modified Mann–Kendall and Theil–Sen slope models. Temperature varied with location, with minimum temperature being more variable than maximum temperature, and semi-arid regions showing higher variation in minimum temperature compared to humid and coastal environments. The results showed an upward trend in minimum (0.01–0.83 °C over a 33–38 year period) and maximum (0.01–0.09 °C over a 38–57 year period) temperatures at 9 and 15 locations, respectively.
Keywords: climate change; global warming; heat stress; smallholder agriculture; temperature variability

TITLE: Zoom in at African country level: potential climate-induced changes in areas of suitability for survival of malaria vectors
AUTHORS: Henri E.Z. Tonnang, David P. Tchouassi, Henry S. Juarez, Lilian K. Igweta, Rousseau F. Djouaka
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Africa (continental scope, with country-level analysis across all African nations)
METHODS: CLIMEX was used to simulate current and future distributions of Anopheles gambiae and An. arabiensis based on climatic variables (temperature, rainfall, humidity). Two climate change scenarios were applied. Results were processed in ArcGIS to compute absolute area changes (in kmÂ²) per country in vector suitability zones. The eco-climatic index (EI) was reclassified into five classes of vector survival likelihood and change was quantified per country under both scenarios.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate change is the central driver in the analysis. The study uses IPCC-based projections and focuses on adaptation responses. No discussion of climate mitigation or emissions.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
An. gambiae and An. arabiensis will expand into new zones in Eastern and Southern Africa

West African risk may decline slightly due to drying trends

Country-specific maps and risk values provided

Recommendations include cross-border malaria surveillance, information systems, and adaptive control strategies

Climate variables used: temperature, rainfall, humidity
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Climate is not the only factor influencing malaria spread

Other factors like health infrastructure, GDP, and urbanization are not modeled

Results are sensitive to model assumptions and parameter estimates

An. gambiae subspecies diversity not fully captured due to limited chromosomal form data
NOTES:
Highlights importance of regional coordination and capacity building

Suggests development of GIS-based malaria early warning tools

Funded through the African Climate Change Fellowship Programme (ACCFP), with IDRC and DFID support
Abstract: Predicting anopheles vectors' population densities and boundary shifts is crucial in preparing for malaria risks and unanticipated outbreaks. Although shifts in the distribution and boundaries of the major malaria vectors (Anopheles gambiae s.s. and An. arabiensis) across Africa have been predicted, quantified areas of absolute change in zone of suitability for their survival have not been defined. We developed a model using CLIMEX simulation platform to estimate the potential geographical distribution and seasonal abundance of these malaria vectors in relation to climatic factors. Angola, Ethiopia, Kenya, Mozambique, Tanzania, South Africa and Zambia appear most likely to be affected in terms of absolute change of malaria vectors suitability zones under the selected climate change scenarios. The potential shifts of these malaria vectors have implications for human exposure to malaria, as recrudescence of the disease is likely to be recorded in several new areas and regions.
Keywords: Climate change, Eco-climatic index, African countries, Anopheles gambiae s.s, Anopheles arabiensis, malaria vectors

TITLE: Tectonics, climate and the diversification of the tropical African terrestrial flora
AUTHORS: Thomas L.P. Couvreur, Bonaventure SonkÃ©, Leo-Paul Dagallier, Olivier J. Hardy, Jan J. Wieringa, Quentin Luke, Vincent Droissart, Tarin Toledo, Pierre A. Taedoumg, Gilles Dauby, CÃ©dric M. Nana, George Gosline, HervÃ© Sauquet
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Tropical Africa, especially Central Africa and the Guineo-Congolian rainforest zone
METHODS: This paper is a synthetic review that integrates phylogenetic studies, paleoclimate reconstructions, fossil records, and ecological analyses to trace the diversification of tropical African terrestrial plants. It explores how tectonic uplift, climatic shifts, and geological events over the last 40 million years shaped species richness and endemism. No statistical tests are run, but the study synthesizes genetic, floristic, and geological literature with visual data interpretation (e.g., paleo-maps and evolutionary timelines).
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity
NOTES ON CLIMATE: While the article frequently discusses past climate changes (e.g., Miocene aridification, Pleistocene refugia), it frames them as evolutionary drivers rather than directly addressing modern mitigation strategies.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Central African flora was shaped by episodic tectonic uplifts and climate shifts.

Many species survived through contraction into forest refugia during dry periods.

Uplift of the East African Rift System led to isolation and diversification.

Pleistocene refugia are key centers of current plant endemism.

Calls for integrating phylogenetic data into conservation planning.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Phylogenetic coverage is still sparse for many African plant groups

Incomplete fossil records limit precise evolutionary reconstructions

Lack of high-resolution paleo-climate data for Africa compared to other continents
NOTES:
Authors recommend focusing conservation on phylogenetically unique and old lineages

Supports the inclusion of "evolutionary refugia" in regional conservation planning

Strong contribution to African biodiversity mapping and historical ecology
Abstract: Africa underwent numerous climatic fluctuations at ancient and more recent timescales, with tectonic, greenhouse gas, and orbital forcing stimulating diversification. Increased aridification since the Late Eocene led to important extinction events, but also provided unique diversification opportunities. The paper discusses three major models of speciation: (i) geographic speciation via vicariance (allopatry); (ii) ecological speciation impacted by climate and geological changes, and (iii) genomic speciation via genome duplication, with geographic speciation being the most widely documented. Two major geo-climatic factors shaped African biodiversity: climatic fluctuations at ancient and recent timescales, and increased aridification since the Late Eocene.
Keywords: Africa, biodiversity, climate change, diversification, evolution, flora, fauna, speciation, tectonics

TITLE: Socio-economic determinants for the deployment of Climate-Smart One-Health innovations: A meta-analysis approach prioritizing Ghana and Benin
AUTHORS: Haruna Sekabira, Ghislain T. Tepa-Yotto, Manuele TamÃ², Rousseau Djouaka, Mustapha Dalaa, Osman Tahidu Damba, Stephen Yeboah, Faustina Obeng, Richard Asare, Tahirou Abdoulaye, Angella Nazziwa
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Ghana and Benin (primary), broader SSA included in supporting cases
IITA OFFICE: IITA Uganda, IITA Benin, IITA Ghana, IITA Mali
METHODS: This is a meta-analysis and scoping review of 43 studies and 11 reports on Climate-Smart (CS) One-Health innovations. The review used systematic search and inclusion criteria to identify determinants, challenges, and impacts related to the deployment of CS One-Health innovations, particularly in Ghana and Benin. Key themes include age, education, land tenure, policy engagement, and investment levels.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Climate mitigation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs

Gender equality, youth, and social inclusion
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The paper explicitly addresses both adaptation and mitigation as pillars of CS agriculture. GHG reduction, improved resilience to zoonotic outbreaks, and reduced environmental contamination are central goals.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Community participation, land tenure, political will, and age are critical adoption factors.

Practices like composting, IPM, cover cropping, rotational grazing, and traditional ecological knowledge are promoted.

Major barriers include cultural practices, lack of diagnostic tools, limited ICT access, and top-down implementation.

Multi-disciplinary collaboration is essential for sustainable deployment.

Participatory research and climate-informed advisory services are necessary for farmer engagement.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Overreliance on international aid

Lack of diagnostic testing infrastructure

Cultural barriers to behavior change

Poor intersectoral collaboration

Weak political buy-in and communication strategies
NOTES:
Funded by the World Bankâ€™s AICCRA project (CGIAR initiative)

Expands One-Health concept to include soil and water health alongside zoonoses

Emphasizes circular economy innovations like black soldier fly composting and CS pest management
Abstract: This is a meta-analysis and scoping review of 43 studies and 11 reports on Climate-Smart (CS) One-Health innovations. The review identified determinants, challenges, and impacts related to the deployment of CS One-Health innovations, particularly in Ghana and Benin. Community participation, land tenure, political will, and age are critical adoption factors. Practices like composting, IPM, cover cropping, rotational grazing, and traditional ecological knowledge are promoted. Major barriers include cultural practices, lack of diagnostic tools, limited ICT access, and top-down implementation. Multi-disciplinary collaboration is essential for sustainable deployment.
Keywords: Climate-Smart agriculture, One-Health, Ghana, Benin, socio-economic determinants, innovation adoption, meta-analysis

TITLE: Tackling the nitrogen management challenge: from global to local scales
AUTHORS: Stefan Reis, Mateete Bekunda, Clare M. Howard, Nancy Karanja, Wilfried Winiwarter, Xiaoyuan Yan, Albert Bleeker, Mark A. Sutton
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Global, with substantial attention to Sub-Saharan Africa (particularly Uganda, Kenya, Lake Victoria Basin)
METHODS: This editorial synthesizes findings from the N2013 Kampala conference on nitrogen management and reviews research contributions focused on nitrogen flows, pollution, human health impacts, climate interactions, and sustainable food systems. It consolidates multiple studies using literature review, modeling outputs, and international policy frameworks (e.g., the Kampala Statement-for-Action).
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Climate mitigation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The article explicitly addresses nitrogenâ€™s role in climate change, especially Nâ‚‚O emissions, climate feedback loops, and adaptation strategies via improved nutrient use efficiency and low-emission technologies.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Both nitrogen surplus (pollution) and deficit (yield loss) require tailored solutions.

Integrated soil fertility management and BNF are crucial in SSA.

Reducing meat consumption in high-N regions can reduce emissions and improve health.

Technologies such as nitrification inhibitors and NUE metrics support sustainable intensification.

â€œNitrogen neutralityâ€ and N footprints are proposed as accountability tools.

Collaborative action (Kampala Statement) is essential for regional and global nitrogen governance.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Lack of complete data for nitrogen flows in many developing countries

Need for better cross-sectoral coordination (agriculture, energy, waste, health)

Calls for more data on nitrogen impacts in Africa
NOTES:
IITAâ€™s Mateete Bekunda contributed to synthesis on African nitrogen policy

Introduces the â€œFive Element Strategyâ€ and â€œ4R Nutrient Stewardshipâ€

Connects nitrogen with planetary boundaries and future GHG emission scenarios
Abstract: This editorial synthesizes findings from the N2013 Kampala conference on nitrogen management and reviews research contributions focused on nitrogen flows, pollution, human health impacts, climate interactions, and sustainable food systems. Both nitrogen surplus (pollution) and deficit (yield loss) require tailored solutions. Integrated soil fertility management and BNF are crucial in SSA. Reducing meat consumption in high-N regions can reduce emissions and improve health. Technologies such as nitrification inhibitors and NUE metrics support sustainable intensification. Nitrogen neutrality and N footprints are proposed as accountability tools.
Keywords: Nitrogen management, climate change, sustainable agriculture, food security, pollution, nitrogen cycle, Africa

TITLE: Understanding the consequences of changes in the production frontiers for roots, tubers and bananas
AUTHORS: Athanasios Petsakos, Steven D. Prager, Carlos Eduardo Gonzalez, Arthur Chibwana Gama, Timothy B. Sulser, Sika Gbegbelegbe, Enoch M. Kikulwe, Guy Hareau
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Global, with regional analysis for Sub-Saharan Africa, Asia, Latin America & the Caribbean
METHODS: The study employs foresight modeling using the IMPACT partial equilibrium model linked to crop, hydrological, and socioeconomic projections to simulate future demand, supply, and investment impacts for RT&B (roots, tubers, and bananas) through 2050 under different scenarios.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The studyâ€™s baseline and investment scenarios are grounded in climate change pathways (SSP2 and RCP8.5), with adaptation potential of RT&B crops a central pillar.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
RT&Bs are underinvested despite their critical food security role in developing countries.

Productivity-focused investment outperforms marketing improvements in food security returns.

Regionally targeted investments (e.g., in SSA) can strengthen self-sufficiency and reduce food import reliance.

Commercialization and improved seed systems are needed to address perishability and post-harvest losses.

Foresight analysis supports greater emphasis on RT&Bs in global agricultural research portfolios.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Does not consider future biofuel demand effects or policy shocks

Assumes continuation of current policies, limiting exploration of disruptive scenarios

Gender and inclusion dynamics not modeled
NOTES:
Funded by CGIARâ€™s Policies, Institutions and Markets (PIM) and Roots, Tubers and Bananas (RTB) programs

Highlights the neglected nature of RT&B crops in global ag R&D despite growing importance

Suggests improved NARS and CGIAR collaboration could amplify RT&B development impacts
Abstract: The widely recognized role of roots, tubers and bananas (RT&Bs) in achieving food security and providing income opportunities in the world's poorest regions, will be challenged by socioeconomic and climate related drivers that will affect demand and production patterns and increase pressure on farming systems. RT&B contribution to food security will increase in developing countries by 2050. Overcoming productivity and market challenges in RT&Bs are constrained by underinvestment in research. Investment in productivity improvement greatly benefits a subset of RT&B crops. Investment in marketing improvements do not seem to offer large benefits.
Keywords: Roots, Tubers, Bananas, Food security, Climate change, Production frontiers, Agricultural investment

TITLE: Stressors and Resilience within the Cassava Value Chain in Nigeria: Preferred Cassava Variety Traits and Response Strategies of Men and Women to Inform Breeding
AUTHORS: Olamide Olaosebikan, Abolore Bello, Obaiya Utoblo, Benjamin Okoye, Nathaniel Olutegbe, Elisabeth Garner, BÃ©la Teeken, Elizabeth Bryan, Lora Forsythe, Steven Cole, Peter Kulakow, Chiedozie Egesi, Hale Tufan, Tessy Madu
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Nigeria (Benue, Osun, Abia States)
METHODS: The study used a gender-disaggregated, mixed-methods approach, including 187 structured surveys and 9 focus group discussions with cassava farmers, processors, and marketers to assess resilience strategies, gendered trait preferences, and climate/conflict stressors. Quantitative data were analyzed using ANOVA and RIMA framework; qualitative data were thematically coded.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs

Gender equality, youth, and social inclusion
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate variability and drought are central challenges discussed, with cassavaâ€™s adaptability and specific stress-tolerant traits emphasized as solutions for resilience.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Women favored traits like in-ground storability, early bulking, and root milking for food and income security.

Men valued traits supporting farm relocation and high yield under conflict pressure.

Both genders highlighted cassavaâ€™s multiple food uses and ability to thrive with low inputs.

Root milking and ratooning emerged as resilience-enabling traits not yet mainstreamed in breeding.

Women had lower resilience capacity scores due to reduced asset access and mobility.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Focused mainly on production; processing/marketing stressors less explored.

Security limitations prevented broader social analysis.

Future work should investigate resilience across the full cassava value chain.
NOTES:
Emphasizes participatory trait validation and the need to translate emerging traits into measurable breeding parameters.

Suggests policies should address both social and environmental stressors through gender-equitable approaches.

Funded by Gates Foundation; linked to GREAT-RTB training and NextGen Cassava Breeding.
Abstract: The study investigates cassava trait preferences in the context of climate change (CC) and conflict stressors among value-chain (VC) actors in Nigeria, to strengthen social inclusion and resilience within breeding. Multi-stage and purposive sampling procedures were used to select and interview 92 men and 95 women cassava farmers, 15 VC Key Informants and 63 VC participants in Focus Group Discussions (FGDs) in Osun, Benue and Abia States. Data were analyzed using descriptive (percentages), inferential (two way ANOVA) and thematic analysis. Preferred cassava attributes include drought-tolerance, early bulking, multiple-product use and inground storability. While CC manifests itself as drought and increased incidence of pest/diseases, farmer-herder and land conflicts exacerbate productivity; shape gendered coping strategies and stressor-related trait preferences. The major response strategies employed by men included frequent farm visits to prevent theft and engaging in non-agricultural livelihoods. Those employed by women included backyard farming, early harvesting, having preferences for food with fewer processing steps, and depending on remittances. The resilience capacity was higher for men than for women due to their better access to assets.
Keywords: breeding, cassava, climate change, conflict, trait preferences, response strategies, gender disparity

TITLE: Yield Performance and Stability Analysis of Promising Soybean Genotypes under Contrasting Environments in the Semi-arid Zone of Sudan
AUTHORS: T. Ngalamu, P. Bulli, S. Meseka
JOURNAL: Legume Research â€“ An International Journal
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Sudan (Sennar State, Semi-arid Zone)
METHODS:
Evaluated 5 soybean genotypes (sourced from IITA and ORNAS) across 10 environments (5 sowing dates across two years) using a split-plot design in Sennar State, Sudan.

Used base index for genotype selection based on multiple yield-related traits.

Employed AMMI and GGE biplot models to assess genotype Ã— environment interaction (GEI) and yield stability.

Correlation analysis conducted between seed yield and traits including leaf area, pods per plant, 100-seed weight, etc.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The study focuses on yield variability and adaptability in semi-arid regions, implicitly linked to climate variability. Climate adaptation is discussed through genotype-environment interaction and sowing date timing.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Significant genotype Ã— environment interactions were found.

TGX 1904-6F had highest yield stability across environments.

AMMI and GGE models identified optimal environments (early July sowings).

Key traits linked to yield: pods per plant, leaf area, and 100-seed weight.

Late sowing reduced yield; early sowing (Env 1, Env 2) was optimal.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Only five genotypes tested; broader screening needed.

Single geographic location (Sennar) may limit generalizability.

Some environments were not strongly discriminatory.

Limited trait-environment interaction detail.
NOTES:
Highlights potential for soybean expansion in Sudanese semi-arid zones.

Recommends July planting for optimal yield.

Adapted maize base index for soybean genotype selection.

Funded by Sennar State Government; linked to IITA genotype sourcing.
Abstract: The challenge to food security posed by climate change and coupled with the substantial rise in the global population, necessitate a shift in crop improvement programmes towards developing crop cultivars with stable and high yield potentials across a wide range of agro-ecological conditions. New high yielding crop varieties with stable performance across environments are enabling the expansion of their production area into non-traditional environments with semi-arid climates. This study examined genotype × environment interactions of soybean grain yield under contrasting environments in Sudan's semi-arid zone, with the goal of identifying high-yielding and stable soybean genotypes suitable for farmers in that region.
Keywords: Soybean, Genotype-environment interaction, Yield stability, Semi-arid zone, Sudan, Climate change adaptation

TITLE: Spatial-Temporal Trends of Rainfall, Maximum and Minimum Temperatures Over West Africa
AUTHORS: Francis Muthoni
JOURNAL: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: West Africa (Benin, Burkina Faso, Ghana, Ivory Coast, Mali, Togo)
METHODS: The study used 37 years of gridded monthly climate data (1981â€“2017) from CHIRPS-v2, CHIRTSmax, and TerraClimate datasets. Validation was performed against 66 ground-based meteorological stations using Kling-Gupta Efficiency (KGE), RMSE, and bias statistics. Spatial-temporal climate trends were analyzed using a modified Mann-Kendall test (for significance) and Theil-Senâ€™s slope estimator (for trend magnitude). Results were disaggregated by agroecological zones (AEZs) and months, with additional correlation analysis between rainfall and temperature trends
IMPACT AREAS: Climate adaptation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Strong emphasis on climate adaptation, variability, and extremes. Findings support better early warning systems and agroecological targeting of climate-smart agriculture practices.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Rainfall increasing in Sahel and Sudan zones from Juneâ€“October.

Warming trend dominates across all zones and months except localized cooling in Augustâ€“September in Sahel and Sudan areas.

Strong correlation between rainfall and Tmin/Tmax in many zones.

Implications for agricultural timing, flooding, and yield stability.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Data coverage bias due to low density of meteorological stations

Resolution mismatch between gauge and gridded data

Did not assess yield or socioeconomic impacts
NOTES:
Validation of CHIRPS-v2 and TC datasets for climate monitoring in low-data environments

Encourages scaling of adaptive interventions in AEZs with increasing rainfall

Suggests further research linking these trends to yield impacts
Abstract: This article investigates the magnitude and significance of spatial-temporal trends of 37 years' time series of the gridded data for rainfall, maximum (Tmax) and minimum (Tmin) temperature for West Africa using a modified Mann-Kendall test and Theil-Sen's slope estimator. June to September rainfall showed a positive increase (0.1–5 mm/month/year) that mostly occurred north of 11° latitude, and October rainfall showed a positive trend across the region, but the magnitude was higher south of the same latitude.
Keywords: Rainfall trends, Temperature trends, West Africa, Climate variability, Mann-Kendall test, Spatial-temporal analysis

TITLE: Spatial and temporal distribution of optimal maize sowing dates in Nigeria
AUTHORS: Siyabusa Mkuhlani, Eduardo Garcia Bendito, Abdullahi I. Tofa, Kamaluddin Tijjani Aliyu, Bello Muhammad Shehu, Christine Kreye, Abel Chemura
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Nigeria
METHODS: The study used the DSSAT CERES-Maize crop simulation model to assess optimal sowing dates for maize across Nigeria from 1981â€“2019. It analyzed 37 sowing dates (from March 1 to November 7, each spaced 7 days apart) at 0.5Â° grid resolution using gridded weather (W5E5) and soil (ISRIC) datasets. The model was calibrated with field trials and validated using SPAM maize yield data. Trends in optimal sowing dates and yield penalties from missed windows were analyzed spatially and temporally.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The study is situated squarely in climate adaptation, tracking shifts in rainfall onset and implications for maize farming timing and yield.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Optimal maize sowing dates vary latitudinally: earlier in southern Nigeria (~1 March), later in the north (~late July)

Long-term trends show shifting dates; earlier sowing in northern drylands, delayed dates in southern humid zones

Model shows yield losses of 5% per week with delayed sowing

Comparison with existing crop calendars (e.g., SAGE) reveals widespread mismatch, reinforcing need for updated recommendations

DSSAT model calibrated with high agreement (d > 0.6) against SPAM yields

Climate change, especially rainfall variability, is driving spatiotemporal shifts in optimal sowing dates
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
The DSSAT model is static and does not simulate year-to-year farmer adaptation

Data limitations at sub-national level for long-term validation

Findings are not immediately scalable without local capacity to interpret gridded outputs
NOTES:
Funded by CGIARâ€“Excellence in Agronomy Initiative, with BMGF support

Study provides a potential foundation for a national maize sowing advisory dashboard

Emphasizes importance of aligning sowing guidance with climate data to build farmer resilience
Abstract: Climate change and inter-annual variability cause variation in rainfall commencement and cessation which has consequences for the maize growing season length and thus impact yields. This study therefore sought to determine the spatially explicit optimum maize sowing dates to enable site specific recommendations in Nigeria. Gridded weather and soil data, crop management and cultivar were used to simulate maize yield from 1981-2019 at a scale of 0.5°. A total of 37 potential sowing dates between 1 March and 7 November at an interval of 7 days for each year were evaluated. The results show that optimum sowing dates significantly vary across the country with northern Nigeria having notably delayed sowing dates compared to southern Nigeria which has earlier planting dates. The long-term optimal sowing dates significantly (p<0.05), shifted between the 1980s (1981–1990), and current (2011–2019), for most of the country. The most optimum planting dates of southern Nigeria shifted to later sowing dates while most optimum sowing dates of central and northern Nigeria shifted to earlier sowing dates.
Keywords: Maize, Sowing dates, Climate change, Nigeria, Crop modeling, DSSAT, Agro-ecological zones

TITLE: Worldâ€™s soils are under threat
AUTHORS: Luca Montanarella, Daniel Jon Pennock, Neil McKenzie, Mohamed Badraoui, Victor Chude, Isaurinda Baptista, Tekalign Mamo, Martin Yemefack, Mikha Singh Aulakh, Kazuyuki Yagi, Suk Young Hong, Pisoot Vijarnsorn, Gan-Lin Zhang, Dominique Arrouays, Helaina Black, Pavel Krasilnikov, Jaroslava SobockÃ¡, Julio Alegre, Carlos Roberto Henriquez, Maria de Lourdes MendonÃ§a-Santos, Miguel Taboada, David Espinosa-Victoria, Abdullah AlShankiti, Sayed Kazem AlaviPanah, Elsiddig Ahmed El Mustafa Elsheikh, Jon Hempel, Marta Camps Arbestain, Freddy Nachtergaele, Ronald Vargas
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: n/a
METHODS: This short paper summarizes key findings from the first State of the Worldâ€™s Soil Resources Report (FAO, 2015), including major global threats to soil health, their drivers, and the needed policy responses. The analysis draws from over 200 scientists across 60 countries and highlights region-specific threats, such as erosion in Sub-Saharan Africa and sealing in Western Europe.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Climate mitigation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate change is presented as one of three major global drivers of soil change, affecting moisture regimes, soil carbon, erosion risk, and degradation dynamics.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Major threats to soil: erosion, SOC loss, nutrient imbalance

Most global soils are in poor or very poor condition

Urgent need to stabilize or increase SOC stocks

Fertilizer use must be reduced globally but increased in deficient regions (e.g., West Africa)

Outdated soil data limits current policy responsiveness

Four top priorities: sustainable soil management, SOC stabilization, nutrient management, and improved soil monitoring
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Regional assessments rely on outdated soil data (often from 1980sâ€“1990s)

Lack of soil monitoring capacity in many developing countries
NOTES:
The report was a key output of the Global Soil Partnership and the Intergovernmental Technical Panel on Soils (ITPS)

The paper frames 2015 (International Year of Soils) as a turning point for global soil governance
Abstract: The Intergovernmental Technical Panel on Soils completed the first State of the World's Soil Resources Report. Globally soil erosion was identified as the gravest threat, leading to deteriorating water quality in developed regions and to lowering of crop yields in many developing regions. Stores of soil organic carbon are critical in the global carbon balance, and national governments must set specific targets to stabilize or ideally increase soil organic carbon stores.
Keywords: Soil degradation, Soil erosion, Soil organic carbon, Food security, Climate change, Soil resources

TITLE: Use of modelling tools to assess climate change impacts on smallholder oil seed yields in South Africa
AUTHORS: Priscilla Ntuchu Kephe, Siyabusa Mkuhlani, Farirai Rusere, Abel Chemura
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: South Africa (Limpopo Province: Syferkuil, Ofcolaco, Punda Maria)
METHODS: The study combines crop modeling (DSSAT v4.7) and GHG assessment (Cool Farm Tool) to simulate the yield and carbon footprint of groundnut, soybean, and sunflower under four RCP climate scenarios through 2050. Field trials were conducted in Limpopo, South Africa, and model calibration was based on two seasons of yield and phenology data.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Climate mitigation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Central to the study. Projects site- and crop-specific yield changes under RCPs and links to future emissions and adaptation needs.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Groundnut performs better under future climate at 2 of 3 sites; sunflower and soybean yields decline

Sunflower has highest carbon footprint; groundnut lowest

Climate change reduces GHG emissions per ton for groundnut at 2 sites but increases it sharply for sunflower and soybean at Punda Maria

DSSAT and Cool Farm Tool are complementary for assessing trade-offs in adaptation and emissions

Climate-smart policy should be tailored by site and crop
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Limited calibration data (no biomass, LAI, etc.)

No modeling of pest/disease effects

GHG estimates exclude transport and irrigation
NOTES:
Strong integration of climate modeling with field data

IITA-affiliated co-author (Mkuhlani) contributed to modeling and emissions analysis

Emphasizes practical, location-specific insights for planning low-carbon agriculture
Abstract: This study presents an approach using decision-making tools to assess potential climate change impacts on groundnut, soybean and sunflower yields and greenhouse gas emissions in South Africa. The researchers used the Decision Support Tool for Agrotechnology Transfer (DSSAT v4.7) for crop modeling and the Cool Farm Tool for GHG calculations. The study examines climate change impacts on smallholder oil seed yields under different climate scenarios.
Keywords: Oil seeds, Climate change, Crop modeling, DSSAT, South Africa, Smallholder farming, Greenhouse gas emissions

TITLE: A spatial framework for ex-ante impact assessment of agricultural technologies
AUTHORS: JosÃ© F. Andrade, Juan I. Rattalino Edreira, Andrew Farrow, Marloes P. van Loon, Peter Q. Craufurd, Jairos Rurinda, Shamie Zingore, Jordan Chamberlin, Lieven Claessens, Julius Adewopo, Martin K. van Ittersum, Kenneth G. Cassman, Patricio Grassini
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Sub-Saharan Africa (case study in Ethiopia, Nigeria, and Tanzania)
METHODS: The study develops and applies a generic, spatially explicit framework to guide ex-ante impact assessment for agricultural R&D (AR&D). It integrates biophysical and socio-economic variables to prioritize where to test and scale technologies. The framework is demonstrated using TAMASAâ€™s maize agronomy program in Nigeria, Ethiopia, and Tanzania, comparing alternative site allocation scenarios based on yield potential, rural population, and access to markets.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: While climate adaptation is not framed explicitly, the paper deals with yield stability and climate-driven yield gaps. The framework helps address climate-related vulnerability through spatial targeting.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Introduces TEDs: agro-ecological zones combining soil and climate data

Incorporates socio-economic factors (e.g., rural population, market access) into trial site selection

Demonstrates that better site allocation improves maize area and population coverage per dollar invested

Highlights potential for reducing poverty and malnutrition through strategic R&D site targeting

ROI can increase substantially when trials are located in high-impact areas
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Socioeconomic weighting is country- and program-specific

Local field variation is hard to capture spatially

Institutional capacity and logistics still play key roles in site selection
NOTES:
Funded by CGIAR MAIZE, CCAFS, and Gates Foundation

The framework is publicly accessible and adaptable for different stakeholders (govt., NGOs, private sector)
Abstract: This study developed a data-rich spatial framework to guide agricultural research and development (AR&D) prioritization and to perform ex-ante impact assessment, using technology extrapolation domains based on climate, soil, and socio-economic factors. The framework enables systematic assessment of agricultural technologies across different agro-ecological zones and farming systems.
Keywords: Ex-ante assessment, Agricultural technologies, Spatial framework, Technology extrapolation, Impact assessment, Agro-ecological zones

TITLE: Strategic Foresight analysis of droughts in southern Africa and implications for food security
AUTHORS: Sika Gbegbelegbe, David Chikoye, Arega Alene, Stephen Kyei-Boahen, Godfree Chigeza
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Southern Africa (includes Zimbabwe, Malawi, Zambia, Angola, South Africa, Mozambique, Eswatini, Lesotho, Namibia, Madagascar, Botswana)
IITA OFFICE: IITA Malawi, IITA Zambia, IITA Mozambique
METHODS: The study uses a strategic foresight approach with bio-economic modeling (IMPACT 3.2 model) to simulate the effects of recurrent droughts on food production, prices, and food insecurity in Southern Africa. It evaluates historical drought data, projects future scenarios to 2040, and identifies adaptation strategies such as stress-resilient maize breeding and diversification.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The study is fully focused on understanding and responding to the effects of droughts under climate change scenarios in Southern Africa.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Recurrent and regional droughts will substantially increase hunger risk in multiple countries

Maize production is highly vulnerable; cowpea and non-traded oilseeds (e.g., groundnut, soybean) show potential as alternatives

Two-year droughts (e.g., like 2014â€“16) will increase food insecurity by 10% or more in several countries

Suggested responses: stress-resilient crop breeding, crop/diet diversification, and rainwater harvesting infrastructure

Countries most at risk include Zimbabwe, Mozambique, South Africa, Malawi, and Lesotho
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Model does not account for indirect economic impacts (e.g., on industry, services)

Food insecurity might be underestimated due to lack of behavioral or institutional response modeling

Positive crop responses (e.g., groundnut acreage increase) were excluded from the simulations
NOTES:
Developed in part by IITA researchers in three regional offices

Informs both research and policy communities on climate-food risk in the region
Abstract: Southern Africa has been experiencing long-term changes in its climate with future projections implying droughts should last longer and become more intense, with the region already experiencing an increase in consecutive drought years. This foresight study contributes by quantifying the bio-economic impact of future droughts in southern Africa to support disaster policies in the region. The study recommends an integrated approach for enhancing regional food security under drought that includes breeding improved maize varieties with enhanced tolerance to abiotic stresses, diversifying the diet to incorporate more drought-tolerant food crops such as cassava and cowpea, and investing in rainwater harvesting technologies.
Keywords: Drought, Southern Africa, Food security, Strategic foresight, Climate adaptation, Maize breeding, Crop diversification

TITLE: Temperature-based phenology model to predict the development, survival, and reproduction of the oriental fruit fly Bactrocera dorsalis
AUTHORS: Dongmo K. Michel A., Komi K. M. Fiaboe, SÃ©vilor Kekeunou, Samuel N. Nanga, Apollin F. Kuate, Henri E. Z. Tonnang, DÃ©sirÃ© Gnanvossou, Rachid Hanna
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: YaoundÃ©, Cameroon (and general relevance to Sub-Saharan Africa)
METHODS: The study used laboratory and semi-field trials to develop a temperature-dependent phenology model for Bactrocera dorsalis. It evaluated survival, development, and reproduction at constant and fluctuating temperatures, using life table analyses and the ILCYM software. The goal was to simulate population dynamics under climate variation for pest risk assessment and management planning.
IMPACT AREAS: Climate adaptation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The study is framed as a tool to understand and manage pest population dynamics under climate change scenarios, making it directly relevant to adaptation planning.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
B. dorsalis develops optimally between 20â€“30â€¯Â°C, with highest fecundity at 25â€¯Â°C.

Development and reproduction are significantly reduced outside this range, especially at 10â€¯Â°C and 35â€¯Â°C.

The phenology model accurately simulates life cycle parameters under fluctuating temperatures (e.g., YaoundÃ©, 2013).

The findings can help predict pest pressure under warming scenarios, aiding in targeted control strategies.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Geographic specificity: results based on a Cameroonian strain may not generalize to other regions

The model does not account for natural enemies, host plant variability, or pesticide effects

Further field validation is needed across agroecologies
NOTES:
This is one of the few studies combining full life-table data and climate modeling to assess pest dynamics in SSA.

Strong contribution to climate-smart integrated pest management (IPM).
Abstract: This study developed temperature-based phenology models to predict the development, survival, and reproduction of the oriental fruit fly Bactrocera dorsalis. Bactrocera dorsalis completed its development at temperatures ranging between 15 and 33 °C with developmental times varying across life stages, and the models predicted temperatures ranging between 20 and 30 °C as favorable for development and survival. The study provides important insights for pest management under changing climate conditions.
Keywords: Bactrocera dorsalis, Oriental fruit fly, Phenology model, Temperature, Pest management, Climate change, Thermal biology

TITLE: Upper canopy tree crown architecture and its implications for shade in cocoa agroforestry systems in the Western Region of Ghana
AUTHORS: Winston Adams Asante, Gabriel Ahoma, Benjamin Apraku Gyampoh, Boateng Kyereh, Richard Asare
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Western Region, Ghana (Bonsu Nkwanta district)
METHODS: Transect sampling across 12 cocoa agroforestry plots (1 ha each) using dendrometric and crown measurements of 160 trees representing 44 species. Crown form and parameters were analyzed using multiple regression, ANOVA, and correlation analysis to estimate crown area, volume, and shade potential. Shade silhouettes of solitary trees were also measured.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Cocoa agroforestry is promoted as a climate-smart practice. Study explores how crown forms affect shade provision, microclimate regulation, and resilience to rising temperatures and solar radiation. Crown parameters relate to buffer effects against climate stress.
THEMES/FINDINGS: Trees with 'spreading to cylindrical' and 'elongate' crown forms provided the most shade (>100 mÂ²). â€¨â€¨However, these forms were underrepresented. Crown area, volume, and crown diameter were the most significant predictors of effective shade provision. â€¨â€¨Strong positive correlations between tree size and shade area. Species like Ceiba pentandra, Milicia excelsa, and Ricinodendron heudelotii were found to be optimal for climate-smart shading.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Study localized to one cocoa-growing district; based on physical crown and silhouette measurements rather than direct microclimate outcomes.
NOTES: Backed by the CGIAR-CCAFS and funded by Touton SA. Suggests combining crown attributes with farmer preferences to design better shade strategies.
Abstract: This study examined species-specific crown architecture of upper canopy trees and its influence on shade provision in cocoa agroforestry systems in Ghana's Western Region. Five crown forms were identified and the study found that spreading to cylindrical and elongate crown forms are the most suitable crown forms to incorporate in cocoa agroforestry systems for shade provision.
Keywords: Cocoa, Agroforestry, Crown architecture, Shade management, Ghana, Tree species, Climate adaptation

TITLE: Status of Integrated Soil Fertility Management (ISFM) in Southwestern Nigeria
AUTHORS: O.T. Ande, J. Huising, A.O. Ojo, J. Azeez, K.S. Are, S.A. Olakojo, I.O. Fademi, S.O. Ojeniyi
JOURNAL: International Journal of Sustainable Agricultural Research
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Southwestern Nigeria (Oyo, Ogun, Ekiti, Osun, Ondo, Lagos)
METHODS: Literature review and synthesis of research, extension, and farmer practices on ISFM components in Southwestern Nigeria. Descriptive review of soil properties, fertility constraints, and adoption levels of organic/inorganic fertilizers and biofertilizers. Includes policy analysis, farmer training reports, and stakeholder engagement summaries.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Addresses climate variability and soil degradation as major risks to sustainable agriculture. Recommends ISFM as a climate-resilient solution integrating soil conservation, improved germplasm, and adaptive practices.
THEMES/FINDINGS: ISFM adoption remains low due to poor extension, lack of farmer awareness, and weak policy support. â€¨â€¨Organic inputs, mineral fertilizers, biofertilizers, and improved seed varieties are available but poorly integrated. â€¨â€¨Farmers apply inputs based on affordability, not recommendation. Improved crop varieties and composting techniques are underused. â€¨â€¨Calls for policy reform, farmer training, and local production of ISFM technologies.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Review-based study with no primary quantitative data; focuses on southwestern Nigeria only; many conclusions are drawn from secondary sources.
NOTES: Funded by the Nigeria Soil Health Consortium (South) and supported by AGRA. Suggests demonstration plots and localized farmer training as key to scaling ISFM.
Abstract: The soils of South western Nigeria are rapidly degrading due to nutrient mining, soil loss, inappropriate land use, low inherent soil fertility coupled with adverse effects of climate change. These have resulted to persistent low yields and farmers' poverty. This study assesses the status of Integrated Soil Fertility Management (ISFM) practices in southwestern Nigeria.
Keywords: Integrated Soil Fertility Management, ISFM, Soil degradation, Nigeria, Smallholder farming, Climate change, Agricultural sustainability

TITLE: Willingness to pay for weather index-based insurance in semi-subsistence agriculture: evidence from northern Togo
AUTHORS: Essossinam Ali, Aklesso Y. G. Egbendewe, Tahirou Abdoulaye, Daniel B. Sarpong
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Northern Togo
METHODS: Choice modeling approach based on primary survey data from 704 farm households in three regions of northern Togo. Used conditional logit model to assess willingness to pay (WTP) for weather index insurance (WII), and examined how attributes like drought-tolerant seeds (DTS), loans, and weather information affect enrollment preferences.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The study addresses climate-induced drought risks in semi-subsistence farming systems and assesses WII as a climate adaptation strategy. It emphasizes the importance of bundling WII with drought-tolerant seeds, credit, and localized weather information.
THEMES/FINDINGS: Over 97% of farmers preferred drought insurance over flood or heat insurance, especially for maize. â€¨â€¨The most preferred insurance package combined WII with drought-tolerant seeds, loans, and mobile weather info. â€¨â€¨Farmers were willing to pay ~$11/ha. Education level, farm size, and access to credit significantly influenced WTP.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: WTP derived from hypothetical insurance contracts; findings limited to northern Togo context; smallholder education constraints noted.
NOTES: Study suggests linking WII with advisory services and credit access to enhance uptake. Supported by AGRA, IDRC, CORAF/WECARD, and IITA.
Abstract: This study assesses farmers' willingness to pay for weather index-based insurance (WII) as a market option for sharing climatic risks, using a choice modeling approach based on data collected from 704 randomly selected households in northern Togo, West Africa. The research found that respondents are willing to participate in a WII market and would prefer insuring crops, such as maize over sorghum and rice against drought by paying on average about $14.5 per hectare.
Keywords: Weather index insurance, Climate risk management, Willingness to pay, Togo, West Africa, Agricultural insurance, Climate adaptation

TITLE: Understanding the response of sorghum cultivars to nitrogen applications in the semi-arid Nigeria using the agricultural production systems simulator
AUTHORS: Folorunso M. Akinseye, Hakeem A. Ajegbe, Alpha Y. Kamara, Elijah A. Adefisan, Anthony M. Whitbread
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Semi-arid Nigeria (Sokoto, Kano, Samaru)
METHODS: Field trials (2014â€“2016) for two sorghum varieties were used to calibrate and validate the APSIM model. Long-term simulations assessed grain yield and water use efficiency under six nitrogen application rates (0â€“100 kg N/ha) across three agroecological zones. Model performance was evaluated with RMSE, and simulations captured cultivar-specific responses to nitrogen and water variability.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Evaluates long-term impacts of rainfall variability and optimal nitrogen management as climate adaptation strategies. Findings support zone-specific fertilizer recommendations under changing climatic conditions.
THEMES/FINDINGS: APSIM simulations showed different nitrogen application thresholds for optimal sorghum yield depending on zone. â€¨â€¨60 kg N/ha was optimal in Sudano-Sahelian and Sudan savanna zones, while 40 kg N/ha sufficed in wetter Northern Guinea savanna. â€¨â€¨Yield and water use efficiency varied by zone, cultivar, and climate variability, suggesting blanket N recommendations should be revised.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Potentials may vary beyond the calibrated zones; modeling did not include phosphorus or biotic stressors.
NOTES: Study supports precision nitrogen management in climate-vulnerable sorghum systems using APSIM; funded in part by CGIAR-CCAFS and GLDC.
Abstract: This study used the Agricultural Production Systems simulator (APSIM) model to calibrate and evaluate two improved sorghum varieties in experiments conducted at two research stations in Nigeria from 2014-2016. The results showed that the model replicated observed yield and accounted for yield differences and variations in phenological development between the two sorghum cultivars, with the medium-maturing Improved-Deko cultivar showing better calibration compared to the early-maturing ICSV-400 cultivar.
Keywords: Sorghum, APSIM model, Nitrogen fertilization, Nigeria, Crop modeling, Semi-arid agriculture, Climate adaptation

TITLE: Adoption of agronomic practices and their impact on crop yield and income: An analysis for black gram and green gram in India
AUTHORS: Poornima Varma, Julius Manda
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: India â€“ Madhya Pradesh, Rajasthan, Maharashtra, Andhra Pradesh
METHODS: Used a multinomial endogenous treatment effects regression model to analyze the adoption of climate and plant management practices among 789 farmers. The model assessed determinants of adoption and impacts on yield, revenue, and net income, accounting for selection bias.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate adaptation is a key objective, especially through improved management of irrigation and sowing practices, but climate mitigation is not addressed.
THEMES/FINDINGS: Adoption of climate and plant management improves yield and revenue but not always net income; suggesting high input costs for plant management. â€¨â€¨Contact with extension services and information from KVKs significantly influences adoption.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Risk aversion, implementation gaps in crop insurance, and unobserved heterogeneity challenge attribution; quality differences in crops not measured.
NOTES: Highlights that education sometimes negatively influences adoption due to rural-to-urban labor shifts. The paper also finds agronomic practice adoption to be scale-neutral.
Abstract: Black gram and green gram are important pulse crops in India, but their production has faced fluctuations and stagnancy in yields over the last few decades. This study analyses the determinants of the adoption of climate and plant management practices among black gram and green gram farmers and their impact on yield, crop revenue and net income across four major crop-producing Indian states using a multinomial endogenous treatment effects model. The analysis shows that information, contact with government extension services and access to off-farm activities are crucial in adopting climate and plant management practices.
Keywords: Black gram, Green gram, Pulses, Climate-smart practices, Adoption, India, Crop management, Agricultural extension

TITLE: Can sub-Saharan Africa feed itself?
AUTHORS: Martin K. van Ittersum, Lenny G. J. van Bussel, Joost Wolf, Patricio Grassini, Justin van Wart, Nicolas Guilpart, Lieven Claessens, Hugo de Groot, Keith Wiebe, Daniel Mason-Dâ€™Croze, Haishun Yang, Hendrik Boogaard, Pepijn A. J. van Oort, Marloes P. van Loon, Kazuki Saito, Ochieng Adimo, Samuel Adjei-Nsiah, Alhassane Agali, Abdullahi Bala, Regis Chikowo, Kayuki Kaizzi, Mamoutou Kouressy, Joachim Mako, Korodjouma Ouattara, Kindie Tesfaye, Kenneth G. Cassman
JOURNAL: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences (PNAS)
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Sub-Saharan Africa â€“ 10 countries: Burkina Faso, Ghana, Mali, Niger, Nigeria, Ethiopia, Kenya, Tanzania, Uganda, Zambia
METHODS: The study uses yield gap analysis and spatial upscaling based on agronomically robust, site-specific data from the Global Yield Gap Atlas (GYGA). It evaluates potential cereal self-sufficiency by 2050 under different yield improvement and land expansion scenarios.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Considers climate variability and future uncertainty but does not assess long-term climate change impacts. Focuses on adapting through sustainable intensification.
THEMES/FINDINGS: Even with accelerated yield growth and cropping intensity, most SSA countries will struggle to meet future cereal demand without land expansion or increased imports. â€¨â€¨SSA must invest in R&D, supportive policies, and infrastructure to avoid food insecurity.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Did not model long-term climate change impacts due to data uncertainties. Assumes optimistic scenarios for new land productivity and policy support.
NOTES: Emphasizes need for biophysical and policy integration. Highlights risk of environmental degradation if yield gap closure is not achieved and land is expanded unsustainably.
Abstract: This study estimates it will not be feasible to meet future sub-Saharan Africa cereal demand on existing production area by yield gap closure alone, despite projections that by 2050 the region's population will increase 2.5-fold and demand for cereals will approximately triple. The analysis examines yield gaps, land availability, and sustainable intensification pathways for major cereal crops across sub-Saharan Africa.
Keywords: Sub-Saharan Africa, Food security, Yield gaps, Cereal production, Population growth, Sustainable intensification, Climate change

TITLE: Analysis of adoption of conservation agriculture practices in southern Africa: mixed-methods approach
AUTHORS: Adane H. Tufa, Joseph S. Kanyamuka, Arega Alene, Hambulo Ngoma, Paswel P. Marenya, Christian Thierfelder, Happy Banda, David Chikoye
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Southern Africa â€“ Malawi, Zambia, Zimbabwe
IITA OFFICE: IITA Malawi, IITA Kenya, IITA Zambia
METHODS: Used both quantitative (structured surveys with 4,373 households) and qualitative (278 focus groups) data. Data were analyzed using descriptive statistics and a multivariate probit model to identify drivers and barriers to conservation agriculture (CA) adoption.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs

Gender equality, youth, and social inclusion
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate adaptation is central. The study discusses how CA helps cope with drought, heat, and soil degradation, but does not focus on mitigation.
THEMES/FINDINGS: Adoption of CA is low despite high awareness. Full CA is preferred but is labor- and input-intensive. â€¨â€¨Extension, training, wealth, and land ownership are critical to adoption. Dis-adoption is significant in Malawi due to labor demands and pest issues.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: The CA promotion model (lead farmer approach) has limited reach. Dis-adoption is high due to lack of inputs, labor burden, and pest issues. Knowledge transfer remains weak.
NOTES: Findings emphasize that CA needs to be supported through access to inputs, harmonized training, and local bylaws to manage crop residues and livestock grazing.
Abstract: This paper applies a multivariate probit model and other methods to survey data from 4,373 households and 278 focus groups to identify the drivers and barriers of conservation agriculture adoption in Malawi, Zambia, and Zimbabwe. The study uses a mixed-methods approach combining quantitative and qualitative data to understand adoption patterns and constraints.
Keywords: Conservation agriculture, Southern Africa, Adoption, Mixed-methods, Malawi, Zambia, Zimbabwe, Climate-smart agriculture

TITLE: Assessment of climate change impact and adaptation strategy for millet in the Sudanoâ€‘Sahelian region of Niger
AUTHORS: Abdullahi I. Tofa, Alpha Y. Kamara, Ali M. L. Mohamed, Maman Garba, Abdoulkader M. Souley, Hanarou Salissou, Balkissa I. Kapran, Tahirou Abdoulaye
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Niger (Sudan, Sudan-Sahel, and Sahel agroecological zones)
IITA OFFICE: IITA Nigeria, IITA Niger, IITA Mali
METHODS: Using field-calibrated DSSAT CERES-Millet crop models, the study simulates the impact of climate change (RCP4.5 and RCP8.5 for mid- and end-century) on millet yields in Niger. It assesses three millet varieties (CHAKTI, HKP, H80-10GR) and five sowing windows across six sites in three agroecological zones. Historical weather, soil, and crop data were used for model calibration and validation. Adaptation strategies include variety selection and sowing window optimization.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Adaptation is explicitly addressed; the entire study evaluates millet productivity under RCP climate scenarios. Climate mitigation is not addressed.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Millet yields decline under RCP4.5 and RCP8.5, especially under end-century projections

Early-maturing varieties are more vulnerable to heat stress

Medium-maturing variety H80-10GR performs best under climate change

Early sowing (June) is best in the Sudan AEZ; later sowing (July 1â€“7) better in Sahel zones

Tailored strategies by AEZ are recommended for national planning
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Focused on specific soil types; generalizability is limited

Seed system constraints could limit access to improved varieties

Future work should include broader spatial modeling and farmer adoption constraints
NOTES:
Funded by Norwegian Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Climate Smart Agricultural Technologies project)

Uses latest DSSAT v4.8 modeling protocol

Offers clear policy recommendations on variety deployment and planting calendar revision
Abstract: This study used The Decision Support System for Agrotechnology Transfer (DSSAT) CERES-Millet model to assess the impact of climate change and adaptation strategies on millet in Niger. The research found that the impact of climate change varied with the agroecological zone (AEZ) with higher yield reduction in the Sahel than in the other AEZs.
Keywords: Millet, Climate change, DSSAT, Niger, Sahel, Adaptation strategies, Crop modeling

TITLE: Assessing the use of a drought-tolerant variety as adaptation strategy for maize production under climate change in the savannas of Nigeria
AUTHORS: Abdullahi I. Tofa, Alpha Y. Kamara, Bashir A. Babaji, Folorunso M. Akinseye, Jenneh F. Bebeley
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Nigeria (Savanna agroecological zones: Abuja (SGS), Zaria (NGS), Kano (SS))
METHODS: The study used the DSSAT CERES-Maize model to simulate maize yield under current and future climate scenarios (RCP4.5 and RCP8.5) for mid-century and end-century periods. It compared performance of two maize varieties â€” a drought-tolerant (SAMMAZ-26) and a non-drought-tolerant (SAMMAZ-16) â€” across three Nigerian savanna zones. Model calibration and validation were conducted using historical weather data, field trials, and soil properties.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Adaptation is the central focus; climate modeling uses RCP scenarios and projects future yield losses. Mitigation is not addressed.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Yields of both maize varieties are projected to decline due to increased temperatures.

Drought-tolerant variety shows 1â€“6% less reduction in yield compared to the non-drought-tolerant variety.

Strong spatial variability exists, with northern locations more negatively impacted.

The study recommends breeding for combined drought and heat tolerance.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Does not model combined drought + heat stress response in varieties

Only evaluates two existing maize varieties

Field validation limited to specific sites
NOTES:
Authors recommend refining breeding to combine drought and heat stress tolerance

Important evidence for CGIAR climate adaptation research in West Africa

Suggests more robust modeling for future stress combinations and regional variability
Abstract: This study used DSSAT to assess climate change impacts on drought-tolerant versus non-drought-tolerant maize varieties in Nigeria's savanna zones, with projections for mid-century (2040–2069) and end-century (2070–2099) under RCP 4.5 and 8.5 scenarios. The research evaluates the effectiveness of drought-tolerant varieties as an adaptation strategy for maize production under changing climate conditions.
Keywords: Drought-tolerant maize, Climate change adaptation, Nigeria, DSSAT, Savannas, Crop modeling, RCP scenarios

TITLE: Evaluating the use of nitrogen and phosphorous fertilization as crop management options for maize adaptation to climate change in the Nigeria savannas
AUTHORS: Abdullahi I. Tofa, A.Y. Kamara, B.A. Babaji, A.A. Adnan, T.D. Ademulegun, J.F. Bebeley
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Nigeria (Sudan, Northern Guinea, and Southern Guinea Savannas)
METHODS: A 30-year simulation using the DSSAT CERES-Maize model assessed how nitrogen and phosphorus fertilization affects maize productivity under future climate scenarios (RCP4.5 and RCP8.5) at three agroecological zones. The study simulated crop response under varying fertilizer levels and analyzed yield changes for two varieties across different climates and soils. Model validation was based on field trials, and the study included economic analysis of fertilizer profitability.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The study centers on adaptation to climate change through improved fertilizer management under different RCP scenarios. Mitigation is not addressed.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Yields decline under future climate scenarios, especially without P

Application of 30 kg P haâ»Â¹ consistently reduces yield losses

SAMMAZ-15 performs better than SAMMAZ-16 in all environments

Economic analysis shows highest profitability at 150 kg N + 30 kg P haâ»Â¹

Current national fertilizer recommendations are suboptimal and should be tailored to AEZs and varieties
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
No field trials for other crop management practices (e.g., irrigation, planting date)

Focused only on two maize varieties

Future research should explore early-maturing varieties and additional management strategies
NOTES:
Reinforces the critical role of phosphorus in optimizing nitrogen response

Uses detailed cost-benefit analysis, showing fertilizer access must be supported for adaptation to be viable

Suggests that without access to P, applying N may have diminishing returns under climate change
Abstract: This study assesses the impact of current and future heat stress on maize and the benefit of heat-tolerant varieties in South Asia. Annual mean maximum temperatures may increase by 1.4–1.8 °C in 2030 and 2.1–2.6 °C in 2050, with large monthly, seasonal, and spatial variations across SA. The extent of heat stressed areas in SA could increase by up to 12% in 2030 and 21% in 2050 relative to the baseline. The research examines both rainfed and irrigated maize production systems.
Keywords: Heat-tolerant maize, South Asia, Climate change, Heat stress, Temperature increase, Crop adaptation

TITLE: Climate change impacts and potential benefits of heat-tolerant maize in South Asia
AUTHORS: Kindie Tesfaye, P.H. Zaidi, Sika Gbegbelegbe, Christian Boeber, Dil Bahadur Rahut, Fite Getaneh, K. Seetharam, Olaf Erenstein, Clare Stirling
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: South Asia (Afghanistan, Bangladesh, Bhutan, India, Nepal, Pakistan, Sri Lanka)
METHODS: The study uses climate projections from two GCMs (MIROC and CSIRO), with RCP6.0 climate scenarios for 2030 and 2050. It applies the CERES-Maize model to simulate maize yield under both current and future climate conditions across 7 South Asian countries. Yield comparisons are made between standard and heat-tolerant maize varieties under both irrigated and rainfed conditions using spatial modeling, calibration datasets, and soil databases.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate change is the central frame of the study, with a focus on adaptation. Mitigation is not discussed or implied.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Rainfed maize yields may drop by 5â€“12% by 2050; irrigated by up to 14%.

Heat-tolerant varieties could reduce these losses by up to 93% under irrigation and 36% under rainfed conditions by 2030.

Temperature impacts differ by planting season; Marchâ€“May is most vulnerable.

Model calibration shows accurate representation of maize performance across South Asia.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Results assume no pest or disease losses

COâ‚‚ fertilization not modeled due to maize's C4 physiology

Irrigation availability assumed stable (may not hold in future)

Limited to heat stress; other stressors (e.g., drought) not simulated in full
NOTES:
Supported by CGIAR-PIM, USAID HTMA project, and CCAFS

Provides strategic input for maize breeding priorities in the Indo-Gangetic Plain

Highlights the need for regional targeting of variety deployment
Abstract: An overview of groundwater recharge estimation techniques is presented, covering numerical approach, tracer method, hydrologic budget method, and multiple methods. The norms of recharge estimation through rainfall, canal seepage, and field percolation are also covered in this study. The literature analysis revealed that the tracer techniques are extensively used in water-scarce areas for estimating groundwater recharge.
Keywords: Groundwater recharge, Estimation techniques, Sustainable resource management, Hydrogeology, Water resources, Climate variability

TITLE: An assessment of groundwater recharge estimation techniques for sustainable resource management
AUTHORS: Ajay Singh, Sudhindra N. Panda, Veronica N.E. Uzokwe, Peter Krause
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Global, with relevance to water-scarce and semi-arid areas, especially in India and Sub-Saharan Africa
METHODS: This article reviews and synthesizes groundwater recharge estimation methods, including numerical models, tracer methods, hydrologic budget methods, water table fluctuation analysis, and rainfall/canal seepage/percolation models. It compares advantages and limitations of each approach and advocates for integrated techniques to support sustainable groundwater resource planning and policy.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Recharge estimation is framed as a key tool for adapting to climate variability and ensuring hydrological sustainability, particularly in semi-arid regions. However, there is no direct focus on mitigation or GHG outcomes.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Multiple recharge estimation methods are needed due to limitations of each.

Numerical models like MODFLOW and SMAR offer flexible tools for arid regions.

Tracer methods are especially useful in water-scarce zones but can be expensive.

Integrated models improve groundwater management under uncertainty.

Recommendations are made for method selection based on context-specific data.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
High data demands for numerical models

Tracer methods may cause cost and sampling delays

Specific yield estimation often limits budget-based methods

No one-size-fits-all method; local context is key
NOTES:
Advocates the use of GIS and remote sensing in recharge estimation

IITA connection via author based in Tanzania

Strong emphasis on integrated modeling for policy formulation
Abstract: This paper develops a global spatial framework to delineate technology extrapolation domains based on key climate and soil factors that govern crop yields and yield stability in rainfed crop production. A technology extrapolation domain (TED) is defined as a unique combination of biophysical and socio-economic circumstances. The framework adequately represents the spatial pattern of crop yields and stability when evaluated over the US Corn Belt, and facilitates evaluation of cropping system performance across continents.
Keywords: Technology extrapolation domains, Agronomic science, Spatial framework, Crop yields, Rainfed agriculture, Scaling

TITLE: Beyond the plot: Technology extrapolation domains for scaling out agronomic science
AUTHORS: Juan I. Rattalino Edreira, Kenneth G. Cassman, Zvi Hochman, Martin K. van Ittersum, Lenny van Bussel, Lieven Claessens, Patricio Grassini
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Global, with validation in the U.S., Australia, and Argentina
METHODS: This study proposes and validates a geospatial framework for defining â€œTechnology Extrapolation Domainsâ€ (TEDs) using key biophysical factors (climate and soil) to identify zones with similar responses to crop technologies, aiming to improve the efficiency of field trials and accelerate scaling of agronomic innovations globally. The model was validated using maize and soybean yield data across the U.S. Corn Belt and tested in cross-continental analogs.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity
