Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The TED framework is specifically developed to handle climate variability and assess how it affects crop yield stability and technology adoption potential.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
TEDs group areas with similar biophysical conditions to predict consistent crop response.

The moderate-resolution TEDs effectively explain yield differences in maize and soybean systems.

Extrapolation across analog TEDs (e.g., Australiaâ€“Argentina) shows potential for transferring cropping systems internationally.

Foresight mapping improves the efficiency and geographic targeting of agronomic field trials.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Current model does not incorporate socio-economic factors, which are essential for adoption modeling.

Higher resolution TEDs are more precise but require significantly more data and investment to implement.

Technology adoption risks (e.g., pests, market access) are not modeled.
NOTES:
Supported by CCAFS and Gates Foundation

Open-access tools available at YieldGap.org and NutrientStar for users to access TEDs

Introduces a globally scalable strategy for agronomic R&D efficiency and impact tracking
Abstract: This paper addresses how West Africa is faced with significant challenges from climate change, including parts of the region becoming hotter with more variable rainfall. It discusses resilient alternative crops especially underutilized legumes particularly Bambara groundnut, African yam bean, winged bean and Kersting's groundnut and emphasizes using genomic tools for developing climate-resilient cultivars.
Keywords: Legumes, West Africa, Climate change, Underutilized crops, Bambara groundnut, African yam bean, Genomics

TITLE: Developing the role of legumes in West Africa under climate change
AUTHORS: Rajneesh Paliwal, Michael Abberton, Benjamin Faloye, Olaniyi Oyatomi
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: West Africa (with reference to broader SSA regions including Nigeria, Cameroon, Mali, Ghana)
METHODS: This review synthesizes climate impact projections, crop modeling, and breeding strategies for key legumes in West Africa, emphasizing genomics-assisted breeding, genetic resource conservation, and crop diversification as adaptation measures to climate change. The paper summarizes current and future breeding research and resource development at IITA.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate change is addressed directly, with projected impacts (temperature increases, droughts, shortened rainy seasons) and targeted responses through legume crop breeding and system diversification.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Cowpea and orphan legumes are critical for climate-resilient food systems.

Climate modeling projects significant yield losses in West Africa unless adaptation measures are taken.

IITAâ€™s GRC holds world-class legume collections, used for SNP genotyping, GWAS, and breeding.

Crop diversification, genomic tools, and nutritional profiling are essential for future food system stability.

Traits like heat tolerance, nitrogen fixation, early maturity, and drought resistance are prioritized.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
AYB, winged bean, and Kerstingâ€™s groundnut lack sufficient genomic and breeding investment

Limited climate modeling for minor crops

No current GWAS data available for some species
NOTES:
GRC-IITA hosts the largest global collection of many featured crops

Emphasizes the role of orphan legumes in shifting from climate-sensitive staples

Paper is supported by CGIAR Genebank Platform and Crop Trust
Abstract: This paper discusses the breeding potentials of Bambara groundnut for enhancing food and nutrition security in the context of climate change. Bambara groundnut is a balanced food that can help eradicate food and nutritional insecurity if it is incorporated into the major food system. The study examines genetic diversity, breeding strategies, and the role of this climate-resilient crop in African food systems.
Keywords: Bambara groundnut, Breeding, Food security, Nutrition security, Climate change, Sahel, Underutilized crops

TITLE: Breeding Potentials of Bambara Groundnut for Food and Nutrition Security in the Face of Climate Change
AUTHORS: Oluwaseyi Samuel Olanrewaju, Olaniyi Oyatomi, Olubukola Oluranti Babalola, Michael Abberton
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Sub-Saharan Africa (especially Nigeria, Burkina Faso, Niger, Cameroon, Ghana, Mali, Senegal, CÃ´te dâ€™Ivoire, Togo)
METHODS: This review synthesizes literature on Bambara groundnut (BGN) as an underutilized legume with high nutritional and climate-resilient value. It explores genetic resources, breeding techniques (NGS, gene editing), and agronomic potential. The authors discuss drought tolerance, genomic breeding opportunities, constraints (e.g., lack of reference genome), and opportunities for scaling up BGN through national policies, genomics-assisted selection, and awareness campaigns.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The entire review is framed around climate adaptation, highlighting BGNâ€™s value in drought-prone regions and resilience to abiotic stress. No mitigation co-benefits are identified.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
BGN is underutilized but nutrient-rich, drought-tolerant, and adaptable

Yield constraints stem from underground podding, pest resistance, and post-harvest loss

Modern breeding tools (e.g., CRISPR, GWAS, NGS) can accelerate BGN improvement

Policies and funding are critical to scaling up BGN

Lack of reference genome and poor awareness limit adoption

BGN contributes to food security and biodiversity preservation
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Lack of BGN reference genome

Poor funding and policy support for underutilized crops

Post-harvest and yield challenges

Low consumer and farmer awareness
NOTES:
BGN is positioned as a critical â€œorphanâ€ crop for climate-smart agriculture

Calls for global germplasm sharing and breeding collaboration

Cites IITAâ€™s role in maintaining >1900 accessions of BGN

Emphasizes importance of integrated omics approaches and bioinformatics tools
Abstract: This paper is organized in three parts. The first part highlights and discusses current problems including soil erosion, desertification, nutrient imbalances, acidity, salinization, deforestation, and soil compaction. The authors concluded that integrated approaches promoting sustainable soil management, such as conservation agriculture, crop rotation, agroforestry, afforestation, organic farming, and community engagement, would have significant impact in resolving soil degradation in Africa.
Keywords: Soil degradation, Africa, Soil erosion, Desertification, Sustainable soil management, Conservation agriculture, Climate change

TITLE: Assessment of Genetic Diversity for Drought, Heat and Combined Drought and Heat Stress Tolerance in Early Maturing Maize Landraces
AUTHORS: Charles Nelimor, Baffour Badu-Apraku, Antonia Y. Tetteh, Assanvo S. P. Nâ€™guetta
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: West Africa (Burkina Faso, Ghana, Togo), Trials in Nigeria (IITA-Ikenne and Kadawa)
METHODS:
Evaluated 33 landraces and 3 checks across two seasons (2017â€“2019) under four conditions: optimal, managed drought stress (MDS), heat stress (HS), and combined drought and heat stress (DSHS).

Conducted trials at IITA-Ikenne (MDS) and Kadawa (HS & DSHS) using alpha lattice design with two replications.

Measured multiple agronomic traits including grain yield, plant height, anthesis-silking interval, ear and plant aspect, ears per plant, lodging, stay green, leaf firing, and tassel blast.

Data analysis included combined ANOVA, REML, repeatability, phenotypic and genetic correlations, stepwise regression, and path coefficient analysis.

Cluster analysis conducted to classify accessions by tolerance and trait expression
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Addresses climate adaptation by identifying maize landraces capable of withstanding elevated heat and water stress conditions likely to intensify with climate change.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Drought, heat, and combined stresses caused yield losses of 46%, 55%, and 66%, respectively.

Tolerance mechanisms were genetically distinct for each stress.

GH-3505 (drought) and GH-4859, TZm-1353 (all three stresses) were most promising.

EASP, PASP, and EPP were top contributors to yield.

Path analysis revealed stress-specific trait relationships.

Genetic clustering distinguished tolerant vs. susceptible landraces.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Moderate number of accessions; only two years of data.

Tolerance mechanisms not fully confirmed genetically.

Trials limited to Nigeria, not in original collection countries.
NOTES:
Recommends targeted use of specific landraces in gene discovery.

Confirms that DSHS is genetically distinct from individual stress types.

Strong relevance to breeding for climate resilience in SSA maize systems.
Abstract: For 33 months, twelve-year cocoa plants in Ghana were subjected to three levels of rainwater suppression under full sun or 40% uniform shade. The study found that rainwater suppression led to drought and decreased performance of cocoa trees, while shade enhanced physiology, growth, and yield compared to full sun conditions. However, shade will benefit cocoa but will not save it from the negative effects of drought. Rainwater suppression decreased leaf water potentials, reaching −1.5 MPa in full sun conditions indicating severe drought.
Keywords: Cocoa, Drought stress, Shade effects, Climate change, Ghana, Agroforestry, Water relations

TITLE: Assessing the Potential of Extra-Early Maturing Landraces for Improving Tolerance to Drought, Heat, and Both Combined Stresses in Maize
AUTHORS: Charles Nelimor, Baffour Badu-Apraku, Antonia Yarney Tetteh, Ana LuÃ­sa Garcia-Oliveira, Assanvo Simon-Pierre Nâ€™guetta
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: West Africa (Burkina Faso, Ghana, Togo) and Nigeria (field trials)
METHODS:
Evaluated 66 maize landraces and six drought/heat-tolerant checks under four conditions: non-stress, drought stress (DS), heat stress (HS), and combined drought and heat stress (DSHS) over two years (2017â€“2019).

Trials conducted in IITA-Ikenne, IITA-Mokwa, and Kadawa using alpha lattice design.

Traits measured: agronomic, physiological, and yield-related, including anthesis, silking, plant and ear height, stay green, ear and plant aspect, tassel blast, leaf firing, etc.

Analysis: ANOVA, REML, broad-sense heritability, phenotypic and genetic correlations, stepwise regression, path coefficient analysis, and PCA.

Grain yield loss was calculated under each stress condition.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Emphasizes climate change-induced stress (drought + heat) as the driving challenge. Proposes using landraces to adapt breeding programs to these conditions.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
DS and HS caused 49%, 23%, and 58% mean yield loss under DS, HS, and DSHS respectively.

Landraces TZm-1162, TZm-1167, TZm-1472, and TZm-1508 were top performers under all stresses.

HS and DS tolerance were genetically distinct traits.

Identified key yield-contributing traits (e.g., ear aspect, plant aspect, lodging resistance) via path analysis.

Stresses affected flowering time, stay green, and other morpho-physiological traits.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Lack of collection-site geocoordinates limits environmental interpretation

Single-location trial for heat and combined stress

Possibility of misclassified landraces

Genetic redundancy in genebanks may exist
NOTES:
Suggests use of top landraces for gene discovery and GWAS

Calls for routine screening of landraces for DSHS tolerance

Funded by WASCAL and Gates Foundation; conducted under IITAâ€™s Maize Improvement Program
Abstract: Bambara groundnut is a nitrogen-fixing, stress-tolerant legume with potential for climate-resilient agriculture. This paper discusses the crop's resilience characteristics including drought tolerance, heat tolerance, and ability to grow in marginal soils, making it an exemplar underutilised legume for adaptation to climate change.
Keywords: Bambara groundnut, Underutilized crops, Climate resilience, Drought tolerance, Nitrogen fixation, Africa

TITLE: Assessing the skill of gridded satellite and reanalysis precipitation products over in East and Southern Africa
AUTHORS: Francis Kamau Muthoni, Francis Michael Msangi, Exavery Kigosi
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: East and Southern Africa (Tanzania, Kenya, Uganda, Rwanda, Burundi, Zambia, and Malawi)
METHODS:
Used 82 rain gauge stations (1981â€“2018) and three gridded precipitation products (GPPs): CHIRPS-v2, CHELSA-v2.1, and TerraClimate.

Validation included Taylor diagrams (correlation, RMSE, standard deviation) and Kling-Gupta Efficiency (KGE) analysis with decomposition into bias, correlation, and variability components.

Trend analysis used modified Mann-Kendall and Theil-Sen slope estimators on seasonal and annual time series.

Comparisons were made between trends from GPPs and two ground-based rain gauge stations.
IMPACT AREAS: Climate adaptation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The study provides critical information for climate adaptation through precipitation trend mapping, emphasizes error reduction in climate products, and cautions against unvalidated algorithm updates.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
All GPPs performed well overall but struggled at >1500 masl elevations.

CHELSA-v2.1 had high bias and low skill in mountainous areas.

CHIRPS-v2 and TerraClimate aligned best for trend analysis.

Evidence of wetting trends in southern Zambia and parts of Uganda/Kenya.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Limited independent station coverage

Biases in high-elevation data

Overestimation by some GPPs, especially CHELSA-v2.1

Validation limited by data quality from volunteer-maintained stations
NOTES:
Highlights that newer, higher-resolution products may introduce biases

Emphasizes the role of satellite datasets in areas with sparse rain gauge coverage

Supports development of early warning systems and adaptation strategies in rainfed systems
Abstract: The study evaluated farmer's local knowledge and perceptions of sustainable cocoa agroforestry and sustainable soil carbon management in Ghana and Côte d'Ivoire. About 83% and 50.5% of smallholder cocoa farmers from Côte d'Ivoire and Ghana respectively practiced agroforestry on their farms, though the respondents had limited knowledge about the term agroforestry.
Keywords: Cocoa agroforestry, Farmer perceptions, Soil carbon management, Ghana, Côte d'Ivoire, West Africa, Climate-smart agriculture

TITLE: Allometric equations, wood density and partitioning of aboveground biomass in the arboretum of Ruhande, Rwanda
AUTHORS: Athanase Mukuralinda, Shem Kuyah, Marcel Ruzibiza, Alain Ndoli, Nsharwasi Leon Nabahungu, Catherine Muthuri
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Rwanda (Southern Province, Ruhande)
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate mitigation

Environmental health and biodiversity
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The article explicitly discusses carbon sequestration and references climate mitigation mechanisms such as REDD+ and the Paris Agreement. It recommends more accurate biomass estimation for national reporting and mitigation accounting.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
DBH is a strong predictor for aboveground biomass (RÂ² > 0.90).

Including wood density and tree height improves model accuracy.

Eucalyptus species store the most carbon.

New equations reduce biomass estimation error by up to 8%.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Equations are site-specific (may not generalize across Rwanda).

Wood density and height data increase precision but are costly/difficult to obtain.

Belowground biomass not includedâ€”recommended for future work.
NOTES:
Data supports use of species- and site-specific equations over IPCC generic ones.

Equations can aid in reporting for NDCs under the Paris Agreement.

Collaboration between IITA, ICRAF, UR, and IUCN is noteworthy.
Abstract: Climate change resulted in a decrease of 100 mm isohyets between two 30-year periods. The rainy season structure changed slightly, with the average start delayed by 6 days and the end occurring 4 days earlier. Maximum temperatures increased significantly from +0.44°C to +1.53°C and minimum temperatures increased from +1.05°C to +1.93°C. Despite these climate changes, statistics of major agronomic food crop production in Mali from 1984 to 2013 showed an average increase from 985 to 4492 thousand tons, representing a 22% increase per year. This positive trend is attributed to a combination of agricultural extension, agronomic research application, and smallholder farmer management in the Sahel.
Keywords: Cereal production, Climate change, Sahel, Mali, Agricultural adaptation, Temperature trends, Rainfall variability

TITLE: Editorial: Modelling approaches for climate variability and change mitigation and adaptation in resource constrained farming systems
AUTHORS: Siyabusa Mkuhlani, Priscilla Ntuchu Kephe, Farirai Rusere, Kingsley Ayisi
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Global South focus, with examples from Ghana, India, West Africa, and South Africa
METHODS: This editorial introduces a special issue on modeling for climate adaptation and mitigation in resource-constrained farming systems. It synthesizes key findings from several studies using crop models (DSSAT, APSIM), statistical models (regression, SEM, PCA), and big data approaches to simulate climate impacts, evaluate climate-smart agriculture (CSA), and identify adaptation strategies for smallholder farmers.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Climate mitigation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The editorial is entirely focused on how climate modeling can inform both adaptation and mitigation in agriculture, with strong emphasis on resource-constrained contexts.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Crop and statistical models are essential tools for simulating CSA outcomes

DSSAT and APSIM models used to evaluate crop sequences, nutrient strategies, and conservation agriculture

Regression and SEM used to assess behavioral drivers of technology adoption

Emission models used to quantify GHG outputs and explore renewable energy integration

Modeling informs both long-term planning and real-time agricultural decision support

Integration of data science and local knowledge is key for effective modeling uptake
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Models must be coupled with stakeholder engagement and localized validation to be useful in practice

Access to data and technical capacity remain limiting factors for wider adoption
NOTES:
Funded by the One CGIAR Excellence in Agronomy Initiative (BMGF-supported)

Emphasizes integrating behavioral, economic, and agroecological modeling to design inclusive CSA interventions

Builds directly on CGIAR systems modeling research across West and East Africa
Abstract: This review discusses how molecular breeding approaches have proven helpful in enhancing the stress adaptation of crop plants, and recent advances in high-throughput sequencing and phenotyping platforms have transformed molecular breeding to genomics-assisted breeding (GAB), elaborating the progress and prospects of GAB for improving climate change resilience in crops.
Keywords: Genomics-assisted breeding, Climate resilience, Crop improvement, Molecular markers, Plant breeding, Stress tolerance

TITLE: Current Problems Leading to Soil Degradation in Africa: Raising Awareness and Finding Potential Solutions
AUTHORS: Samuel A. Mesele, Mouna Mechri, Michael A. Okon, Theophilus O. Isimikalu, Omnia M. Wassif, et al.
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Sub-Saharan Africa (entire African continent, with specific references to Nigeria, Sudan, Ethiopia, Ghana, Egypt, etc.)
METHODS: This is a comprehensive literature-based review of soil degradation issues in Africa. The article is structured in three parts: (1) identification of key soil degradation drivers (e.g., erosion, salinization, acidification, pollution, deforestation, compaction); (2) consequences for agriculture, ecosystem health, and human well-being; and (3) potential policy and technical solutions such as agroforestry, conservation agriculture, afforestation, and rangeland management. The study compiles data from national assessments, published research, and international reports.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Climate mitigation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate change is framed both as a cause and a consequence of soil degradation, reinforcing the need for adaptation and mitigation. Land degradation is directly linked to GHG emissions and climate-induced water stress.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
65% of agricultural land in Africa is degraded

Major causes include erosion, desertification, salinization, acidification, compaction, and pollution

Effects include loss of fertility, biodiversity, water retention, and carbon storage; links to food insecurity and malnutrition

Solutions include agroforestry, cover cropping, crop rotation, mulching, terracing, and improved rangeland management

Successful examples are cited from Egypt, Tanzania, Ethiopia, and Nigeria

Implementation gaps persist due to limited policy enforcement and awareness
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Acknowledges the lack of a one-size-fits-all solution

Emphasizes the need for site-specific, community-led interventions and better data on soil nutrient budgets
NOTES:
Strong integration of CGIAR and FAO data sources

Several maps and tables illustrate regional soil degradation types and severity

Advocates for ratifying global conventions on pollution and land use
Abstract: The study used the maximum entropy (Maxent) model on 1,022 georeferenced BXW datapoints and 20 environmental variables. Precipitation of the coldest quarter, average maximum monthly temperature, annual precipitation, and elevation were the strongest predictors. Banana Xanthomonas wilt (BXW) is a major threat to banana production in Rwanda, causing up to 100% yield loss.
Keywords: Banana Xanthomonas wilt, BXW, Disease modeling, Climate risk, Rwanda, Maxent, Plant disease

TITLE: Combined effects of shade and drought on physiology, growth, and yield of mature cocoa trees
AUTHORS: Eric Opoku Mensah, Anders RÃ¦bild, Richard Asare, Christiana A. Amoatey, Bo Markussen, Kwadwo Owusu, Bismark Kwesi Asitoakor, Philippe Vaast
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Ghana
METHODS: A 33-month split-plot field experiment on 12-year-old cocoa trees tested effects of 40% shade and varying drought conditions (via rainwater suppression) on physiological performance, growth, carbon accumulation, and yield. Measurements included chlorophyll fluorescence, photosynthesis, stomatal conductance, water potential, and bean yield. Mixed-effects statistical modeling was applied.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The study is centered on adaptation to heat and drought stress, simulating future climate conditions and assessing physiological, agronomic, and yield impacts.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Shade improves cocoa microclimate and yields but does not fully offset drought effects

Drought stress reduced photosynthesis, growth, and bean yield

Shade increased relative humidity, reduced temperature and PAR, and supported greater carbon accumulation and pod weight

Interactions between shade and drought were limited; effects were mostly additive

Results suggest shade is beneficial but insufficient as a stand-alone strategy under severe drought
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Rainwater exclusion may not fully replicate natural drought dynamics

Shade netting is not scalable for smallholdersâ€”natural shade systems may yield different results

Does not assess long-term impacts beyond the study period
NOTES:
Funded by DANIDA (CLIMCOCOA project)

Suggests further research on shade tree species interactions and hydrological impacts in cocoa agroforestry

Highlights physiological thresholds critical for yield under climate stress
Abstract: This study used farm household survey data from Nigeria and examined whether and to what extent the yield and income impacts of adopting drought-tolerant maize varieties are heterogeneous, using stratification-multilevel, matching-smoothing and smoothing-differencing econometric methods. The results show that the adoption of drought-tolerant maize varieties significantly increased maize yield, crop and household income. The yield and income impacts are heterogeneous and depend on the farm households' propensity to adopt, with estimated yield effects being larger for farmers with the highest propensity to adopt the varieties.
Keywords: Drought-tolerant maize, Impact heterogeneity, Nigeria, Adoption, Climate adaptation, Yield impacts, Income effects

TITLE: Bambara groundnut: an exemplar underutilised legume for resilience under climate change
AUTHORS: Sean Mayes, Wai Kuan Ho, Hui Hui Chai, Xiuqing Gao, Aloyce C. Kundy, Kumbirai I. Mateva, Muhammad Zahrulakmal, Mohd Khairul Izwan Mohd Hahiree, Presidor Kendabie, Luis C. S. Licea, Festo Massawe, Tafadzwanashe Mabhaudhi, Albert T. Modi, Joseph N. Berchie, Stephen Amoah, Ben Faloye, Michael Abberton, Oyatomi Olaniyi, Sayed N. Azam-Ali
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Sub-Saharan Africa (especially Nigeria, Ghana, Botswana, Zimbabwe); also Indonesia, Malaysia
METHODS: This review synthesizes knowledge about Bambara groundnutâ€™s agronomy, genetic resources, drought resilience, and breeding potential. It emphasizes the cropâ€™s adaptive traits, nutritional value, and potential for inclusion in climate-resilient agricultural systems. The paper also reviews barriers to adoption (e.g., hard-to-cook trait, photoperiod sensitivity) and outlines advances in molecular breeding, phenotyping, and germplasm conservation.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs

Gender equality, youth, and social inclusion
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate resilience is a core theme, particularly related to drought tolerance and suitability under projected climate change scenarios.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Bambara groundnut is highly drought-tolerant and well-suited for marginal environments

Underutilised status has led to limited research and development

Potential to enhance food and nutritional security, especially for women-led production systems

Genetic resources are being mobilized for breeding through modern tools

Traits like hard-to-cook, photoperiod sensitivity, and seed system weaknesses limit scaling
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Limited public investment and institutional support for underutilised crops

Knowledge gaps in seed systems, cooking traits, and photoperiod sensitivity

Challenges in breeding due to genetic complexity and cultural preferences
NOTES:
The review supports Bambara groundnut inclusion in the African Orphan Crops Consortium and CGIAR breeding pipelines

Highlights extensive international collaboration across Africa and Southeast Asia

Promotes integration of socio-cultural knowledge in breeding for adoption
Abstract: Based on evidence from regional research, conservation agriculture has good potential for climate change adaptation in both high and low rainfall areas of Rwanda. However, decreased yield observed in high rainfall areas, increased labour requirements when herbicides are not used and lack of mulch due to priority given to feeding of livestock constrained CA adoption.
Keywords: Conservation agriculture, Climate change adaptation, Rwanda, Smallholder farming, Mulching, Agricultural sustainability

TITLE: Do farmersâ€™ perceptions and socio-economic factors drive cocoa agroforestry and sustainable soil carbon management practices in West Africa?
AUTHORS: Florence Jessica Kumah, Abo Kouabenan, Aster Gebrekirstos, Daouda KonÃ©, Samuel Adjei-Nsiah
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Ghana and CÃ´te dâ€™Ivoire
METHODS: The study combines semi-structured surveys (201 smallholder cocoa farmers), focus groups, and stakeholder interviews to assess awareness and practices related to agroforestry and soil carbon management. Data were analyzed using descriptive statistics and multinomial logit regression to identify socio-economic drivers of knowledge and adoption.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Climate mitigation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs

Gender equality, youth, and social inclusion
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The study focuses on both mitigation (via carbon sequestration) and adaptation (resilient agroforestry systems), linking these to farmer perceptions and knowledge.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Awareness of agroforestry and soil organic carbon (SOC) was low despite high rates of traditional agroforestry practice

Educational level was the main predictor of awareness

There is a disconnect between stakeholder strategies and farmersâ€™ knowledge

Perceptions of livelihood improvement from agroforestry were mixed

Stakeholders recommended training, field demonstrations, and harmonization across the cocoa supply chain
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Limited training access among farmers

Gaps between farmer and stakeholder understanding

Need for broader outreach and harmonized messaging
NOTES:
Funded by WASCAL and Climcocoa

Calls for integration of agroforestry and SOC into agricultural curricula

Proposes regional policy coordination to address cocoa-driven deforestation and emissions
Abstract: This study assesses farmers' local knowledge of shade trees at two locations in Ghana with different climatic conditions and vulnerability to climate change, and found that cocoa farmers have detailed knowledge on the functions of shade trees in cocoa systems and prefer species that provide specific needs according to the location.
Keywords: Shade trees, Cocoa agroforestry, Farmer knowledge, Ghana, Climate vulnerability, Traditional knowledge, Tree species selection

TITLE: Climate Change and Cereal Production Evolution Trend in the Sahel: Case Study in Mali from 1951 to 2010
AUTHORS: M. Kouressy, B. Sultan, M. Vaksmann, J.F. BeliÃ¨res, L. Claessens, M. Dingkuhn, N. TÃ©mÃ©
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Mali (Sahel, Sudanese, and Sudano-Guinean zones)
METHODS: This study analyzes long-term climate trends (1951â€“2010) across six agroecological zones in Mali and their impact on millet, sorghum, maize, and rice production (1984â€“2013). It compares rainfall, temperature, and rainy season structure over two 30-year periods and correlates these with cereal production trends.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity 

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Strong climate framingâ€”focuses on rising minimum and maximum temperatures, shortened rainy seasons, and spatial rainfall decline.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Temperatures increased significantly from 1951â€“2010, especially minimums

Rainfall patterns shifted, rainy seasons shortened in many zones

Despite adverse climate, cereal production rose from 985 to 4492 thousand tons (22% average increase)

Farmers adapted via planting calendars, varietal diversity, photoperiod sensitivity, and cropping system complexity

Challenges assumptions that climate change will inevitably reduce production

Recommends deeper study of local adaptation for policy design
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
More research needed to understand adaptation success and causality

Climate impacts vary by region and are influenced by socio-technical change
NOTES:
Strong evidence base from meteorological and agricultural datasets

Endorses using farmer strategies as models for sustainable intensification

Refutes deterministic climate-crop decline narratives with empirical data
Abstract: This study developed parameters for modern cultivars representing all 17 CIMMYT wheat Mega Environments (MEs) using field experimental data and genetic cultivar relationships for the CROPSIM-CERES model in DSSAT v 4.5. Crop simulations were carried out at 0.5 × 0.5° pixels for global wheat-growing areas, using cultivars representing MEs, soil information, region-specific crop management, and initial soil conditions. The simulated yields and production showed a Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) of 1.3 t/ha for yield and 2.2 M t/country for country production when compared to FAO statistics.
Keywords: Wheat production, CIMMYT, Mega-environments, DSSAT, Crop modeling, Global agriculture, Climate change

TITLE: Application of genomics-assisted breeding for generation of climate resilient crops: progress and prospects
AUTHORS: Chittaranjan Kole et al. (total of 50 co-authors, including Michael Abberton, IITA Nigeria)
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: n/a
METHODS: This global review summarizes scientific progress in genomics-assisted breeding (GAB) to develop climate-resilient crops, covering a wide range of crops (cereals, legumes, millets, fruit trees, forest trees). It presents technological tools such as marker-assisted selection (MAS), genome-wide association studies (GWAS), QTL mapping, and gene editing (e.g., CRISPR/Cas9), and their applications for drought, heat, flood, cold, and salinity tolerance. The authors emphasize integrating genomics into mainstream breeding programs to secure food systems under climate change.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Strong climate framing throughout, focusing on how genomics can help crops withstand multiple climate-related stresses.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Genomics can enhance climate resilience across major and minor crops

Traits such as drought tolerance, salinity tolerance, flood resilience, and heat/cold tolerance can be bred using MAS and GWAS

Highlights case studies in rice, wheat, maize, chickpea, pigeonpea, foxtail millet

Emphasizes using wild relatives to broaden genetic bases

Describes CRISPR as a key tool for gene editing climate traits

Advocates multilayered strategies combining breeding, gene editing, and phenomics
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Functional gene validation remains a bottleneck

Genomic tools are less available for underutilized crops

Climate-resilience is polygenic and complex, requiring multi-trait strategies
NOTES:
Co-authored by leaders from IITA, IRRI, CIAT, ICRISAT, and universities worldwide

Important framing piece for genomics in climate-smart agriculture
Abstract: This study focused on Cameroon and examined the relationship between climate change, agricultural productivity (particularly plantain crops), and school attendance rates from 1950-2013, with predictions extending to 2080. The research demonstrates how climate warming causes declines in crop yields and subsequently affects school attendance in Central Africa.
Keywords: Climate warming, Crop yields, School attendance, Central Africa, Cameroon, Plantain, Climate impacts

TITLE: Climate-Related Risk Modeling of Banana Xanthomonas Wilt Disease Incidence in the Cropland Area of Rwanda
AUTHORS: Regina Kilwenge, Julius Adewopo, Rhys Manners, Charles Mwizerwa, Michel Kabirigi, Svetlana Gaidashova, Marc Schut
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Rwanda
METHODS: This study used the Maxent species distribution model on 1,022 georeferenced BXW data points and 20 environmental variables to assess present and future disease risks under RCP 6.0 and 8.5 climate scenarios. Environmental variables included precipitation, temperature, elevation, land cover, and proximity to roads. Risk maps were generated to identify current and future hotspots of BXW in banana-growing regions of Rwanda. Data was crowdsourced through the BXW smartphone app.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The study assesses current vs. future risk of BXW under RCP 6.0 and 8.5, showing increased spatial spread due to changing rainfall and temperature patterns.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
BXW risk is influenced most by precipitation (coldest quarter), temperature, and elevation

Disease hotspots likely to expand under future climates

Crowdsourced data and geospatial tools can guide disease management and extension

Findings advocate early action to avoid significant yield and land losses

Digital tools (e.g., the BXW app) provide scalable diagnostic and surveillance capabilities
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Sampling bias toward banana-producing areas

Crowdsourced data may not be exhaustive or representative of all ecological zones

Surveillance gaps in less-populated or highland areas
NOTES:
Funded by GIZ/BMZ and Belgian DGD via CIALCA

Supports CGIARâ€™s RTB (Roots, Tubers, and Bananas) program

Promotes integration of ICT and climate risk mapping for crop disease surveillance
Abstract: Collection missions and all surveys were undertaken by the Ministry of Agriculture in Tanzania (now Tanzanian Agricultural Research Institute (TARI)) in collaboration with the International Institute of Tropical Agriculture (IITA) from June 2016 to January 2017. SNP genotyping was used as the basis to identify duplicate samples and assess diversity among the collected landraces. The study documented farmer knowledge and eliminated viruses from cassava landraces in Tanzania.
Keywords: Cassava, Landraces, Genotyping, Virus elimination, Tanzania, Farmer knowledge, Genetic diversity

TITLE: Beyond average: are the yield and income impacts of adopting drought-tolerant maize varieties heterogeneous?
AUTHORS: Amadu Yaya Kamara, Oyakhilomen Oyinbo, Julius Manda, Alpha Kamara, Ezekiel O. Idowu, Joseph James Mbavai
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Nigeria (Katsina State â€“ Musawa and Dan Musa LGAs)
METHODS: The study uses farm household survey data (600 households) and applies heterogeneous treatment effect modelsâ€”stratification-multilevel, matching-smoothing, and smoothing-differenceâ€”to assess the yield and income impacts of adopting drought-tolerant maize varieties (DTMVs) in drought-prone regions of northern Nigeria. Propensity score matching was used to address selection bias.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate change is the core framing. DTMVs are positioned as a key adaptation strategy to drought in smallholder systems.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
DTMVs significantly increased maize yields and crop income

Effects were heterogeneousâ€”greater for adopters with high likelihood of adoption (yield), but income effects were greater for those with lower propensity

Policy recommendations emphasize the need to tailor interventions to farmersâ€™ access to capital and resources

Positive selection exists for yield, but not always for income

Proposes improved targeting to reach the most vulnerable and ensure equitable outcomes
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Observational design, not experimental

No long-term or nutrition-related outcomes measured

Possible unobserved heterogeneity not accounted for despite propensity score methods
NOTES:
Study aligns with IITA projects (e.g., DTMA, ISMA, KKM-PLS)

Strong methodological contribution to understanding heterogeneous adoption effects

No gender/youth-specific results or targeting despite existing literature on intra-household impacts
Abstract: This comprehensive review addresses enhancing carbon sequestration capacity of crops, improving photosynthesis, engineering climate resilience in crops, and describes the vision of a sustainable global bioeconomy rooted in plant biology. The paper covers systems biology approaches for sustainable agriculture in the context of climate change challenges.
Keywords: Systems biology, Sustainable agriculture, Climate resilience, Plant biology, Crop improvement, Photosynthesis

TITLE: Applicability of conservation agriculture for climate change adaptation in Rwandaâ€™s situation
AUTHORS: M. Kabirigi, B. Musana, F. Ngetich, J. Mugwe, A. Mukuralinda, N.L. Nabahungu
JOURNAL: Journal of Soil Science and Environmental Managemen
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Rwanda
METHODS: A literature-based synthesis evaluating the potential for conservation agriculture (CA) as a climate change adaptation and mitigation strategy in Rwanda. The paper examines CAâ€™s role in soil and water conservation, crop productivity, labor, and gender dynamics in both high- and low-rainfall regions.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Climate mitigation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs

Gender equality, youth, and social inclusion
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The paper centers on climate risks in Rwanda (droughts, variable rainfall, erosion) and proposes CA as a dual adaptation-mitigation solution.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
CA reduces soil loss, increases organic carbon, and improves water-use efficiency

Challenges include labor constraints, lack of mulch, and resistance to herbicides

Gender section highlights how CA reduces drudgery and increases womenâ€™s control over production

Policy must account for trade-offs, especially in residue use for livestock vs. mulching

Emphasizes need for locally adapted CA recommendations and broader farmer engagement
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Lack of field data in Rwanda on long-term CA impacts

Social and cultural barriers to residue use and herbicide adoption

Yield increases may take several years to manifest, discouraging short-term adoption
NOTES:
Strong gender-aware framing

Includes detailed discussion of trade-offs (e.g., mulch for livestock vs. soil)

Supported by the SIMLESA project (Sustainable Intensification of Maize-Legume Systems in ESA)
Abstract: The study systematically reviewed literature published between 2005 and 2020 and identified 35 relevant studies, analyzing yield changes of major staple crops (maize, sorghum, rice, millet, yam, cassava and groundnuts) caused by different climate change and field management scenarios. Yields declined by a median of 6% (-8% to +2% depending on the crop) due to climate change in all scenarios analysed. Common adaptation strategies could increase crop yields affected by climate change by 13% (-4% to +19% depending on the strategy) as compared to business-as-usual field management.
Keywords: Climate change impacts, Adaptation strategies, West Africa, Crop yields, Systematic review, Food security

TITLE: Evaluating Farmersâ€™ Knowledge of Shade Trees in Different Cocoa Agro-Ecological Zones in Ghana
AUTHORS: Sophie Graefe, Lennart Flavio Meyer-Sand, Katja Chauvette, Issaka Abdulai, Laurence Jassogne, Philippe Vaast, Richard Asare
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Ghana (Akumadan â€“ Northern Ashanti Region; Asankragua â€“ Western Region)
METHODS: The study combined farmer interviews, tree inventories, and ecological measurements at two sites in Ghanaâ€™s cocoa belt to assess farmer knowledge and perception of shade trees. The sites differ in climate and vulnerability to climate change. Farmers were surveyed about tree benefits and drawbacks, and data were collected on species frequency, structure, and regeneration. Farmers ranked shade tree species for cocoa compatibility and perceived ecosystem services.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The study explicitly frames agroforestry as an adaptation strategy to climate variability. Differences in tree preference reflect climate-driven adaptation priorities (e.g., fruit trees for income diversification in drier areas).
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Cocoa farmers possess detailed local knowledge of tree functions

Preferred species differ by location: fruit trees in drier areas (income + soil benefits); timber trees in wetter zones (shade + structural traits)

Perceptions are species-specific, considering benefits (e.g., moisture, weed suppression) and harms (e.g., pest attraction, mechanical damage)

Farmers' rankings do not always match species abundanceâ€”indicating constraints to tree management choices

Policies restricting tree ownership discourage investment in timber species
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Local context limits generalizability

Policies influencing tree planting not systematically analyzed
NOTES:
Supported by CGIAR-CCAFS and FTA

Highlights the risk of losing agroecologically suitable species due to climate change and policy constraints
Abstract: This study investigated the capacity of institutions to support the planning, implementation, and resource mobilization needed to integrate climate change mitigation, conservation, and livelihood goals in a forest mosaic landscape in East Cameroon, and found that diverse institutions are present in the landscape, including government agencies, local government, local non-government, private sector, and hybrid institutions.
Keywords: Institutional capacity, Integrated landscape management, Cameroon, Climate change mitigation, Resource management

TITLE: Baseline simulation for global wheat production with CIMMYT mega-environment specific cultivars
AUTHORS: S. Gbegbelegbe, D. Cammarano, S. Asseng, R. Robertson, U. Chung, M. Adam, A. Abdalla, T. Payne, M. Reynolds, K. Sonder, B. Shiferaw, G. Nelson
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Global (with emphasis on agroecological zones defined by CIMMYT Mega Environments)
METHODS: This study developed and validated a global wheat simulation model using region-specific cultivar parameters from CIMMYTâ€™s 17 Mega Environments. The model, implemented in DSSAT (CROPSIM-CERES), was calibrated with field data and tested with global climate, soil, and management datasets. The purpose was to produce a robust baseline for assessing future climate and policy scenarios on wheat production.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate change is the central driver for the simulation exercise. The goal is to establish a global baseline that can be used to analyze climate impacts on food systems.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Developed cultivar-specific parameters for 17 Mega Environments

Used global datasets to simulate wheat yields across irrigated and rainfed systems

Found better yield prediction accuracy with ME-specific cultivars than using generic ones

Created a validated simulation baseline to support future climate impact and breeding studies
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Lack of pest and disease modeling

Input data gaps in soil and management practices, especially in small wheat-producing countries

No socioeconomic or gender disaggregation
NOTES:
Strong CGIAR alignment (CCAFS and IFPRI support)

Offers a foundational resource for future climate resilience studies in wheat systems
Abstract: This study found the promotion of new maize and wheat varieties to be an effective adaptation option, on average, especially when accompanied by policy interventions such as credit and fertilizer subsidy. However, the effectiveness of available adaptation options is quite different across the heterogeneous smallholder population in Ethiopia, implying that policy assessments based on average farm households may mislead policy makers to adhere to interventions that are beneficial on average albeit ineffective in addressing the particular needs of poor and food insecure farmers.
Keywords: Smallholder farmers, Climate adaptation, Policy interventions, Ethiopia, Agent-based modeling, Agricultural policy

TITLE: Climate warming causes declines in crop yields and lowers school attendance rates in Central Africa
AUTHORS: Trevon L. Fuller, Paul R. Sesink Clee, Kevin Y. Njabo, Anthony TrÃ³chez, Katy Morgan, Demetrio Bocuma MeÃ±e, Nicola M. Anthony, Mary Katherine Gonder, Walter R. Allen, Rachid Hanna, Thomas B. Smith
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Cameroon, Central Africa
METHODS: The paper uses climate data, national crop surveys, and educational statistics to model how rising temperatures in Cameroon have affected plantain yields and school attendance rates. It uses instrumental variables regression to analyze linkages from climate â†’ yield â†’ income â†’ education over a 20-year period (1991â€“2011), with future projections to 2080 under various IPCC scenarios.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Climate mitigation

Nutrition, health, and food security 

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Central to the study. Models past and projected impacts of climate warming on plantain yield and educational attainment. Ties climate stress to social and economic resilience.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
0.8Â°C temperature rise from 1950â€“2013; 6.2% increase in dryness

Plantain yield dropped 43% (1991â€“2011); school attendance dropped 6 months

Predicts 39% decline in yield and 51% decline in school completion by 2080 without intervention

Suggests climate change undermines household income, thereby reducing education access
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
No household-level income or education expenditure data

Does not disaggregate other social vulnerability indicators (e.g., gender, illness, or labor decisions)

Future projections are dependent on global emission pathway assumptions
NOTES:
Groundbreaking in linking climate change directly to educational outcomes

Provides a strong case for including social vulnerability in climate impact assessments
Abstract: This study investigates whether historical climate change has impacted the evolutionary history of African Aedes mosquitoes using a comparative phylogeographic approach. The research examines the role of past climatic change in shaping mosquito evolution within Africa.
Keywords: Aedes mosquitoes, Phylogeography, Climate change evolution, Africa, Vector ecology, Palaeoclimate

TITLE: Collection, genotyping and virus elimination of cassava landraces from Tanzania and documentation of farmer knowledge
AUTHORS: Ferguson, M.E., Tumwegamire, S., Chidzanga, C., Shah, T., Mtunda, K., et al.
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Tanzania (all major cassava-growing zones)
IITA OFFICE: IITA Kenya, IITA Tanzania, IITA Nigeria
METHODS: A comprehensive germplasm collection was conducted across 66 districts in Tanzania to identify, document, and genotype cassava landraces and associated farmer knowledge. Data were collected through farmer interviews and field sampling. Landraces were genotyped for diversity and duplicates, tested for cooking and organoleptic traits, and conserved in gene banks. Virus elimination was initiated for unique clones.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs

Gender equality, youth, and social inclusion
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Cassavaâ€™s resilience to drought and poor soils is central to its role under climate change. The study frames conservation of landraces as a strategy for climate resilience and long-term adaptation.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
427 landraces were collected and narrowed to 277 unique varieties

Traits varied by zone and use (e.g., fresh, flour, dual-use)

Landraces are closely tied to farmer preferences and adapted to local agroecologies

Limited diffusion across zones highlights weaknesses in informal seed systems

The diversity supports future breeding for climate and consumer-oriented traits
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Possible misclassification of improved vs. landrace varieties

Limited replication of landraces across regions

Weakness in farmer-to-farmer seed exchange limits diffusion
NOTES:
Rich integration of genetic, social, and agronomic data

Valuable for gender-responsive breeding, though not fully analyzed through that lens

The conservation strategy is active and ongoing via KEPHIS and IITA
Abstract: This study evaluates the heterogeneous impacts of adopting climate-smart agricultural technologies on rural household welfare in Mali using farm household survey data and econometric methods. The analysis shows differential impacts across different farmer groups and household characteristics.
Keywords: Climate-smart agriculture, Heterogeneous impacts, Mali, Rural welfare, Technology adoption, Household impacts

TITLE: Systems biology for sustainable agriculture
AUTHORS: Nancy A. Eckardt
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: N/a
METHODS: This article is a multi-author perspective and synthesis piece discussing various ways plant science can address climate change. It highlights innovations in breeding, biotechnology, and physiology to enhance crop resilience, photosynthesis, carbon sequestration, and soil health. It includes subsections on annual cropping systems, perennial grains, seagrasses, synthetic biology, and climate-smart crop engineering.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Climate mitigation

Nutrition, health, and food security
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The article is framed entirely around responding to climate change with plant-based mitigation and adaptation strategies. It explicitly discusses both aspects and emphasizes urgent action.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Development of climate-resilient and carbon-sequestering crops

Soil health and deep root systems for durable carbon storage

Synthetic biology to improve photosynthesis and COâ‚‚ capture

Bioeconomic transition rooted in plant systems

Emphasis on innovation and cross-disciplinary research for climate solutions
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Challenges of translating plant traits to field-scale impacts

Limited public understanding and acceptance of some technologies

Difficulty of engineering soil microbiomes and long breeding timelines
NOTES:
Multiple CGIAR-affiliated scientists involved (including IITA)

Calls for global scientific mobilization akin to COVID-19 response

Emphasizes role of plant science in meeting IPCC climate targets
Abstract: This study assessed the potential climate change impact on four key rainfed cereals (maize, millet, sorghum and wheat) in ten SSA countries namely Burkina Faso, Ghana, Mali, Niger, Nigeria, Ethiopia, Kenya, Tanzania, Uganda, and Zambia using local data and national expertise, and the potential of cultivar adaptation to climate change for the four crops. By 2050, climate change impact on rainfed potential yields was insignificant in West and modest in East and Southern Africa. By 2090, average impacts were much higher in West than in East and Southern Africa.
Keywords: Climate change impact, Rainfed cereals, Sub-Saharan Africa, Crop adaptation, Maize, Millet, Sorghum, Wheat

TITLE: Climate change impacts and adaptation strategies for crops in West Africa: A systematic review
AUTHORS: Tony W. Carr, Siyabusa Mkuhlani, Alcade C. Segnon, Zakari Ali, Robert ZougmorÃ©, Alan D. Dangour, Rosemary Green, Pauline Scheelbeek
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: West Africa (regional, including Benin, Ghana, Mali, Nigeria, Niger, Senegal, Burkina Faso, CÃ´te d'Ivoire, Cameroon, Gambia, and others)
METHODS: This systematic review synthesizes 35 studies (2005â€“2020) using crop simulation models to assess climate change impacts and the effectiveness of adaptation strategies on seven key crops in West Africa (maize, sorghum, rice, millet, yam, cassava, and groundnut). The analysis includes both qualitative synthesis and quantitative comparisons of yield outcomes under different Representative Concentration Pathways (RCPs), with and without adaptation strategies such as optimized planting dates, fertilizer application, and climate-resilient cultivars.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Climate mitigation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Central focus on climate change impacts, emphasizing yield changes under current and future scenarios, and quantifying the mitigating potential of adaptation strategies.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Climate change negatively affects staple crop yields in West Africa, with median declines of 6%.

Adaptation strategies can increase yields by a median of 13% compared to business-as-usual.

Optimized planting dates and cultivars with extended crop cycles are the most effective adaptations.

Fertilizer use improves yields but doesn't offset climate impacts alone.

Combining multiple strategies (e.g., planting dates + fertilization + drought-tolerant varieties) is most effective.

Adaptation success is highly context-specific, requiring localized implementation and data-driven policy.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Limited data on crops like yam and cassava

Lack of simulations for fruits, vegetables, and complex land management (e.g., agroforestry)

Crop models do not fully simulate pests, diseases, or extreme weather events

Context-specific constraints not always captured in model-based studies
NOTES:
Reinforces the importance of bridging climate-smart agriculture with yield-gap closing efforts.

Highlights need for more robust data, better regional climate models, and expanded research on neglected crops and farming systems.

TITLE: Diversity within village institutions and its implication for resilience in the context of climate change in Cameroon
AUTHORS: H. Carolyn Peach Brown, Denis J. Sonwa
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Cameroon (Centre, East, and Southwest provinces; humid forest zone)
METHODS: The study analyzed how internal village institutions in 13 Cameroonian forest communities shape resilience to climate change. Using 232 household surveys and key informant interviews, it assessed institutional diversity, gender participation, group collaboration, and potential for social learning. Statistical methods included cross-tabulations, chi-square tests, and linear regression to explore how institutional characteristics differ across regions and demographics.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs

Gender equality, youth, and social inclusion
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The study directly investigates how local institutions can foster adaptive capacity in response to climate variability and long-term climate change in rural Cameroon.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Nearly all villagers participate in at least one local institution (formal/informal), which are diverse in gender, age, occupation, and education.

These institutions are underutilized for climate adaptation but have the potential to become platforms for social learning and resilience.

Women tend to dominate savings, NTFP collection, and processing groups, while men dominate marketing and community forest groups.

Institutional diversity (in gender, education, and occupation) positively contributes to community resilience.

Local institutions need external support, training, and inclusion in climate governance frameworks to become effective agents of adaptation.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Limited formal engagement from national and local government institutions in climate efforts

Diversity in ethnicity within groups was low in some remote areas, possibly limiting knowledge exchange

Data is cross-sectional and may not reflect evolving dynamics over time
NOTES:
Authors call for gender-sensitive, long-term facilitated learning strategies to build institutional climate resilience.

Paper highlights how informal institutions can be key entry points for climate adaptation planning in forest-dependent regions.

TITLE: Can smallholder farmers adapt to climate variability, and how effective are policy interventions? Agent-based simulation results for Ethiopia
AUTHORS: Thomas Berger, Christian Troost, Tesfamicheal Wossen, Evgeny Latynskiy, Kindie Tesfaye, Sika Gbegbelegbe
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Ethiopia (national, with disaggregated data from the Ethiopian Rural Household Survey)
METHODS: The study used a stochastic agent-based simulation model (MPMAS) to assess how Ethiopian smallholder farmers adapt to climate and price variability. Based on the Ethiopian Rural Household Survey, 1,300 farm households were modeled to simulate decisions under different policy and climate scenarios over 15 years. Policy interventions (credit, fertilizer subsidies, improved seed access) were tested alongside â€œidealâ€ innovation diffusion. The model accounted for household-specific constraints and decision-making under imperfect foresight.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The study evaluates how different types of climate variability (rainfall and price shocks) affect smallholder livelihoods, and whether planned interventions can improve their adaptive capacity under such stress.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Climate and price variability reduce average household income by ~5%, but effects are uneven across households.

About 10% of households benefit from variability due to favorable conditions and assets.

Policy interventions (especially fertilizer subsidies and improved seeds) can raise incomes, but are most beneficial to better-off households.

Poorer households often remain vulnerable or experience â€œmaladaptationâ€ (worse outcomes due to poorly matched interventions).

Policy effectiveness depends on asset levels, agroecological conditions, and innovation diffusion.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Risk aversion not explicitly modeled

Some simulation parameters (e.g., yields, climate impacts) based on aggregated or proxy data

Exclusion of social networks and informal safety nets (e.g., kinship)

Challenges in scaling agent-based modeling to national policy relevance
NOTES:
The study provides robust evidence for tailoring adaptation policy to household diversity.

Demonstrates the usefulness of bioeconomic agent-based models in climate and food security policy design.

TITLE: Comparative phylogeography of Aedes mosquitoes and the role of past climatic change for evolution within Africa
AUTHORS: Kelly Louise Bennett, Martha Kaddumukasa, Fortunate Shija, Rousseau Djouaka, Gerald Misinzo, Julius Lutwama, Yvonne Marie Linton, Catherine Walton
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Uganda, Tanzania, and Republic of Benin (West, East, and Central Africa)
METHODS: The study employed population genetics and Approximate Bayesian Computation to investigate the evolutionary history and demographic responses of five Aedes mosquito species in Africa to past climate change. Molecular markers (nuclear and mitochondrial) were analyzed from specimens collected in Uganda, Tanzania, and Benin to test for lineage diversification, genetic differentiation, and historical population dynamics.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The paper focuses on the evolutionary impact of historical climate changesâ€”particularly forest fragmentation and expansion during the Pleistocene and Holoceneâ€”on Aedes mosquito diversification and population structure across Africa.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Historical climate-driven habitat changes shaped the phylogeographic patterns of Aedes mosquitoes.

Multiple species showed signals of population expansion, lineage divergence, and genetic structure consistent with allopatric divergence in forest refugia.

Evidence of significant isolation by distance in several species.

The study underlines the long-term ecological consequences of past climate events on vector biodiversity.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Sample sizes for some species/populations were small, limiting statistical power in Approximate Bayesian Computation.

Potential recombination in nuclear markers had to be managed by trimming sequences.

Admixture and secondary contact complicated clear evolutionary inference for some taxa.
NOTES:
The study offers important evolutionary context for understanding vector distribution and potential responses to current and future climate change.

Supports the use of molecular phylogeography in conservation and disease vector management strategies in Sub-Saharan Africa.

TITLE: Evaluating the Heterogeneous Impacts of Adoption of Climate-Smart Agricultural Technologies on Rural Householdsâ€™ Welfare in Mali
AUTHORS: Bola Amoke Awotide, Adebayo Ogunniyi, Kehinde Oluseyi Olagunju, Lateef Olalekan Bello, Amadou Youssouf Coulibaly, Alexander Nimo Wiredu, BourÃ©mo Kone, Aly Ahamadou, Victor Manyong, Tahirou Abdoulaye
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Mali
METHODS: Quantitative analysis using Instrumental Variable Quantile Regression (IV-QTE) on survey data from 2,240 households across 4 regions in Mali. Mixed methods used to evaluate welfare outcomes from CSAT adoption, with emphasis on heterogeneity of impacts across income distributions.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs

Gender equality, youth, and social inclusion
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Focuses on climate adaptation through Climate-Smart Agricultural Technologies (CSATs) to enhance the welfare of smallholder farmers in Maliâ€™s Sahelian region, which is increasingly vulnerable to climate variability and drought.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
CSAT adoption significantly improves welfare indicators, especially for the poorest households.

Positive impact on income, food and non-food expenditure, and total consumption.

Key adoption drivers: access to credit, training, extension services, social networks, and media exposure.

Adoption has a pro-poor biasâ€”benefits are higher among lower-income households.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Study based only in CSAT project areas, limiting generalizability.

Cross-sectional data limits assessment of time-varying factors.
NOTES:
Strong policy implications: targeted support to poor, vulnerable farmers can enhance climate resilience.

Funded by the Royal Norwegian Embassy in Mali; conducted in collaboration with IITA and national partners.

TITLE: Climate change impact and adaptation of rainfed cereal crops in sub-Saharan Africa
AUTHORS: Seyyedmajid Alimagham, Marloes P. van Loon, Julian Ramirez-Villegas, Samuel Adjei-Nsiah, Freddy Baijukya, Abdullahi Bala, Regis Chikowo, JoÃ£o Vasco Silva, Abdelkader Mahamane SoulÃ©, Godfrey Taulya, Fatima Amor Tenorio, Kindie Tesfaye, Martin K. van Ittersum
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Sub-Saharan Africa (Burkina Faso, Ghana, Mali, Niger, Nigeria, Ethiopia, Kenya, Tanzania, Uganda, Zambia)
IITA OFFICE: IITA Ghana, IITA Tanzania, IITA Uganda
METHODS: Multi-location crop modeling study using WOFOST and Hybrid-Maize to assess climate change impacts on maize, millet, sorghum, and wheat. Simulated yield responses (Yw) to SSP3â€“7.0 and SSP5â€“8.5 scenarios through 2050 and 2090 using data from 120 locations in 10 SSA countries. Assessed impact of cultivar adaptation by modifying thermal time requirements of cultivars.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Climate mitigation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Models climate change impacts under two SSP scenarios. Simulates cultivar adaptation by selecting existing or hypothetical cultivars with 10â€“15% longer/shorter thermal time. Emphasizes high temperatures and altered rainfall as climate risks.
THEMES/FINDINGS: Climate change is projected to decrease cereal yields by 6% in East and Southern Africa (ESA) and 3% in West Africa (WA) by 2050, with larger losses in 2090. â€¨â€¨Adaptation using longer-duration cultivars can offset some losses, especially in ESA. In WA, adaptation effects are weaker. Maize, millet, and sorghum yields decline, while wheat yields rise. â€¨â€¨Environmental conditions are projected to become harsher, especially in WA, making adaptation more difficult. Closing yield gaps and cultivar adaptation are critical for food security.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Models do not simulate extreme events (e.g., storms, floods); study assumes spatial crop distributions remain unchanged; focus is on growing season duration, not heat/drought tolerance traits.
NOTES: Robust use of GYGA datasets and local agronomist input; results support cultivar-focused adaptation as a policy strategy. Study was part of CGIAR Excellence in Agronomy and Climate Resilience initiatives.

TITLE: Carbon Storage in Cocoa Growing Systems Across Different Agroecological Zones in Ghana
AUTHORS: John Tennyson Afele, Evans Dawoe, Akwasi Adutwum Abunyewa, Victor Afari-Sefa, Richard Asare
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Ghana (Dry Semi-Deciduous Fire Zone, Moist Evergreen Zone, Upland Evergreen Moist Zone)
METHODS: Field-based comparative study of carbon storage and cocoa yield in shaded vs. full-sun cocoa systems across six districts in three agroecological zones in Ghana. Soil samples were analyzed at multiple depths, biomass of cocoa and shade trees measured via allometric models, and statistical analysis conducted with two-way ANOVA.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Climate mitigation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Study focuses on carbon sequestration potential of cocoa agroforestry vs. full sun systems as a climate mitigation and adaptation strategy. Carbon levels are analyzed in soil, biomass, and belowground components under varying rainfall and shade conditions.
THEMES/FINDINGS: Shaded cocoa systems stored more total carbon than full-sun systems, though pod yields were higher in full-sun. â€¨â€¨SOC decreased with soil depth; highest carbon storage found in UEMZ. Shade trees contributed significantly to aboveground and belowground biomass carbon. â€¨â€¨A positive relationship was observed between carbon stocks and yield. Authors recommend carbon incentives for farmers to adopt shade-based systems.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Study focused on 15-year-old farms; limited generalizability beyond agroecological zones studied; carbon value estimates based on assumed market rates.
NOTES: Research funded by IITA and part of CGIAR programs on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS) and Cocoa Soils. Supports integration of cocoa agroforestry into carbon credit schemes.

TITLE: Editorial: Soil Fertility Management for Sustainable Food Production in sub-Saharan Africa
AUTHORS: Samuel Adjei-Nsiah, Andrews Opoku, Kwame Agyei Frimpong, Isaac Danso
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Sub-Saharan Africa
METHODS: Editorial overview of a special issue containing ten research articles on integrated soil fertility management. Summarizes evidence from case studies across SSA, including the role of organic amendments, rhizobia, satellite data, and modeling for sustainable land and crop management.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Highlights how climate-induced drought and soil degradation are key biophysical constraints to food production in SSA. Emphasizes integrated fertility strategies as a climate adaptation tool.
THEMES/FINDINGS: Integrated soil fertility managementâ€”including the use of organic amendments, rhizobia, and site-specific practicesâ€”can restore soil health, reduce erosion, and improve productivity. â€¨â€¨The editorial showcases a range of studies promoting sustainable intensification and resilience.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Being an editorial, it does not present new data but synthesizes findings across multiple studies.
NOTES: Serves as an introduction to a curated research topic; provides insight into key innovations and interventions promoted by IITA and partners in SSA.

TITLE: Combining temperature-dependent life table data into Insect Life Cycle Model to forecast fall armyworm Spodoptera frugiperda (JE Smith) distribution in maize agro-ecological zones in Africa
AUTHORS: Marian Adan, Henri E. Z. Tonnang, Cojdo E. F. Kassa, Klaus Greve, Christian Borgemeister, Georg Goergen
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Africa (sub-regional focus including Central and West Africa)
METHODS: Laboratory and modeling-based study using temperature-dependent life table data of fall armyworm (FAW) across five temperature levels (20, 25, 28, 30, 32Â°C) combined with the Insect Life Cycle Modeling (ILCYM) software to simulate pest development and forecast distribution across current and future climate scenarios (RCP 2.6 and 8.5 for 2050 and 2070).
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Climate mitigation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Direct focus on modeling pest distribution under climate scenarios. Shows potential FAW range contraction under RCP 2.6 and expansion under RCP 8.5. Highlights temperatureâ€™s critical role in pest phenology and the benefits of climate mitigation for pest control.
THEMES/FINDINGS: FAW development, fecundity, and mortality vary with temperature. 25â€“28Â°C optimal for population growth. â€¨â€¨High temperatures reduce reproduction. FAW activity and establishment risks decline under RCP 2.6, especially in Central Africa, but increase under RCP 8.5. â€¨â€¨Model shows climate mitigation can limit pest pressure.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Does not incorporate precipitation or biotic interactions; predictions are temperature-based only; phenology models validated but subject to regional variation.
NOTES: Model integrates empirical life table data with GIS for pest forecasting. Supported by BMZ/GIZ and IITA. Contributes to climate-smart pest management.

TITLE: Climate-resilient aquatic food systems require transformative change to address gender and intersectional inequalities
AUTHORS: Rahma Adam, Anamika Amani, Rob Kuijpers, Katrine Danielsen, Esther Smits, Froukje Kruijssen, Nicole Moran, Michelle Tigchelaar, Colette Wabnitz, Alexander Tilley, Matteo Luzzi, Rumana H. Peerzadi, Anouk Ride, Cristiano Rossignoli, Edward H. Allison, Steven Cole, Irene Zatti, Kevin Ouko, Cathy Rozel Farnworth
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Global, with case analysis in Bangladesh and regional insights from Asia and Africa
METHODS: Scoping review of 32 empirical studies (2017â€“2022) combined with quantitative analysis of the Bangladesh Integrated Household Survey (2018â€“2019) and data from the Illuminating Hidden Harvests (IHH) project. Explores how gender and intersectional identities affect resilience in aquatic food systems (AFS).
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs

Gender equality, youth, and social inclusion
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Focuses on climate-related shocks and stresses on aquatic food systems. Highlights the disproportionate burden on women and marginalized groups, and the need for gender-responsive, equity-driven climate adaptation strategies.
THEMES/FINDINGS: Gendered and intersectional inequalities shape vulnerability and adaptive capacity in AFS. â€¨â€¨Women face systemic exclusion from decision-making, extension, and financing. Coping strategies often reinforce burdens. â€¨â€¨Data show women perform essential, yet undervalued, roles in AFS value chains. Transformative gender-responsive interventions are needed.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Scoping review limited to English-language peer-reviewed journals; limited sex-disaggregated data in fisheries; quantitative findings focus primarily on Bangladesh.
NOTES: Supports the CGIAR GENDER Impact Platform. Recommends systemic reforms including gender-responsive policy design, intersectional analysis, and recognition of women's roles in fisheries and aquaculture value chains globally.

TITLE: Cocoa agroforestry is less resilient to sub-optimal and extreme climate than cocoa in full sun
AUTHORS: Issaka Abdulai, Philippe Vaast, Munir P. Hoffmann, Richard Asare, Laurence Jassogne, Piet Van Asten, Reimund P. RÃ¶tter, Sophie Graefe
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Ghana (forestâ€“savannah transition zone)
METHODS: Field experiment from 2014 to 2016 comparing cocoa under full sun with cocoa shaded by two tree species (Albizia ferruginea and Antiaris toxicaria) across wet, dry, and extreme dry seasons in Ghana. Monitored microclimate, soil moisture, sap flux density, and transpiration. Assessed resilience and recovery of cocoa under extreme drought conditions (2015/16 El NiÃ±o event).
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Focuses on climate resilience of cocoa systems under extreme heat and drought. Finds that full-sun cocoa was more drought-resilient than shaded systems, challenging assumptions about agroforestry resilience in marginal zones.
THEMES/FINDINGS: Cocoa under shade trees showed higher mortality and lower sap flux during extreme drought than full-sun cocoa. â€¨â€¨The leguminous Albizia ferruginea led to complete cocoa plant death. Soil water competition between shade trees and cocoa reduced system resilience. â€¨â€¨Study suggests reconsidering cocoa agroforestry as a drought-resilient strategy under extreme conditions.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Study site-specific results; sap flux measurements limited by sensor malfunctions; only two shade species tested.
NOTES: Funded by BMZ; links to CGIAR climate programs; relevance for West Africa's cocoa adaptation planning under climate change.

TITLE: Characterization of cocoa production, income diversification and shade tree management along a climate gradient in Ghana
AUTHORS: Issaka Abdulai, Laurence Jassogne, Sophie Graefe, Richard Asare, Piet Van Asten, Peter LÃ¤derach, Philippe Vaast
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Ghana (dry, mid, and wet climate zones)
