METHODS: Cross-sectional observational study with 150 cocoa farmers along a climate gradient in Ghana. Data collection included structured farmer interviews, field inventories, and statistical analyses such as ANOVA and cluster analysis to evaluate cocoa yield, income sources, shade tree species and management, and perceived climate effects.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Explores adaptation to climate variability and drought along a climatic gradient. Farmers in dry regions implement shade tree management and income diversification as adaptation strategies to declining cocoa suitability.
THEMES/FINDINGS: Cocoa yields and income dependence vary significantly by climate zone. Farmers in dry zones adapt through income diversification and use of medium shade systems. â€¨â€¨Shade trees serve ecological and income functions. Agroforestry adaptation needs to be climate-specific.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Inability to assess timber value of trees; variation in certification participation across zones; limited generalizability beyond the studied zones.
NOTES: Research supported by IITA, CGIAR's CCAFS and FTA programs, with funding from the German Ministry for Economic Cooperation and Development (BMZ).

TITLE: The potential of stable carbon isotope ratios and leaf temperature as proxies for drought stress in banana under field conditions
AUTHORS: Mathilde Vantyghem, Roel Merckx, Bert Stevens, Rebecca Hood-Nowotny, Rony Swennen, Gerd Dercon
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: East Africa â€“ Arusha, Tanzania
METHODS: Field experiment on banana plants (mother and daughter) with two irrigation treatments (rainfed vs. irrigated) measured drought stress using stable carbon isotope ratios (Î´13C) and leaf temperature (DANS index). Measurements included volumetric water content, microclimate variables, and leaf-specific thermal and isotopic responses.
IMPACT AREAS: Climate adaptation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Addresses climate adaptation through improved field-based drought stress detection methods; relevant to increasing resilience in rainfed banana systems under climate variability.
THEMES/FINDINGS: Î´13C and DANS are effective drought indicators in banana. Rainfed plants showed significantly higher stress markers. â€¨â€¨Daughter plants were less stressed than mother plants, possibly due to light exposure and internal carbon allocation.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Î´13C interpretation complicated by carbon flux between mother and daughter plants; leaf temperature affected by microenvironment; DANS requires reference plots.
NOTES: Study promotes low-cost, scalable drought assessment tools like handheld infrared thermometers and recommends best practices for banana phenotyping in the field.

TITLE: Prospect for increasing grain legume crop production in East Africa
AUTHORS: Marloes P. van Loon, Nanyan Deng, Patricio Grassini, Juan I. Rattalino Edreira, Endalkachew Wolde-meskel, Frederick Baijukya, HÃ©lÃ¨ne Marrou, Martin K. van Ittersum
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: East Africa â€“ Ethiopia, Kenya, Tanzania
METHODS: Applied crop simulation modeling (SSM-legumes) and a bottom-up spatial framework following Global Yield Gap Atlas protocols to estimate water-limited yield potential (Yw), yield gaps (Yg), and food self-sufficiency scenarios for five legumes: chickpea, common bean, cowpea, groundnut, and pigeonpea in Ethiopia, Kenya, and Tanzania.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Focuses on climate-sensitive yield modeling (rainfed systems) and variability in yield potential, but does not address long-term climate change or mitigation.
THEMES/FINDINGS: Large yield gaps exist for legumes in East Africa, especially in Kenya and Tanzania. â€¨â€¨Closing 80% of the yield gap could help Kenya and Tanzania reach self-sufficiency in legumes by 2050, but not Ethiopia. â€¨â€¨Chickpea and common bean show the highest potential.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Did not include climate change impacts; uncertainties in Kenyaâ€™s yield data; lack of robust water-use efficiency data and on-farm experiments under potential conditions.
NOTES: Chickpea offers off-season growth potential. Common bean is least water-limited and most widely grown legume in region. Calls for more data on legume intercropping systems.

TITLE: Modeling carbon stock dynamics under fallow and cocoa agroforest systems in the shifting agricultural landscape of Central Cameroon
AUTHORS: Francis B. T. Silatsa, Martin Yemefack, Nathalie Ewane-Nonga, Adolph Kemga, Rachid Hanna
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Central Cameroon â€“ Ayos subdivision
METHODS: Field-based study using a 50-year chronosequence across cocoa agroforest (CAF) and fallow systems. Carbon stocks in multiple pools (trees, soil, litter, roots) were empirically measured in 147 plots. Empirical models (logistic and rational quadratic functions) were fitted to estimate carbon stock accretion rates. The study also used rapid assessment methods and allometric equations to estimate above- and below-ground biomass and applied statistical analysis (ANOVA, t-tests) to compare C dynamics.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Climate mitigation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate change mitigation is a central aim; CAF and fallow systems are compared for their GHG reduction potential through carbon sequestration, specifically for use in REDD+ strategies.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
CAF and fallow systems both accumulate up to ~200 Mg C haâ»Â¹ over 30 years.

Maximum carbon growth occurs around 12â€“13 years, tapering off by year 45.

Soil carbon accumulation was higher in CAF than fallow across most of the chronosequence.

CAF offers a more stable and biodiversity-rich system for long-term carbon storage.

Logistic models are well-suited for predicting carbon stock dynamics in land use transitions
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Soil carbon measured only to 30 cm depth, potentially underestimating deeper stocks

Differences in CAF management could affect generalizability

Results may vary with species composition and local practices
NOTES:
Strong recommendation for CAF as an entry point for REDD+

Authors advocate for financial incentives for carbon services to prevent deforestation

Understory vegetation management in CAF contributes significantly to soil carbon

TITLE: Potential of genomics for the improvement of underutilized legumes in subâ€Saharan Africa
AUTHORS: Rajneesh Paliwal, Taofeek Tope Adegboyega, Michael Abberton, Ben Faloye, Olaniyi Oyatomi
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Sub-Saharan Africa (focus on Nigeria, Ghana, Cameroon, Benin, Burkina Faso, Ethiopia)
METHODS: This review presents current genomic resources and breeding strategies for underutilized legumesâ€”African yam bean, Bambara groundnut, and Kerstingâ€™s groundnutâ€”held at IITAâ€™s GRC. It synthesizes developments in genomic-assisted breeding (e.g., DArTseq, GWAS, QTL mapping) and discusses challenges and opportunities for improving these crops in the face of climate change, food insecurity, and genetic erosion.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The article extensively discusses how climate variability (drought, poor soils) is driving the need to breed and promote resilient legumes using genomic tools.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
IITAâ€™s GRC maintains the worldâ€™s largest collections of Bambara, AYB, and Kerstingâ€™s groundnut.

Genomic tools such as GWAS, DArTseq, and CRISPR are being applied for drought, yield, and nutritional traits.

These legumes are ideal for marginal environments and offer high nutritional and ecological value.

There are gaps in geographic representation in collections, especially for AYB and Kerstingâ€™s groundnut.

Genomic research is beginning to catch up to major crops, but still lags in funding and visibility.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Limited genomic and breeding investment for some species (e.g., Kerstingâ€™s groundnut)

Lack of broad geographic representation in genebank collections

Genomic tools (e.g., GS, QTL mapping) still in early stages for many crops
NOTES:
Funded by CGIAR and Global Crop Diversity Trust

Highlights AOCC and ACACIAâ€™s roles in sequencing underutilized crops

Provides detailed catalog of genotyping and molecular breeding efforts for multiple legumes

TITLE: A review of biotechnological approaches towards crop improvement in African yam bean (Sphenostylis stenocarpa Hochst. Ex A. Rich.)
AUTHORS: Olubusayo O. Oluwole, Oluwadurotimi S. Aworunse, Ademola I. Aina, Olusola L. Oyesola, Jacob O. Popoola, Olaniyi A. Oyatomi, Michael T. Abberton, Olawole O. Obembe
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Sub-Saharan Africa (Nigeria, Ghana, Ethiopia, Kenya, Malawi)
METHODS: This article is a literature review of 109 studies covering morphological, genomic, and biotechnological research on African yam bean (AYB). It outlines progress in tissue culture, molecular markers, genome sequencing, and omics tools, and highlights bottlenecks and opportunities for breeding AYB for resilience, nutrition, and productivity.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security
NOTES ON CLIMATE: AYBâ€™s suitability for poor soils and dry zones is discussed throughout, establishing its role in climate adaptation, although mitigation is not addressed.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
AYB has high genetic diversity but remains underutilized and low-yielding.

Advances in molecular breeding, genome sequencing, and gene editing (CRISPR) offer opportunities for rapid improvement.

Current limitations include lack of a reference genome, long cooking time, disease susceptibility, and inadequate farmer awareness.

Whole genome sequencing and GWAS are ongoing via IITAâ€™s Genetic Resources Centre.
NOTES:
Highlights IITAâ€™s collection of AYB accessions and ongoing efforts with ACACIA and other institutions to sequence the AYB genome.

Points to CRISPR/Cas9 as a future tool to reduce anti-nutritional factors, improve cooking time, and enhance stress tolerance.

TITLE: Narrowing the rice yield gap in East and Southern Africa: Using and adapting existing technologies
AUTHORS: Nhamo Nhamo, Jonne Rodenburg, Negussie Zenna, Godswill Makombe, Ashura Luzi-Kihupi
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: East and Southern Africa (including Madagascar, Tanzania, Uganda, Kenya, Rwanda, Malawi, Mozambique, Burundi)
METHODS: This is a literature synthesis and meta-analysis drawing from 105 journal articles and FAOSTAT. It calculates relative yield gains from improved rice technologies (nutrient, water, weed management, and improved varieties) and uses partial budgeting for economic evaluation. It explores interactions between agronomic practices and socioeconomic conditions, and proposes adapted Good Agricultural Practices (GAP) as a framework to reduce yield gaps.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate adaptation is a recurring theme; strategies include bunding, conservation agriculture, SRI, and site-specific fertilizer practices to combat rainfall variability and land degradation. Mitigation is not mentioned.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Highest relative yield gains were from weed management (91.6%) and organic fertilizers (90.4%).

Adoption is hindered by socioeconomic factors like labor constraints and limited access to inputs.

Recommends use of decision-support tools and locally adapted technologies rather than blanket recommendations.

Integrated practices yield more than isolated ones, yet are underused.

Stresses the need for participatory research and technology adaptation.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Lack of disaggregated data on farm-level yields

Low technology adoption due to labor, financial, and knowledge constraints

Most studies are isolated interventions, not integrated approaches

Limited empirical evaluation of integrated GAP packages
NOTES:
Recommends moving beyond generic technology packages to tailor-fit solutions.

Introduces interactive decision support tools (e.g., AFROweeds, Nutrient Manager) under development.

Proposes use of farm typologies to target interventions efficiently.

Focused on smallholder rice systems in ESA but methods applicable to other crops.

TITLE: Phenotypic characterization of maize landraces from Sahel and Coastal West Africa reveals marked diversity and potential for genetic improvement
AUTHORS: Charles Nelimor, Baffour Badu-Apraku, Simon P. A. Nguetta, Antonia Y. Tetteh, Ana LuÃ­sa Garcia-Oliveira
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: West Africa (Burkina Faso, Ghana, Togo)
METHODS:
Evaluated 196 landraces and 14 improved maize varieties at IITA-Nigeria using 26 agro-morphological traits across two seasons.

Used a lattice design with replications, followed by ANOVA, REML estimation, and cluster analysis.

Conducted PCA, biplot, and path coefficient analysis to understand trait contributions.

Mahalanobis distances and Shannon-Weaver diversity index (H') calculated to assess genetic distances and trait variability.

Stepwise regression identified key contributors to grain yield.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Stresses that climate change is reducing maize productivity and highlights early-flowering, short-statured landraces as a means of adaptation to heat and drought. No mitigation elements are included.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Burkinabe landraces are genetically distinct from improved varieties and other landraces.

Traits like early flowering, good ear husk cover, and stay-green correlate with grain yield.

Shannon-Weaver index confirms higher diversity in landraces than improved lines.

Landraces can address breeding gaps in stress-prone environments.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Single-location trial may overestimate repeatability.

Some improved varieties may have been misclassified as landraces.

Limited genetic/environmental characterization of some accessions.

High environmental control may mask trait-environment interactions.
NOTES:
Results support targeted hybridization between Sahelian and Coastal landraces to balance earliness and yield.

Suggests urgent need to systematically screen landraces for combined abiotic stress tolerance.

Funded by Gates Foundation and WASCAL.

TITLE: Long-term spatial-temporal trends and variability of rainfall over Eastern and Southern Africa
AUTHORS: Francis Kamau Muthoni, Vincent Omondi Odongo, Justus Ochieng, Edward M. Mugalavai, Sixbert Kajumula Mourice, Irmgard Hoesche-Zeledon, Mulundu Mwila, Mateete Bekunda
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Eastern and Southern Africa (Tanzania, Kenya, Uganda, Rwanda, Burundi, Zambia, Malawi)
METHODS: Used 37 years (1981â€“2017) of CHIRPS-v2 satellite rainfall data validated against 4,243 monthly rain gauge observations from 65 stations. Validation employed modified Kling-Gupta Efficiency (KGE), RMSE, and correlation analysis. Rainfall trends were analyzed using modified Mann-Kendall and Theil-Sen slope estimators, and spatial-temporal variability assessed using rainfall anomalies and coefficient of variation. Trend significance tested at p < 0.1, adjusted for serial autocorrelation.
IMPACT AREAS: Climate adaptation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Strong focus on adaptation. Discusses how inter- and intra-annual variability affects crop performance and CSA uptake, emphasizing the role of spatially explicit data in informing interventions.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
CHIRPS-v2 is reliable for ESA (KGE = 0.68)

Rainfall increasing in southwest Zambia and northern Lake Victoria Basin

Declines observed in Mt. Kilimanjaro, SE Kenya, SW Rwanda

High inter-annual variability in NE Kenya

Monthly variability often more extreme than annual trends
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Satellite estimates biased by topography and algorithm assumptions

Sparse rain gauge networks in ESA reduce validation power

Over- and under-estimation at low and high rainfall intensities

CHIRPS-v2 overestimates long rains in mountainous areas
NOTES:
Emphasizes need for pixel-level rainfall maps to guide varietal selection and CSA trials

Suggests use in recommendation domain mapping and climate-resilient programming

Funded by USAID via Africa RISING under Feed the Future

TITLE: Mechanisms and approaches towards enhanced drought tolerance in cassava (Manihot esculenta)
AUTHORS: Samwel K. Muiruri, Valentine O. Ntui, Leena Tripathi, Jaindra N. Tripathi
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Sub-Saharan Africa (focus on cassava systems, with mention of Uganda, Nigeria, Tanzania, Mozambique)
METHODS: The article is a comprehensive review of physiological, molecular, and genetic mechanisms contributing to drought tolerance in cassava. It covers conventional breeding challenges, genomics, gene expression analysis, and the role of biotechnologies such as genetic engineering and CRISPR/Cas9 in enhancing drought resilience. It references numerous empirical studies, but itself presents no original data.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate change is the backdrop of the paper; the authors argue that biotechnology is essential to adapt cassava to increasing drought stress across Africa.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Drought tolerance in cassava involves four physiological strategies: avoidance, escape, tolerance, and recovery

Conventional breeding is limited by cassavaâ€™s heterozygosity, poor flowering, and propagation constraints

CRISPR/Cas9 and gene overexpression (e.g., IPT, DREB, AREB1) show promise for engineering drought tolerance

Identification of QTLs and drought-related genes (e.g., MeRD28, MeZFP, MeALDH) paves the way for marker-assisted breeding

Long non-coding RNAs (lncRNAs) are also implicated in drought signaling pathways in cassava
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Functional validation of candidate genes remains limited

Genetic engineering in cassava is challenged by low transformation efficiency and regulatory hurdles

Most gene identification work is based on homology with model species, not functional studies in cassava
NOTES:
Funded by CGIARâ€™s Roots, Tubers, and Bananas Program

Includes several tables summarizing drought-tolerance genes, markers, and biotechnological studies across crops

Emphasizes combining conventional and modern tools to accelerate cassava improvement

TITLE: Phenotyping cowpea for seedling root architecture reveals root phenes important for breeding phosphorus efficient varieties
AUTHORS: Saba B. Mohammed, James D. Burridge, Mohammad F. Ishiyaku, Ousmane Boukar, Jonathan P. Lynch
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Nigeria (Zaria and Kano)
METHODS: This study evaluated 50 elite cowpea genotypes for seedling root phenotypes (root hairs and architecture) using controlled growth chamber conditions. A subset of 20 genotypes was field-tested under high and low phosphorus (P) conditions in Kano and Zaria, Nigeria. Traits included root hair density/length, branching density, primary root length, and field performance (fodder and grain yield). Statistical methods included PCA, heritability estimates, correlation, and linear discriminant analysis (LDA).
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Cowpeaâ€™s tolerance to drought and poor soil fertility is central. The study explores how phenes like deep primary roots and dense root hairs can improve performance in low-P and water-limited conditions.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Significant variation in root traits across 50 genotypes

High heritability for primary root length (77%) and basal root number (72%)

Field trials confirmed that traits like lateral root hair density correlate with grain yield under low phosphorus

PCA revealed three distinct phenotypic clusters

Seedling root traits like hair length/density can guide selection for drought/P stress tolerance

No direct correlation found between seed size and root phenotypes
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Root traits were evaluated at seedling stage; limited field validation

Root hair phenotyping is still labor-intensive and may lack precision in field conditions

Limited correlations between seedling and mature plant traits
NOTES:
Field sites in Kano and Zaria had inherently low soil phosphorus

Root hair phenes like LRHD were positively associated with yield under low P (r = 0.65, p < .01)

Potential for root traits to contribute to cost-effective breeding in resource-limited environments

TITLE: Lessons from integrated seasonal forecastâ€“crop modelling in Africa: A systematic review
AUTHORS: Siyabusa Mkuhlani, Nkulumo Zinyengere, Naomi Kumi, Olivier Crespo
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Sub-Saharan Africa (primarily West Africa, with some studies from East and Southern Africa)
METHODS: This systematic review analyzed 19 peer-reviewed studies published between 2007â€“2022 that integrate seasonal forecast information with crop models in African contexts. The review assessed geographic coverage, forecast and model types, crops studied, farm management practices modeled, and the decision-making applications. Methodologies covered included hindcasts, dynamic and statistical forecasts, stochastic disaggregation, and GCM-based simulation tools like DSSAT, APSIM, and GLAM.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Climate mitigation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate variability is positioned as the greatest threat to rainfed agriculture in Africa. The study promotes forecastâ€“model integration as a solution to improve resilience, farm decisions, and early warning systems.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Most reviewed studies were in West Africa; maize was the most frequently modeled crop

Mechanistic models (e.g., DSSAT, APSIM) were used in 74% of the studies

GCMs and stochastic disaggregation were key techniques to convert forecasts into model inputs

Most applications supported tactical decisions (e.g., planting dates, cultivar choice)

Only 2 of 19 studies linked modeling outcomes to policy

Integration still limited by lack of daily weather data and farmer-accessible information
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Geographic and thematic gaps (Central and North Africa underrepresented)

Weak coupling between forecasts and crop models due to incompatible data scales

Underuse of integrated models in national agricultural policy
NOTES:
Proposes better packaging of forecastâ€“model insights for smallholders

Advocates for broader application to non-cereal crops and wider African regions

Stresses the need to bridge researchâ€“practice divides through stakeholder co-production of knowledge

TITLE: Protein-rich legume and pseudo-cereal crop suitability under present and future European climates
AUTHORS: Rhys Manners, Consuelo Varela-Ortega, Jacob van Etten
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: European Union (EU-28 countries)
METHODS: The study models the present and future (2050) suitability of 13 protein-rich crops (legumes and pseudo-cereals) across the EU using the EcoCrop model under RCP4.5. It uses downscaled climate data from 30 GCMs and incorporates soil pH data to analyze suitability shifts and identify promising future crops under climate change. Sensitivity analyses and validation against current production data are included.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate suitability is central; analysis directly compares current and future crop suitability across 30 GCMs under RCP4.5.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Suitability for cool-weather legumes (e.g., chickpea, lentil, faba bean) declines in southern Europe

Suitability for warm-weather crops and pseudo-cereals (e.g., quinoa, cowpea) increases in northern Europe

Climate suitability is a key limiting factor in current crop distribution

Quinoa emerges as the most climate-resilient protein crop for future Europe

Geographic variation in climate impacts calls for differentiated R&D and breeding strategies
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
EcoCrop is a coarse model and lacks calibration to detailed crop responses

Climate data uncertainty and soil variability are limiting factors

Does not include socioeconomic constraints on adoption
NOTES:
Supported by CGIAR CCAFS and EU Horizon 2020 (PROTEIN2FOOD project)

Suggests shift in EU protein crop strategy for health, sustainability, and climate resilience

TITLE: Pheromone traps and climate variations influence populations of Sahlbergella singularis and associated damage of cocoa in Cameroon
AUTHORS: Hermine C. Mahot, LeÃ¯la Bagny Beilhe, Raymond J. Mahob, et al.
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Cameroon (Ayos and Konye cocoa-growing regions)
METHODS: A 2-year field study in 15 cocoa plantations evaluated the effectiveness of pheromone traps in managing Sahlbergella singularis (a major cocoa pest). The study examined relationships between climate variables (temperature, humidity, rainfall), pest abundance (trap and visual counts), and cocoa pod damage. Structural equation modeling was used to analyze interactions between pest populations, climate, and crop damage dynamics.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate variability (e.g., relative humidity, temperature) is a central variable in pest damage modeling. Climate-linked seasonal pest dynamics are explicitly explored.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Pheromone traps reduce mirid populations and cocoa pod damage

Relative humidity is a significant environmental driver of damage severity

Visual pod damage may serve as a proxy for mirid population levels

Cocoa pod availability is a key determinant of mirid population size

Pheromone traps may offer a scalable alternative to conventional insecticides in IPM frameworks
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
No blank traps in control plots (assumption-based control)

Only short-term (2-year) data

Other unmeasured factors may contribute to mirid population fluctuations
NOTES:
Funded by SNV, GIZ, and CGIAR Humidtropics

Practical implications for IPM scale-up in Central Africa

Highlights pest-climate-resource dynamics as critical under climate change

TITLE: Shifting focus from external to in situ organic resources â€“ The redesign of four tropical long-term experiments
AUTHORS: Moritz Laub, Marc Corbeels, Samuel Mathu Ndungu, Monicah Wanjiku Mucheru-Muna, Daniel Mugendi, Rebecca Yegon, Wycliffe Waswa, Bernard Vanlauwe, Johan Six
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Kenya (Aludeka, Sidada, Embu, Machanga)
METHODS: This paper documents the 20-year evolution and subsequent redesign of four long-term experiments (LTEs) in Kenya. The initial LTEs tested organic resource quality and quantity with/without mineral N on SOC and maize yield. Results showed degradation across most treatments. In response, the LTEs were redesigned via co-design with stakeholders to focus on regenerative cropping systemsâ€”emphasizing in situ root inputs over external amendments. Redesign strategies included maize-legume intercropping (MBILI), relay with forage grasses, and rotation with legumes, evaluated by degradation level.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Climate mitigation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Strong and explicit: soil carbon sequestration framed for both adaptation and mitigation, linked to global initiatives and farmer-oriented solutions.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
SOC declined in most treatments, despite external organic inputs

External low-C:N resources (e.g., Tithonia, manure) helped, but were insufficient alone

Root inputs hypothesized as more effective for SOC regeneration

New systems (MBILI, grass relay, rotations) co-designed with local stakeholders

Treatments re-randomized across degradation levels to track recovery

Plans for continued monitoring of SOC fractions and productivity
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Unrealistically high rates of organics in original design

Gaps in SOC data at some sites due to transitions in management

Deep SOC sampling less reliable than surface samples
NOTES:
Redesign aligns with modern SOC theories (microbial necromass, root-based formation)

Strong participatory and cross-institutional collaboration (IITA, CIRAD, ETH, KU, Univ. of Embu)

Planned indicators: root biomass, microbial biomass, mineral-associated SOC, yield-SOC tradeoffs

TITLE: Rural transformation and the future of cereal-based agri-food systems
AUTHORS: Gideon Kruseman, Khondoker Abdul Mottaleb, Kindie Tesfaye, Subir Bairagi, Richard Robertson, Diagne Mandiaye, Aymen Frija, Sika Gbegbelegbe, Arega Alene, Steve Prager
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Global with regional focus on Asia, Sub-Saharan Africa (SSA), Latin America, MENA
METHODS: The paper uses the DPSIR (Drivers, Pressures, State, Impact, Response) framework and CGIARâ€™s IMPACT model to examine how rural transformation processes influence cereal-based agri-food systems and investment needs. It includes scenario analysis for different R&D investment strategies across regions and cereals (rice, wheat, maize), with foresight modeling to 2050.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs

Gender equality, youth, and social inclusion
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate change is integrated in all scenario modeling (RCP8.5). Effects vary by region and cereal type (e.g., maize hardest hit in SSA).
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Rural transformation is central to the future of agri-food systems

Investment in cereal R&D can significantly improve food supply, particularly in SSA and Asia

Climate change reduces cereal yield potential and raises food prices

Gender, migration, and market access mediate transformation outcomes

R&D investments must be region-specific, informed by rural trends and environmental constraints
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
IMPACT model lacks ability to fully capture rural transformation nuances

Gender and social differentiation are underrepresented

Model assumptions limit direct policy application without contextual refinement
NOTES:
Funded by CGIAR CRPs (MAIZE, RICE, WHEAT, PIM)

Provides region-specific insights using foresight tools

Strong call for iterative modeling plus localized rural diagnostics

TITLE: Resistance Genes in Global Crop Breeding Networks
AUTHORS: K.A. Garrett, K.F. Andersen, F. Asche, R.L. Bowden, G.A. Forbes, P.A. Kulakow, B. Zhou
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: N/a
METHODS: The paper uses expert elicitation and systems analysis to map and model global crop breeding networks for cassava, potato, rice, and wheat. It applies concepts from complex adaptive systems (CAS) and epidemic network theory to assess how resistance genes are developed, shared, and deployed globally. It evaluates the role of CGIAR centers, including IITA, in shaping disease resistance strategies through gene flow in breeding systems.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate change is a key system stressor shaping pathogen evolution, germplasm needs, and breeding network design.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
CGIAR centers, including IITA, are central hubs in resistance gene flow

Modularity and connectivity of networks affect the speed and equity of resistance deployment

Privatization and IP law may constrain sharing and coordination

Policies should support diversity, feedback loops, and global surveillance to maintain gene effectiveness

Network resilience depends on redundancy, decentralized participation, and effective governance
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Bias from expert elicitation

Lack of data on private breeding network structures

Limited capacity in regional breeding programs
NOTES:
Highlights the unique structure of cassava networks (public-sector dominant)

Advocates for stewardship plans to prevent resistance breakdown

Funded in part by CCAFS and the NextGen Cassava project

TITLE: Local adaptation in thermal tolerance for a tropical butterfly across ecotone and rainforest habitats
AUTHORS: Michel A. K. Dongmo, Rachid Hanna, Thomas B. Smith, K.K.M. Fiaboe, Abraham Fomena, Timothy C. Bonebrake
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Cameroon (ecotone and rainforest habitats)
METHODS: The study assessed critical thermal maxima and minima (CTmax, CTmin) of Bicyclus dorothea butterflies from ecotone and forest habitats in Cameroon. Second-generation lab-reared butterflies were tested under controlled temperatures to evaluate whether differences in thermal tolerance had a genetic basis, indicating local adaptation. Microclimate modeling was also conducted to relate field conditions to thermal limits.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The study directly links butterfly thermal tolerance to climate change vulnerability, showing that populations from variable (ecotone) habitats have higher CTmax and lower CTmin, suggesting greater resilience to warming.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Local adaptation in thermal tolerance was observed between rainforest and ecotone populations.

These differences persisted across generations, suggesting a genetic component.

Microclimatic simulations showed temperature variation consistent with habitat-driven selection pressures.

The study contributes to understanding how physiological traits influence vulnerability to climate change in tropical ectotherms.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Only one rearing temperature used (26Â°C)

CTmax ramping rate may not reflect ecological extremes

Does not assess field fitness or behavioral thermoregulation

Findings are limited to one butterfly species and region
NOTES:
Supports broader discussions around microclimate buffering and physiological plasticity as responses to warming.

Highlights the need for additional research into evolutionary and ecological resilience in tropical species.

TITLE: Organic matter cycling along geochemical, geomorphic, and disturbance gradients in forest and cropland of the African Tropics â€“ project TropSOC database version 1.0
AUTHORS: Sebastian Doetterl et al.
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Central and East Africa â€“ Democratic Republic of the Congo, Rwanda, Uganda
METHODS: This study presents TropSOC v1.0, a landscape-scale open-access database combining soil, plant, and climate data across 136 forest and cropland sites. Sampling occurred along gradients of erosion, land use, and soil parent material across three distinct geochemical regions. The study included soil physical and chemical properties, microbial activity, vegetation traits, and above/below-ground biomass data. Monitoring spanned 2017â€“2020.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate change is referenced as a background driver of degradation and ecosystem stress but is not the focus of analysis or intervention.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Soil carbon stabilization is highly variable across landforms and soil types.

Cropland systems often show severe degradation of organic matter stocks.

The data fill gaps in understanding of African tropical soil processes and support modeling of biogeochemical cycles.

TropSOC is positioned as a baseline for future research on restoration, land management, and ecosystem functioning.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
SOC dynamics in tropical ecosystems are understudied; extrapolation should be cautious

No socioeconomic, gender, or mitigation-related variables included

Designed for future modeling and analysis, not immediate policy guidance
NOTES:
TropSOC is foundational for regional soil-health and carbon stock assessments in African tropics.

Open-access data can be linked with agronomic or socioeconomic datasets for future impact studies.

TITLE: Push-pull strategy combined with net houses for controlling cowpea insect pests and enhancing crop yields
AUTHORS: Seydou Diabate, Thibaud Martin, Lucy Kananu Murungi, Komi K.M. Fiaboe, John Wesonga, John M. Kimani, Emilie Deletre
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Kenya (Kirinyaga County, Central Kenya)
METHODS: This field-based study assessed the effectiveness of three pest management strategiesâ€”(1) push-pull in open fields, (2) net house, and (3) combined net house + push-pullâ€”on pest reduction and cowpea yields. It was conducted over dry and rainy seasons in Kenya, using statistical analyses of pest infestations and yield outcomes across 16 experimental plots. Push plants (lemongrass, Mexican marigold) and visual lures (sticky traps) were tested alongside netting technology to reduce pest pressure without pesticides.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The study frames net houses and push-pull strategies as climate-resilient pest control alternatives that reduce reliance on chemical pesticides and buffer weather variability in tropical farming systems.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Net houses (with or without push-pull) consistently reduced infestation by large pests such as Maruca vitrata, Empoasca sp., and Clavigralla tomentosicollis.

Small pest control was variableâ€”Aphis craccivora sometimes increased in net houses due to absence of natural enemies.

Crop yields and quality were significantly higher in net house and net house + push-pull treatments, especially during the rainy season.

Push-pull alone had limited added value beyond the net house but did reduce Empoasca sp. in the rainy season.

Off-season cowpea production is viable under net house conditions, helping stabilize income and food supply.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Aphid outbreaks in net houses not fully controlled; repellent plant density may have been insufficient

Push-pull strategy had limited efficacy on certain pests

Netting altered microclimate, potentially favoring some pests without natural predators
NOTES:
The study suggests that combining physical barriers and ecological pest control improves resilience of legume cropping systems in unpredictable weather conditions.

Recommends further refinement of repellent use and integration of biocontrol agents in net house systems.

TITLE: Plant-Parasitic Nematodes and Food Security in Sub-Saharan Africa
AUTHORS: Danny L. Coyne, Laura Cortada, Johnathan J. Dalzell, Abiodun O. Claudius-Cole, Solveig Haukeland, Nessie Luambano, Herbert Talwana
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Sub-Saharan Africa (regional focus including Nigeria, Kenya, Uganda, Tanzania, CÃ´te dâ€™Ivoire, Benin, South Africa)
METHODS: This review synthesizes evidence on the impacts of plant-parasitic nematodes on crop production and food security in Sub-Saharan Africa. It draws on regional case studies, nematology surveys, and personal experiences of the authors to highlight key pest threats (especially root-knot nematodes), their interaction with climate change, and systemic gaps in nematology research, diagnostics, policy, and farmer awareness. The paper emphasizes sustainable management and biotechnology solutions for intensification and resilience.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate change is predicted to intensify nematode impacts by altering species distribution, shortening life cycles, and increasing crop stress. The paper urges greater inclusion of nematodes in climate-resilient breeding and water-use efficiency programs.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Root-knot nematodes (Meloidogyne spp.) are the most pervasive biotic threat across SSA.

Crop losses from nematodes are massive but underreported due to diagnostic and awareness gaps.

Vegetatively propagated crops like banana, yam, and cassava are especially vulnerable due to infected planting materials.

Biotechnology (e.g., RNAi, resistant genes, engineered rhizosphere microbes) offers promising control strategies.

There is a need for stronger policy support, diagnostics, training, and seed system reform.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Scarcity of species-level diagnostics across SSA

Lack of reliable economic loss data

Fragmented policy and phytosanitary enforcement

Limited awareness among national governments despite rising expertise
NOTES:
The article is a comprehensive call to elevate nematology in African agriculture and climate strategies.

IITA is identified as a central hub for research, diagnostics, and capacity development through its Nairobi office and broader regional platforms.

TITLE: Public sector soybean (Glycine max) breeding: Advances in cultivar development in the African tropics
AUTHORS: Godfree Chigeza, Steve Boahen, Melaku Gedil, Eric Agoyi, Hapson Mushoriwa, Nicholas Denwar, Therese Gondwe, Abush Tesfaye, Alpha Kamara, Oladeji Emmanuel Alamu, David Chikoye
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Sub-Saharan Africa (notably Nigeria, Mozambique, Malawi, Zambia, Ghana, Kenya, Benin, and Tanzania)
IITA OFFICE: IITA Nigeria, IITA Mozambique, IITA Zambia
METHODS: This review article documents four decades of public sector soybean breeding led by IITA. It describes breeding goals (drought tolerance, disease resistance, nodulation), regional pipelines, and outcomes from the Tropical Legume projects. The study also details methodologies like participatory variety selection (PVS), germplasm diversity assessment, and molecular breeding tools (e.g., SSRs, SNPs). Results from multilocation trials and variety releases are summarized across 15 African countries.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs

Gender equality, youth, and social inclusion
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Breeding priorities have shifted to meet the demands of climate change, especially through drought tolerance, heat resistance, and low phosphorus efficiency.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
IITA-led efforts have led to over 100 soybean variety releases in Africa.

Key innovations include early- to medium-maturing, rust-resistant, and promiscuously nodulating lines.

The Tropical Legume projects significantly expanded adoption, especially in Nigeria and Malawi.

PVS and decentralized testing were critical in tailoring varieties to agro-ecological zones.

Genomics and trait mapping are becoming integral to modern soybean breeding pipelines.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Limited use of genomic tools in public breeding compared to private sector

Soybean still under-researched relative to other crops in Africa

Genetic bottlenecks in breeding populations require greater germplasm introgression
NOTES:
The paper outlines the role of regional breeding hubs and proposes a stage-gate breeding framework for future product development.

Emphasizes seed system innovations and partnerships to accelerate delivery and adoption of new varieties.

TITLE: Potential impact of climate change on whiteflies and implications for the spread of vectored viruses
AUTHORS: Oluwatosin Z. Aregbesola, James P. Legg, Lene Sigsgaard, Ole S. Lund, Carmelo Rapisarda
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Global (with implications for Sub-Saharan Africa, Europe, and Asia)
METHODS: Narrative literature review assessing how climate change affects life history, population dynamics, and management of whiteflies and their role in transmitting plant viruses. Analyzed empirical and modeling studies across geographies on vector thermal tolerance, generation time, and virus epidemiology.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate change increases thermotolerance and geographic spread of whiteflies such as *Bemisia tabaci*. Higher temperatures may reduce fecundity, longevity, and survival depending on species, but may also facilitate virus epidemics via range shifts and prolonged vector activity.
THEMES/FINDINGS: Warmer temperatures may shift virus-vector dynamics, increasing outbreaks. â€¨â€¨Control methods like natural enemies and insecticides may remain effective, but require localized adaptation. â€¨â€¨Need for multi-trophic research and climate-smart pest management is emphasized. â€¨â€¨The paper highlights trade-offs between stress adaptation and reproductive traits in whiteflies.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Review-based; projections vary with species and location; no primary empirical data; interaction effects of multiple climate stressors need more study.
NOTES: Funded by the European Commission Erasmus Mundus program and CGIAR Roots, Tubers and Bananas. Suggests use of multi-species models for future studies.

TITLE: Life history and temperature-dependence of cassava-colonising populations of Bemisia tabaci
AUTHORS: Oluwatosin Z. Aregbesola, James P. Legg, Ole S. Lund, Lene Sigsgaard, Martin Sporleder, Pedro Carhuapoma, Carmelo Rapisarda
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Tanzania (Dar es Salaam)
METHODS: Experimental study on cassava-colonizing B. tabaci (SSA-ESA) across six constant temperatures (16â€“36Â°C) in controlled chambers and in field conditions. Used ILCYM software to model development, mortality, fecundity, and population dynamics. Life-table parameters estimated under both conditions; survival and development rates modeled with Taylor, BriÃ¨re, and Logan Tb models.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Assesses how climate-induced temperature changes affect the life cycle, fecundity, and survival of B. tabaci on cassava. Findings support pest risk mapping and adaptation planning for future climate conditions.
THEMES/FINDINGS: B. tabaci developed fastest and reproduced most at 24â€“28Â°C; 36Â°C was lethal. â€¨â€¨Peak fecundity (114 eggs/female) was at 20Â°C. â€¨â€¨Adult longevity and immature survival were lowest at temperature extremes. â€¨â€¨Development rates and population growth metrics were modeled and validated. â€¨â€¨Field and lab conditions confirmed higher mortality at early instars and temperature sensitivity across life stages. â€¨â€¨Study supports targeted climate-smart IPM.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Only one B. tabaci genotype and one cassava variety tested; does not address multi-stressor effects or geographic genetic variability.
NOTES: Funded by the International Potato Center and CGIAR. Results contribute to predictive modeling of pest behavior under climate change scenarios.

TITLE: Production Characteristics and Strategies for Adapting to the Impacts of Climate Change on Cassava Whiteflies and Viruses in Tanzania
AUTHORS: Oluwatosin Zacheus Aregbesola, Veronica Nwakaego Uzokwe, Kolawole Adelekan Adeloye, Carmelo Rapisarda, Ole SÃ¸gaard Lund, Lene Sigsgaard, James Peter Legg
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Tanzania (Kakonko, Mkuranga, Unguja)
METHODS: Survey interviews with 320 farmers across three regions, plus 20 expert interviews. Used structured questionnaires, desk reviews, and field observations. Analysis employed SPSS with chi-square and binomial tests. Investigated socio-economic characteristics, adaptation strategies, and control of cassava viruses and whiteflies due to climate change.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Emphasizes climate change as a driver of increased cassava virus and whitefly pressure. Discusses adaptation through pest management, phytosanitation, and novel vector control strategies. Calls for improved extension and institutional support to build adaptive capacity.
THEMES/FINDINGS: Only 38% of farmers controlled cassava viruses and 20% controlled whiteflies. â€¨â€¨Control methods included roguing, clean seed use, and resistant varieties. â€¨â€¨Farmersâ€™ ability to adapt was influenced by extension access, income, and sources of planting materials. â€¨â€¨Experts recommended IPM, monitoring networks, and training to boost adaptation. â€¨â€¨Adoption was hindered by low awareness and resource access.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Survey localized to three regions; limited uptake of pest control limits generalizability; heavy reliance on self-reported practices and perceptions.
NOTES: Study funded by Erasmus Mundus Joint Doctorate and supported by CGIAR-RTB. Emphasizes need for national adaptation strategies linked to cassava health.

TITLE: Selection of extra-early white quality protein maize (Zea mays L.) inbred lines for drought and low soil nitrogen resilient hybrid production
AUTHORS: Benjamin Annor and Baffour Badu-Apraku
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Nigeria (Bagauda, Ikenne, Mokwa, Ile-Ife)
METHODS: Field trials with 96 QPM inbred lines and 4 checks conducted in multi-location trials under drought, low nitrogen, and optimal conditions. Traits evaluated included grain yield, days to silking, plant height, ears per plant, stay-green rating, and more. A base index (BI) was used to assess stress resilience. Statistical methods included ANOVA, heritability estimation, and GGE biplot analysis to evaluate genotype performance and stability.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Targeted combined drought and low soil nitrogen resilience for maize in SSA. Emphasizes cultivar development to overcome dual climate-related stressors.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
57 out of 96 inbred lines showed resilience under combined drought and low nitrogen conditions. 

TZEEQI 7 and TZEEQI 60 were the most stable and high-yielding. 

Grain yield positively correlated with ears per plant and negatively with anthesis-silking interval and leaf death. 

Traits had moderate to high heritability, indicating potential for selection. 

Stress-resilient QPM inbreds can improve food security and reduce malnutrition in SSA.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Stress trials conducted in 2012; while relevant, newer environmental variations not accounted for. No direct socioeconomic assessment included.
NOTES: Backed by Gates Foundation and Stress Tolerant Maize for Africa (STMA) initiative. Data available via IITA CKAN repository.

TITLE: Progress in understanding Pseudocercospora banana pathogens and the development of resistant Musa germplasm
AUTHORS: A. E. Alakonya, J. Kimunye, G. Mahuku, D. Amah, B. Uwimana, A. Brown, R. Swennen
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Tropical and Subtropical regions (focus on Africa, Asia, and Latin America)
IITA OFFICE: IITA Nigeria, IITA Uganda, IITA Tanzania
METHODS: Narrative literature review synthesizing pathogen biology, climate impacts, host-pathogen interactions, genomics, and effector protein research related to Pseudocercospora pathogens in banana. Highlights breeding strategies, including molecular tools, resistant germplasm, and transformation protocols.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Climate mitigation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Explores how climate change may shift Pseudocercospora pathogen distributions, intensify disease severity, and affect resistance durability. Models suggest temperature increases could expand banana growing zones but raise water and pathogen pressure.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Pseudocercospora pathogens are spreading and evolving under climate pressure. 

Conventional fungicides are costly and lose effectiveness. 

Genomic and transformation approaches offer tools for breeding resistance. 

Resistant germplasm (e.g., NARITA, FHIA, PITA lines) are in use but more are needed. 

Marker-assisted selection, transcriptomics, and RNAi technologies can support future resistance strategies.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Synthesis based on secondary data; practical implementation of genetic transformation and screening strategies varies across programs and contexts.
NOTES: Funded by USAID and CGIAR Roots, Tubers and Bananas (RTB). Highlights knowledge gaps in pathogen-host dynamics and need for multi-environment resistance screening protocols.

TITLE: Prevalence of Aflatoxin- and Fumonisin-Producing Fungi Associated with Cereal Crops Grown in Zimbabwe and Their Associated Risks in a Climate Change Scenario
AUTHORS: Juliet Akello, Alejandro Ortega-Beltran, Bwalya Katati, Joseph Atehnkeng, Joao Augusto, Chama M. Mwila, George Mahuku, David Chikoye, Ranajit Bandyopadhyay
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Zimbabwe
IITA OFFICE: IITA Zambia, IITA Nigeria, IITA Malawi, IITA Mozambique, IITA Tanzania
METHODS: Field sampling and laboratory analysis of 980 cereal grain samples (maize, sorghum, finger millet, pearl millet) from 12 districts across 4 agroecological zones in Zimbabwe. Fungal identification, mycotoxin quantification (AF, FM), and exposure risk modeling were conducted. Analytical techniques included PDA culture, HPLC validation, and Bayesian risk modeling.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Highlights climate change as a key driver of mycotoxin risk, with hotter and drier zones linked to higher aflatoxin levels, while wetter seasons increase fumonisin prevalence. Recommends dietary diversification as a climate adaptation strategy.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Maize had high prevalence of AF (52%) and FM (89%), with significant exceedances of health safety limits. 

Small grains were much safer. High mycotoxin levels linked to drought and storage conditions. 

Maize consumption poses elevated liver cancer and toxicity risks, especially for children. 

Diversifying diets with small grains could reduce public health risks.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Did not assess chronic exposure over multiple years; focused on Zimbabwe only; FM regulation levels are lacking in some contexts.
NOTES: First national study assessing small grain mycotoxin risks in Zimbabwe. Supports regional policy reforms for grain safety and storage.

TITLE: Physiological responses of cowpea simultaneously exposed to water deficit stress and varying light intensities at vegetative and reproductive growth stages
AUTHORS: O. I. Adeniyi, S. A. Adejumo, M. Fofana, F. T. Adegbehingbe
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Nigeria
METHODS: Pot experiment in a randomized complete block design with factorial arrangement. Two cowpea varieties were subjected to combinations of water deficit stress (four levels) and three levels of light intensity at vegetative and reproductive stages. Physiological parameters (photosynthesis, stomatal conductance, transpiration, chlorophyll content, leaf temperature) and yield traits (pod number, grain weight, biomass) were recorded and analyzed via ANOVA and Duncanâ€™s test.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The study directly investigates how light and drought stress, worsened by climate change, affect physiological and yield performance of cowpea. Demonstrates importance of shade and water-efficient cropping practices under future climate stress.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
High light intensity combined with water deficit stress significantly reduced yield, photosynthesis, and transpiration in cowpea. 

Reduced light intensity improved tolerance to water stress and yield performance. 

IT99K-573-1-1 variety showed greater resilience. 

Vegetative-stage stress had more severe impacts than reproductive-stage stress.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Study was pot-based and may not fully replicate field-scale conditions. Environmental controls were artificial, and soil type was localized.
NOTES: Supports selection and development of drought- and heat-tolerant cowpea varieties suited to low-input environments in West Africa.

TITLE: The power of narratives: Explaining inaction on gender mainstreaming in Ugandaâ€™s climate change policy
AUTHORS: Mariola Acosta, Margit van Wessel, Severine van Bommel, Edidah L. Ampaire, Laurence Jassogne, Peter H. Feindt
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Uganda
METHODS: Narrative policy analysis based on 30 semi-structured expert interviews and 10 multi-stakeholder meetings. The study identifies 22 policy stories and five narrative frames used by Ugandan policymakers to engage with or resist gender mainstreaming in climate adaptation policy. Atlas.ti was used to code and group story elements.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs; Gender equality, youth, and social inclusion
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Examines how climate change policy in Uganda includes gender mainstreaming on paper, but faces ideological, cultural, and political resistance in practice. Narratives reveal how climate discourse intersects with gender resistance and inaction.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Gender mainstreaming in Ugandaâ€™s climate policy is undermined by competing narratives. 

While the Gender Equality narrative is dominant, it is often weakened by shifts toward narratives of ignorance, structural inertia, male supremacy, and reconciliatory approaches. 

Narrative shifts result in blame-shifting, legitimization of inaction, and resource diversion, ultimately limiting gender transformation.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Potential interviewer effect due to international and gender identity of the lead researcher; study reflects narrated stories rather than direct policy outcomes.
NOTES: Highlights ideational power in shaping resistance to gender policy in climate adaptation; relevant to broader gender policy efforts beyond Uganda.

TITLE: Variations in yield gaps of smallholder cocoa systems and the main determining factors along a climate gradient in Ghana
AUTHORS: Issaka Abdulai, Munir P. Hoffmann, Laurence Jassogne, Richard Asare, Sophie Graefe, Hsiao-Hang Tao, Sander Muilerman, Philippe Vaast, Piet Van Asten, Peter LÃ¤derach, Reimund P. RÃ¶tter
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Ghana (climate gradient zones)
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Focuses on climate suitability zones for cocoa and adaptation potential through sustainable intensification, highlighting drought-prone areas and the need for crop diversification in marginal zones.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Yield gaps in cocoa are strongly influenced by zone-specific climatic and socioeconomic factors. 

In dry zones, older farmers and limited labor contribute to lower productivity. 

In wet zones, training and hybrid varieties help reduce yield gaps. 

Sustainable intensification is recommended in suitable zones to offset losses in marginal areas.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Lack of long-term yield trial data; reliance on farmer-reported data; challenges in isolating biophysical and socioeconomic effects due to zone interactions.
NOTES: Part of CGIAR Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS) and Forest, Trees and Agroforestry (FTA) programs.

TITLE: Adaptation Strategies to Climate Change and Impacts on Household Income and Food Security: Evidence from Sahelian Region of Niger
AUTHORS: Seydou Zakari, Germaine Ibro, Bokar Moussa, Tahirou Abdoulaye
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Niger (Regions: Dosso, TillabÃ©ri, Maradi, Zinder)
METHODS: Used data from 1,783 rural households in Niger. Employed binary logit regression to identify determinants of six climate adaptation strategies and used Propensity Score Matching (PSM) to evaluate the impacts on household income and food security.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Strong adaptation focus; explores perceptions of climate variability and household-level responses to climate shocks (e.g., rainfall variability, droughts).
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Crop and income diversification, changing planting times, and drought-tolerant crops are key strategies. 

Adoption improves household income (by ~7,700 FCFA) and food security (7â€“9% more likely to be food secure). 

Credit access, extension services, and climate information are key enabling factors.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: PSM does not account for unobserved bias; findings are based on reported and observed variables only.
NOTES: Religious and spiritual beliefs influence climate perceptions. Radio/TV is the main source of climate info. The study suggests scaling up institutional support to enhance resilience.

TITLE: Impacts of climate variability and food price volatility on household income and food security of farm households in East and West Africa
AUTHORS: Tesfamicheal Wossen, Thomas Berger, Mekbib G. Haile, Christian Troost
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Ethiopia and Ghana
METHODS: Used agent-based modeling (MPMAS) to simulate the impacts of climate and price variability on farm household income and food security. Model integrated household-level data, DSSAT and FAO yield models, climate and CPI records, and simulated adaptation strategies (credit, off-farm income, fertilizer subsidy, improved seed).
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Direct focus on the impact of climate variability (rainfall, temperature) and its interaction with price shocks. Also evaluates adaptation effectiveness under these stressors.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Climate and price variability significantly reduce income and increase food insecurity, especially for poorer households. 

Policy-supported adaptation measures improve income and food security, but benefits are uneven. 

Multi-strategy packages outperform single measures.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Did not model safety nets or kinship-based coping; adaptation costs not fully assessed; simulations rely on stylized scenarios and aggregate CPI categories.
NOTES: Stresses need for integrated, tailored adaptation packages and policies that consider inequality. Highlights the role of livestock as coping assets and the limitations of self-reliance in the face of compound shocks.

TITLE: Smallholder farmersâ€™ perceptions of and adaptations to climate change in the Nigerian savanna
AUTHORS: Justice Akpene Tambo and Tahirou Abdoulaye
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Nigerian savanna (specifically Borno State: Sudan savanna and Northern Guinea savanna)
METHODS: Primary household survey with 200 smallholder farmers across two agro-ecological zones (AEZs). Complemented by focus group discussions (FGDs). Data analyzed using descriptive statistics and Mannâ€“Whitney U tests to assess differences in adaptation practices between AEZs. The study investigates climate perceptions, adaptation behaviors, and barriers to adaptation.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate change is the central theme of the article. Adaptation is discussed extensively, with emphasis on drought response, variable rainfall, and the need for planned interventions. Mitigation is not mentioned.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
92% of farmers perceive increasing temperature; 84% perceive reduced and erratic rainfall.

Adaptation practices include early-maturing crops, mixed cropping, planting trees, and shifting planting dates.

Adaptation capacity is limited by lack of credit, information, and irrigation infrastructure.

Only ~15% of respondents reported no adaptation; better than most regions in Africa due to donor projects.

Irrigation and agricultural insurance are underutilized but potentially transformative.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Lack of reliable meteorological data limited trend validation

Self-reported adaptation may include habitual behaviors rather than new responses

Study limited to one Nigerian state (Borno); results not generalizable across all savanna regions
NOTES:
Acknowledges IITA and DTMA support for drought-tolerant maize adoption

Suggests index-based insurance and irrigation support as future priorities

Strong connection to farmer memory recall and context-specific responses to climate threats

TITLE: Between rainfall and food poverty: Assessing vulnerability to climate change in an agricultural economy
AUTHORS: Olawale Emmanuel Olayide, Tunrayo Alabi
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Nigeria (National-level study disaggregated by state)
METHODS: The study used geo-referenced rainfall data (1970â€“2015) and food poverty data from Nigeriaâ€™s National Bureau of Statistics to assess the relationship between rainfall variability and food poverty. It applied GIS mapping, decadal trend analysis, and statistical association tests (e.g., Chi-square) to examine vulnerability across regions and identify policy-relevant hotspots for climate adaptation.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The studyâ€™s central theme is vulnerability to climate variability, especially rainfall extremes (droughts, floods), and its implications for food systems in rain-fed agriculture.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
High rainfall variability correlates with high food poverty, especially in northern Nigeria.

Regions with low variability (e.g., FCT, Niger) also tend to have lower food poverty.

The study supports using spatial data for adaptive policy targeting.

Suggests integrating early warning systems and climate-smart agriculture to address vulnerability.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Lack of integrated socioeconomic data limits nuanced poverty interpretation

Results may not capture non-climatic drivers of food insecurity

Focuses on rainfall only; does not account for temperature or other climate variables
NOTES:
Offers a replicable method for spatial targeting of climate adaptation efforts

Highlights Nigeriaâ€™s increasing vulnerability to climatic shocks in both the north (drought) and south (floods)

Grounded in national policy relevance (SDGs 1, 2, and 13)

TITLE: Phenotypic approaches to drought in cassava: review
AUTHORS: Emmanuel Okogbenin, Tim L. Setter, Morag Ferguson, Rose Mutegi, Hernan Ceballos, Bunmi Olasanmi, Martin Fregene
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Sub-Saharan Africa, Latin America, Caribbean, and Asia â€“ with field study references from Nigeria, Tanzania, Kenya, and Colombia
METHODS: This is a methods review synthesizing over three decades of cassava drought research. It evaluates field phenotyping methods, physiological traits, experimental design (e.g., use of water table gradients), and new tools like marker-assisted selection (MAS), marker-assisted recurrent selection (MARS), and genomic selection. It proposes early-growth phase phenotyping protocols to accelerate breeding for drought tolerance.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Cassavaâ€™s resilience under drought is the core theme, with site-specific strategies for measuring tolerance. Emphasizes phenotyping under real-world stress environments like Minjibir, Kano (Nigeria).
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Cassava tolerates drought via mechanisms like early leaf shedding, deep rooting, and stem starch storage.

Modern breeding needs rapid, cost-effective phenotyping (e.g., bulking at 7 MAP, early PI, stem starch).

MARS and GS offer new ways to select for complex traits without full-cycle evaluation.

Early bulking and stem starch content are predictive of yield under stress.

Field phenotyping can be designed without irrigation by using environmental gradients (e.g., water table depth).
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Late-stage traits are hard to phenotype efficiently.

Limited data on field-ready, high-throughput phenotyping tools.

Challenges in controlling drought conditions without irrigation.

Need for more integration between physiological data and genomic selection.
NOTES:
Highlights collaboration between IITA, CIAT, and national systems.

Introduces modified early-phase phenotyping for breeding efficiency.

Suggests cassava breeding could benefit from tools used in maize and rice.

Emphasizes value of African breeding programs using IITA germplasm in drought-prone zones.

TITLE: Reviewing research priorities in weed ecology, evolution and management: a horizon scan
AUTHORS: P. Neve, J. N. Barney, Y. Buckley, R. D. Cousens, S. Graham, N. R. Jordan, A. Lawton-Rauh, M. Liebman, M. B. Mesgaran, M. Schut, et al.
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: n/a
METHODS: The authors conducted a global horizon scanning exercise involving 35 experts from weed ecology, weed science, invasion biology, and social science. Prior to a 2014 workshop in Spain, participants submitted 3â€“5 key research questions. These were compiled into 124 questions, ranked through group discussion, and synthesized into 30 top priorities grouped under seven themes. These themes were then analyzed and contextualized in a transdisciplinary commentary.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Addresses climate changeâ€™s role in weed range shifts, invasiveness, and evolution. Emphasizes adaptation needs and risk from intensifying weed pressure under changing climates.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Seven priority themes: transdisciplinary research, weed evolution, climate change, invasiveness, agroecology, adoption of IWM, and future of weed science.

Major barriers to IWM adoption include reliance on simple technologies and lack of socioeconomic engagement.

Calls for a systems approach integrating public-private partnerships, ecological resilience, and broader agroecosystem thinking.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Horizon scan reflects perspectives from a specific academic network.

Rankings are consensus-based and not statistically validated.

Some questions could not be cleanly categorized.
NOTES:
Recommends repositioning weed research within broader ecological and food systems debates.

Emphasizes the importance of multifunctionality in weed research, including both positive and negative ecosystem roles.

A rare example of interdisciplinary agenda-setting within agricultural science.

TITLE: Structural approaches to modeling the impact of climate change and adaptation technologies on crop yields and food security
AUTHORS: Shahnila Islam, Nicola Cenacchi, Timothy B. Sulser, Sika Gbegbelegbe, Guy Hareau, Ulrich Kleinwechter, Daniel Mason-D'Croz, Swamikannu Nedumaran, Richard Robertson, Sherman Robinson, Keith Wiebe
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Global (with regional modeling examples in Sub-Saharan Africa, South Asia, Latin America, and others)
METHODS: This paper reviews the â€œstructural approachâ€ that integrates crop simulation, physiological, and economic modeling to assess the impact of climate change and adaptation technologies. A case study within the paper applies this framework to analyze drought- and heat-tolerant crop varieties using DSSAT and the IMPACT economic model. It evaluates exogenous (biophysical) and endogenous (market-adjusted) yield outcomes for multiple regions and crops.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate change is central. The paper highlights the uncertainty and complexity of yield responses under RCP 8.5 and emphasizes the importance of stress-resilient technologies for future food security.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Structural modeling integrates climate, crop, and economic models

Drought- and heat-tolerant varieties can offset some negative yield impacts under climate change

Market feedback reduces the full yield benefit of technologies, but still results in positive food security outcomes

Adoption modeling varies by region, crop, and technology suitability

Prices fall with increased supply, benefiting poor consumers
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Food access and utilization dimensions of food security not included

Results vary depending on adoption rates, climate severity, and crop model calibration

Costs of technology development/adoption not modeled
NOTES:
Conducted under the CGIAR Global Futures and Strategic Foresight (GFSF) program

Supports use of ex-ante modeling for climate-resilient agricultural investments

TITLE: Smallholder farmers in eastern Africa and climate change: a review of risks and adaptation options with implications for future adaptation programmes
AUTHORS: Sika Gbegbelegbe, Jared Serem, Clare Stirling, Florence Kyazze, Maren Radeny, Michael Misiko, Songporne Tongruksawattana, Lydia Nafula, Mercy Gakii, Kai Sonder
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Eastern Africa (Kenya, Ethiopia, Uganda, Tanzania)
METHODS: The study uses a realist literature review combined with two case studies in Kenya to identify climate risks, smallholder adaptation strategies, and institutional barriers. It includes 36 empirical studies, national climate reports, and CGIAR/CCAFS site-level baseline surveys, synthesizing adaptation responses by AEZ, proximity to cities, and institutional support.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs

Gender equality, youth, and social inclusion
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The paper is focused entirely on climate change impacts and adaptation. It maps regional risks (less rainfall, temperature rise, erratic rain) and examines social, economic, and environmental factors that shape farmer responses.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Decreased rainfall and rising temperature are the most reported risks across East Africa

Farmers use diverse strategies: crop timing changes, tree planting, conservation agriculture, selling assets

Adaptation capacity is higher near major cities

Constraints include land size, credit access, institutional support, and misperceptions of climate risks

Collaborative programs (CBOs, extension, NGOs) improve adoption of robust strategies
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Farmer climate perceptions donâ€™t always match meteorological data

Case studies may not be generalizable

Survey tools varied in depth and focus
NOTES:
Highlights importance of gender-aware programming and localized adaptation strategies

Recommends integrating indigenous knowledge with scientific forecasts for better targeting of adaptation policies

TITLE: Coffea arabica yields decline in Tanzania due to climate change: Global implications
AUTHORS: A.C.W. Craparo, P.J.A. Van Asten, P. LÃ¤derach, L.T.P. Jassogne, S.W. Grab
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Northern highlands of Tanzania (Mt. Kilimanjaro, Mt. Meru, and Tanga/Ngorongoro Crater regions)
METHODS: The study used long-term time series data (1962â€“2010) on coffee yields and climatic variables from the Tanzanian highlands. It applied ARIMA and non-linear (logistic) regression models to assess the impact of rising minimum night-time temperatures (Tmin) on C. arabica yields. The authors also compared Tmin trends with those in other major coffee-growing countries and projected future impacts under RCP 6.0 scenarios.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Focused explicitly on climate change impactsâ€”specifically minimum temperature increasesâ€”on arabica coffee yields in Tanzania and globally.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Tmin rose by 0.31Â°C per decade, correlating with yield declines of 137 Â± 16.87 kg/ha per Â°C increase.

ARIMA model forecasts yields dropping to 145 Â± 41 kg/ha by 2060 if trends continue without adaptation.

Logistic regression predicts yields may fall below 100 kg/ha under continued warming.

Rainfall variables had limited explanatory power compared to temperature trends.

The impact of shade trees may be context-dependent and not always beneficial at higher Tmin.

Similar warming trends are emerging in Ethiopia, Kenya, Colombia, Brazil, and Costa Ricaâ€”posing global risks to arabica production.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Yield data uncertainty due to smallholder production systems

Limitations of linear and ARIMA models for long-term biological projections

Lack of site-specific socio-economic data and non-climatic stressor modeling
NOTES:
The study provides the first quantitative global evidence that climate change has already reduced arabica yields.

Suggests that adaptation will require moving coffee production upslope, which may threaten biodiversity conservation areas.

TITLE: Establishing Optimal Planting Windows for Contrasting Sorghum Cultivars across Diverse Agro-Ecologies of North-Eastern Nigeria: A Modelling Approach
AUTHORS: Folorunso M. Akinseye, Hakeem A. Ajeigbe, Alpha Y. Kamara, Akinrotimi O. Omotayo, Abdullahi I. Tofa, Anthony M. Whitbread
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: North-Eastern Nigeria (Sudan Savanna, Northern Guinea Savanna, Southern Guinea Savanna)
METHODS: Calibrated and validated APSIM crop model using field experiments and multi-locational on-farm trials.
