Simulated sorghum grain yield under different planting windows (May 16â€“July 15) for five cultivars across 33 sites and 3 AEZs from 1985 to 2010. Evaluated phenology, leaf area index, and water use efficiency under climate variability and soil fertility differences.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Focuses on optimizing sowing periods and cultivar suitability as adaptation to delayed rains and terminal droughts. Simulates outcomes under long-term climate variability using location-specific weather and soil data.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Optimal planting windows varied by cultivar and agroecology. 

Early sowing (Mayâ€“June) improved yield and resilience. 

Photoperiod-sensitive varieties required tighter windows. 

Improved Deko and ICSV400 performed best across zones. 

Simulation showed significant yield gains from aligning cultivar phenology with rainfall onset.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Model does not include phosphorus stress; on-farm data varied in management intensity; yield estimates rely on downscaled climate data.
NOTES: Supports climate-smart decision-making for sorghum intensification. Combines APSIM modeling, field trials, and policy relevance. Funded through CGIAR and TAMASA initiative.

TITLE: Unveiling African rainforest composition and vulnerability to global change
AUTHORS: Maxime RÃ©jou-MÃ©chain, FrÃ©dÃ©ric Mortier, Jean-FranÃ§ois Bastin, Guillaume Cornu, Nicolas Barbier, et al.
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Central Africa â€“ primarily Cameroon, Central African Republic, Democratic Republic of the Congo, Republic of the Congo, and Gabon
METHODS: This large-scale ecological modeling study analyzes a dataset of over 6 million trees across ~185,000 forest plots in Central Africa using supervised component generalized linear regression (SCGLR). It models taxonomic and functional composition, classifies forest types, and assesses their vulnerability to climate and anthropogenic changes projected through 2085.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate change is central to the study, which uses downscaled projections under RCP 4.5 to forecast exposure, sensitivity, and adaptive capacity of forest communities, applying the IPCC framework for ecosystem vulnerability.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Forest composition varies along climate and disturbance gradients.

Vulnerability is highest at the forest margins and in much of DRC.

Human disturbances reduce phylogenetic diversity and adaptive capacity.

Functional traits (e.g., wood density, maximum diameter) correlate with vulnerability.

New floristic and functional maps support conservation planning and prioritization.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Potential underestimation of future anthropogenic pressures (e.g., road expansion not modeled)

Uncertainty in climate projections over Central Africa due to sparse meteorological data

Some forest types are poorly represented in existing protected areas
NOTES:
Data and code are publicly available for replication

Aligns with conservation policy needs in transboundary forest regions

Highlights role of human-induced disturbance in driving rapid forest composition shifts

TITLE: Preparing for, coping with and bouncing back after shocks. A nuanced resilience assessment for smallholder farms and farmers in Northern Ghana
AUTHORS: Mirja Michalscheck, Fred Kizito, Bekele H. Kotu, Franklin K. Avornyo, Carl Timler, Jeroen C. J. Groot
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Northern Ghana (Duko village, Tamale area)
METHODS: The study combined the FarmDESIGN whole-farm model with participatory consultations and interviews in Duko village to assess the vulnerability, coping strategies, and resilience of low-, medium-, and high-resource endowed smallholder farms. Four shock scenarios were tested: drought, pest infestation (fall armyworm), price shock, and labor shock. Farmer-specific responses and gendered coping strategies were also analyzed.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs

Gender equality, youth, and social inclusion
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The study is anchored in addressing climate-related shocks (drought, pests) and designing resilience strategies suited to smallholdersâ€™ realities.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Drought was the most damaging shock across all farm types

Low-resource farms had very limited recovery capacity without external support

Gendered roles significantly shaped coping strategies (e.g., shea nut processing by women)

Adoption of Africa RISING technologies enhanced resilience but with variation across farm types

Expansion of land or livestock was a common strategy, with potential ecological trade-offs

Farmer resilience was often more linked to social and financial capital than just technologies
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
FarmDESIGN is static and does not simulate long-term changes

Model results are based on assumptions from a specific year (2015)

Recovery capacity is contextual and may not be generalizable beyond Duko
NOTES:
Gender-specific strategies were especially insightful (e.g., income from shea, dawadawa, susu groups)

Social cohesion and access to micro-finance were important resilience factors

The project linked closely with Africa RISINGâ€™s technology park and on-farm trials

TITLE: Drought vulnerability of central Sahel agrosystems: a modelling-approach based on magnitudes of changes and machine learning techniques
AUTHORS: Ismaguil HanadÃ© Houmma, Loubna El Mansouri, SÃ©bastien Gadal, Elhousna Faouzi, Amadou Abdourhamane Toure, Maman Garba, Yasmina Imani, Moha El-Ayachi, Rachid Hadria
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Central Sahel (Niger, Mali, Burkina Faso)
METHODS: The study introduces the Multivariate Agricultural Drought Vulnerability Index (MADVI) using machine learning (SVM, RF, KNN, NB) to assess drought risk across the Central Sahel. It uses 28 biophysical, climatic, socioeconomic, and physiographic factors derived from satellite data (1981â€“2021) and PCA to model and map drought exposure and agrosystem vulnerability.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate change is central; the paper documents rising temperature anomalies and climate water deficits that disproportionately impact the Sahelâ€™s farming systems.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
28 vulnerability factors modeled with PCA and ML to predict agricultural drought risk

SVM and RF models had the highest prediction accuracy (ROC > 0.92)

Southwestern Niger, Mali, and Burkina Faso had the highest drought vulnerability

Socioeconomic factors (e.g., rural dependence, infrastructure) were the strongest predictors

Outputs can inform drought early warning systems and regional development planning
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
No local adaptation or irrigation data available

Vulnerability only modeled from satellite and environmental data, no household-level validation

Model assumes linear relationships in some components
NOTES:
First application of magnitude-of-change trend modeling in this context

Highlights relevance for informing early warning, planning, and climate adaptation priorities in vulnerable Sahelian states

TITLE: Climate Change Adaptation Strategies and Its Impact on Household Vulnerability to Food Insecurity: A Micro-Level Evidence from Southwest Ethiopia
AUTHORS: Andualem Begashaw, Mengistu Ketema, Abule Mehare, Mesay Yami, Shiferaw Feleke, Tahirou Abdoulaye
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Bench Maji Zone, Southwest Ethiopia
METHODS: The study used a cross-sectional quantitative research design with multistage sampling to survey 390 smallholder households across four districts. It applied a multinomial endogenous treatment effect model to assess how adoption of various climate change adaptation strategiesâ€”individually and in combinationâ€”impacts household vulnerability to food insecurity. Vulnerability was measured using the Vulnerability as Expected Poverty (VEP) approach with food consumption expenditure as a proxy.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The study explicitly assesses the effectiveness of climate change adaptation strategiesâ€”such as crop management, soil and water conservation, and livelihood diversificationâ€”in improving food security under increasingly variable and extreme weather patterns.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Combined adaptation strategies significantly reduce household vulnerability to food insecurity.

The combination of crop management and soil/water conservation had the largest effect (24% reduction).

Livelihood diversification also contributed substantially to reducing food insecurity risk.

Education, perception of climate risk, and livestock ownership were positively associated with adoption.

A significant proportion of current food-secure households remain vulnerable to future insecurity, highlighting the need for sustained adaptation efforts.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Results are based on cross-sectional rather than longitudinal data

The food insecurity metric relies on expenditure rather than other multidimensional indicators

Potential omitted variables not captured in the survey (e.g., sociocultural dynamics)
NOTES:
The study contributes to policy and NGO efforts by identifying specific strategies that most effectively reduce vulnerability.

IITAâ€™s support of this research underlines its focus on resilience and sustainable development in smallholder agriculture in Sub-Saharan Africa.

TITLE: Remotely-sensed slowing down in spatially patterned dryland ecosystems
AUTHORS: Michiel P. Veldhuis, Ricardo Martinez-Garcia, Vincent Deblauwe, Vasilis Dakos
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Sudan (Western Sector of Southern Kordofan State)
METHODS: Used 20 years of satellite-derived vegetation and climate data (EVI and SPEI) over Sudanâ€™s rainfall gradient. Combined spatial vegetation pattern classification (gaps, labyrinths, spots) with temporal indicators of critical slowing down (autocorrelation, variance, responsiveness). Validated findings against model simulations of vegetation dynamics under aridity stress.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Focused on resilience under increasing aridity. Emphasizes that vegetation responses slow as aridity increases, with transitions between spatial patterns linked to temporary system instability.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Patterns of vegetation (gaps â†’ labyrinths â†’ spots) are linked to changes in responsiveness, autocorrelation, and variance. 

These indicators do not always behave linearlyâ€”transient dynamics complicate their use as early warning tools.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Long transients may limit real-time utility of pattern-based indicators. Variability in rainfall and landscape heterogeneity were not fully captured in the model.
NOTES: Suggests spatial self-organization may be useful for resilience monitoring, but transient effects mean these systems need long-term observation and caution in interpretation.

TITLE: Managing vulnerability to drought and enhancing livelihood resilience in sub-Saharan Africa: Technological, institutional and policy options
AUTHORS: Bekele Shiferaw, Kindie Tesfaye, Menale Kassie, Tsedeke Abate, B.M. Prasanna, Abebe Menkir
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Sub-Saharan Africa (multiple regions, with special attention to East and Southern Africa)
METHODS: This article synthesizes findings from various empirical studies and project experiences (notably Drought Tolerant Maize for Africa) to assess drought risk in SSA. It presents technological, institutional, and policy strategies for managing drought risk, enhancing resilience, and supporting sustainable intensification. It reviews ex-ante and ex-post coping strategies, climate-smart technologies (e.g., DT maize), conservation agriculture, and market-based instruments such as weather index insurance.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Climate mitigation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate change is framed as a primary intensifier of drought risk, and the article emphasizes proactive drought risk management, not just emergency response. Mitigation is mentioned in the context of CA and sustainable intensification.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Drought is the most severe climate risk in SSA, with both direct and indirect impacts.

Farmers often rely on ex-ante coping strategies that reduce income potential (e.g., low input use).

DT maize and CA are key tools for drought adaptation and mitigation.

Climate information, insurance, and market access must be part of a broader strategy.

Institutional innovations and integrated policies are more effective than isolated responses.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Weak uptake of conservation agriculture due to long adoption timelines

Challenges scaling weather index insurance due to data and awareness constraints

Need for more site-specific policy and institutional adaptation strategies
NOTES:
DT maize projected to lift 4 million people out of poverty

Authors advocate moving from crisis response to anticipatory drought risk management

Emphasizes integrated approaches across institutions, geographies, and farming systems

TITLE: Are digital services the right solution for empowering smallholder farmers? A perspective enlightened by COVID-19 experiences to inform smart IPM
AUTHORS: Haruna Sekabira, Ghislain T. Tepa-Yotto, Arnaud R. M. Ahouandjinou, Karl H. Thunes, Barry Pittendrigh, Yusuf Kaweesa, Manuele TamÃ²
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Sub-Saharan Africa, with examples from Uganda, Benin, Nigeria, Ghana, South Africa, Tanzania, and Niger
METHODS: The article is a conceptual and policy review based on secondary data and case studies from SSA. It synthesizes experiences during the COVID-19 pandemic to explore how digital information and communication technologies (ICTs) enhanced the resilience of smallholder farmers and informed smart Integrated Pest Management (IPM) systems. The review also highlights gender and inclusivity concerns in digital adoption.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs

Gender equality, youth, and social inclusion
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate change is described as a driver of pest pressure and farming system instability. The paper frames digital tools as critical for building resilience but does not claim direct mitigation impacts.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
COVID-19 revealed vulnerabilities in food systems, which digital tools helped mitigate.

Smart-IPM, including early pest detection, is more accessible through ICTs.

Gender-sensitive ICT innovations are needed to close access gaps.

Mobile money and digital markets reduced livelihood losses and food insecurity.

Future systems must integrate inclusive, scalable digital strategies for pest and market management.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
High costs and limited access to ICTs for many smallholders

Gender and literacy gaps limit digital adoption

Most innovations are not yet scaled or fully operational for farmers
NOTES:
Recommends scaling up tools like the FIA app for early pest detection

Emphasizes integration of digital innovations with traditional/indigenous knowledge

Links to projects funded by the Royal Norwegian Embassy, USAID, and AICCRA (CGIAR)
Abstract: This is a meta-analysis and scoping review of 43 studies and 11 reports on Climate-Smart (CS) One-Health innovations. The review identified determinants, challenges, and impacts related to the deployment of CS One-Health innovations, particularly in Ghana and Benin. Community participation, land tenure, political will, and age are critical adoption factors. Practices like composting, IPM, cover cropping, rotational grazing, and traditional ecological knowledge are promoted. Major barriers include cultural practices, lack of diagnostic tools, limited ICT access, and top-down implementation. Multi-disciplinary collaboration is essential for sustainable deployment.
Keywords: Climate-Smart agriculture, One-Health, Ghana, Benin, socio-economic determinants, innovation adoption, meta-analysis

TITLE: Diversity within village institutions and its implication for resilience in the context of climate change in Cameroon
AUTHORS: H. Carolyn Peach Brown, Denis J. Sonwa
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Cameroon (Centre, East, and Southwest provinces in the humid forest zone)
METHODS: The study employed quantitative household surveys and key informant interviews in 13 villages across three provinces of Cameroon. Data from 232 respondents were analyzed to assess the structure, membership diversity, inter-group collaboration, and potential for social learning within local institutions, with a focus on implications for climate change adaptation. Statistical analysis included chi-square tests and linear regression.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity 

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs

Gender equality, youth, and social inclusion
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The study explores the role of internal village institutions in building adaptive capacity and resilience to climate change, especially through social capital and learning mechanisms.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Nearly all villagers belong to at least one local institution, with high diversity in gender, education, and occupation.

These institutions are not yet directly addressing climate change but serve as platforms for future adaptation strategies.

Gendered patterns of participation reveal womenâ€™s strong roles in NTFP collection and savings groups, while men dominate marketing and forest exploitation groups.

Social capital and inter-group collaboration vary significantly across regions and influence adaptation potential.

Institutional diversity could enable more effective knowledge-sharing and resilience if supported through intentional capacity building.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
No longitudinal data to track changes in institutions or adaptation behavior

Limited integration between village institutions and external climate adaptation programs

Gender and ethnic homogeneity in some villages may limit innovation
NOTES:
The paper calls for a gender-sensitive, long-term process of facilitated learning and collaboration between local institutions and external actors.

Recognizes IITAâ€™s role via CoFCCA in linking rural institutions with regional adaptation knowledge.

TITLE: Mediation and moderation roles of resilience capacity in the shockâ€“food-security nexus in northern Ghana
AUTHORS: Isaac Gershon K. Ansah, Bekele Hundie Kotu, Julius Manda, Francis Muthoni, Carlo Azzarri
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Northern Ghana
METHODS: Used two waves of household survey data (2014 and 2020) combined with satellite-based weather data to assess food security effects of drought and heat shocks. Applied correlated random effects (CRE), IV regression, and structural equation modeling to evaluate whether resilience capacity moderates or mediates shock effects. Resilience measured via RIMA indicators: access to basic services, assets, adaptive capacity, and social safety nets.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Resilience capacity mediates and moderates the negative impacts of drought and heat stress on food security. Shows how climate shocks undermine calorie intake, diet diversity, and food expenditure, but effects are reduced through adaptive capacity and safety nets.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Heat stress and drought reduce household calorie consumption and food diversity, but resilience capacity mitigates these effects. 

Mediation effects were stronger than moderation. 

Structural equation modeling revealed that 74% (drought) and 537% (heat) of food security impacts were mediated via resilience. 

Promotes investments in sustainable intensification and social protection to build resilience.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: CRE models rely on time-invariant assumptions; resilience indicators exclude psychosocial factors; mediation effects may vary across regions.
NOTES: Supports Africa RISING and CGIAR resilience-building strategies. Uses robust econometrics to distinguish indirect vs. interaction effects.

TITLE: Assessing Climate Smart Agriculture and Its Determinants of Practice in Ghana: A Case of the Cocoa Production System
AUTHORS: Felix Akrofi-Atitianti, Chinwe Ifejika Speranza, Louis Bockel, Richard Asare
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Ghana (Juabeso and Atwima Mponua districts)
METHODS: Data collected using household surveys (n=80), focus group discussions, and expert interviews. Compared CSA/Agroecology and conventional cocoa practices using farm budget analysis, FAO EX-ACT tool for carbon balance, and logistic regression to determine factors influencing CSA adoption.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Climate mitigation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: CSA/Agroecology practices preserved forest cover, sequestered more carbon, and avoided deforestation relative to conventional practices. EX-ACT analysis estimated large emissions reductions and greater resilience under CSA systems.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
CSA cocoa systems yielded 29% more income per hectare and 37% more cocoa yield than conventional systems. 

Key drivers of CSA adoption included secure tenure, access to extension, and migrant status. 

CSA enhanced farmer resilience through social capital, collective action, and learning. 

Carbon sequestration and GHG mitigation were higher in CSA systems due to afforestation and avoided deforestation.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Small sample size; limited generalizability beyond districts studied; some reliance on self-reported farm data.
NOTES: Supports national REDD+ strategies; provides policy recommendations for farmer support systems and tenure security to enable CSA adoption.

TITLE: Vulnerability of Nigerian maize traders to a confluence of climate, violence, disease and cost shocks
AUTHORS: Carolina M. Vargas, Lenis Saweda O. Liverpool-Tasie, Thomas Reardon
JOURNAL: Journal of Agribusiness in Developing and Emerging Economies
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Nigeria (North and South regions, with market sampling in Plateau, Kaduna, Kano, Katsina, and Oyo states)
METHODS: Used survey data from 1,100 maize traders (2021) and previous data from 2017; employed probit models to analyze the likelihood and severity of experiencing shocks (climate, violence, COVID-19, price, spoilage) based on trader characteristics (gender, region, firm size, etc.). Included secondary data from ACLED (conflict) and CHIRPS (climate).
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs

Gender equality, youth, and social inclusion
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate shocks (e.g., floods, road washouts) are core variables. The paper identifies regional vulnerability patterns and their implications for supply chain disruption and adaptation.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Shocks often occur in tandem, especially price with climate and violence. 

Women are more vulnerable to violence shocks. 

Smaller traders are more vulnerable to price spikes. 

Northern traders face more severe impacts. 

Farming maize buffers price shocks but increases violence exposure.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Correlational not causal; limited ability to quantify income losses; self-reported shock severity; challenges in capturing adaptation strategies.
NOTES: Recommends better infrastructure (roads, rail), finance and insurance tools, and conflict mitigation strategies to protect trader resilience.

TITLE: Strategizing research and development investments in climate change adaptation for root, tuber and banana crops in the African Great Lakes Region: A spatial prioritisation and targeting framework
AUTHORS: Elke Vandamme, Rhys Manners, Julius Adewopo, Graham Thiele, Michael Friedmann, Philip Thornton
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: African Great Lakes Region â€” Burundi, DRC, Kenya, Malawi, Rwanda, Tanzania, Uganda
METHODS: The study developed a spatial prioritisation and targeting framework using cluster analysis to identify socio-agroecological homologues (SAHs) based on climate hazards, exposure, and vulnerability. It used biophysical and socio-economic indicators (e.g., HDI, WGI, crop suitability) to guide R&D investment for RT&B crops. Scenario analysis was used to evaluate climate-smart interventions like drought-tolerant varieties and intercropping.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The paper focuses on climate-related risk and the potential for adaptive interventions (e.g., drought-tolerant varieties, mulching, intercropping) to offset climate stress. Mitigation is not addressed.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Crop suitability varies across the GLR due to climate and socioeconomic factors. 

The proposed SAH framework helps identify regions and crops needing priority investments. 

Scenario modeling shows that targeted technologies can improve crop suitability under future climates.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Socioeconomic data used were contemporary rather than projected. The suitability model assumes ideal planting schedules, which may not reflect real farmer practices.
NOTES: The framework is proposed as a tool for CGIAR and national programs to guide breeding, agronomy, and policy strategies for long-term climate resilience planning.

TITLE: Limited effects of shade on physiological performances of cocoa (Theobroma cacao L.) under elevated temperature
AUTHORS: Eric Opoku Mensah, Richard Asare, Philippe Vaast, Christiana A. Amoatey, Bo Markussen, Kwadwo Owusu, Bismark Kwesi Asitoakor, Anders RÃ¦bild
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Ghana
METHODS: A controlled field experiment assessed the physiological responses of cocoa seedlings under combined shade and heat stress. Using a randomized block design, seedlings were exposed to infra-red heating and/or 60% shading nets. The study evaluated parameters including photosynthesis, chlorophyll fluorescence, chlorophyll content, stomatal density, and biomass. Effects of elevated temperature and shade were modeled using linear mixed effects analysis.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The study is grounded in climate change as a stressor, assessing temperature thresholds and physiological limits of cocoa.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Elevated temperatures (5â€“7Â°C above ambient) significantly reduced photosynthesis, chlorophyll, and biomass

Shade moderately improved physiological parameters but did not offset heat stress impacts

Optimum temperature for photosynthesis was ~31â€“33Â°C

Shade improved chlorophyll content, leaf area, and water-use efficiency, but these benefits were limited under high heat

Suggests need for shade + other strategies to protect cocoa from extreme temperatures
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Experiment conducted only on seedlings, not mature plants

Artificial heating may not fully replicate field heat stress dynamics

No competition from shade trees was simulated
NOTES:
Funded by DANIDA under the CLIMCOCOA project

Suggests further research in mature agroforestry settings with realistic shade-tree dynamics

Contributes to empirical data gaps on cocoa heat tolerance

TITLE: Identification of Drought, Heat, and Combined Drought and Heat Tolerant Donors in Maize
AUTHORS: Jill E. Cairns, Jose Crossa, P.H. Zaidi, Pichet Grudloyma, Ciro Sanchez, Jose Luis Araus, Suriphat Thaitad, Dan Makumbi, Cosmos Magorokosho, Marianne BÃ¤nziger, Abebe Menkir, Sarah Hearne, Gary N. Atlin
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Eastern and Southern Africa (Kenya, Zimbabwe), Mexico, India, Thailand
METHODS: The study evaluated 300 maize inbred lines from CIMMYT and IITA breeding programs in testcrosses to CML539 across five countries under four stress regimes: drought, heat, combined drought and heat, and optimal conditions. Grain yield, flowering, and plant height were assessed. Broad-sense heritability and genotype-by-environment interactions were analyzed using linear mixed models to identify donors tolerant to combined stresses.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate change is central. The study directly responds to IPCC projections of increasing drought and heat stress in Southern Africa and evaluates adaptation traits accordingly.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Maize tolerance to combined drought and heat is genetically distinct from tolerance to each stress alone.

Existing drought-tolerant parents may fail under heat stress.

Identified promising donor lines (e.g., La Posta Seq C7-F64-2-6-2-2 and DTPYC9-F46-1-2-1-2) with high performance under both stressors.

Recommends incorporating combined-stress screening in breeding programs.

Shows that performance under well-watered conditions poorly predicts performance under stress.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Large genotype Ã— trial and residual variances reduce heritability under stress.

Managed stress screening must replicate across trials to improve precision.

More donors are needed as gains in drought tolerance have plateaued.
NOTES:
Study supported by several CGIAR programs (CCAFS, MAIZE) and USAID/Gates Foundation.

Data used to guide donor incorporation into breeding pipelines for SSA.

TITLE: Linking agricultural adaptation strategies, food security and vulnerability: evidence from West Africa
AUTHORS: Sabine Douxchamps, Mark T. Van Wijk, Silvia Silvestri, Abdoulaye S. Moussa, Carlos Quiros, NdeÌ€ye Yacine B. Ndour, Saaka Buah, LÃ©opold SomÃ©, Mario Herrero, Patricia Kristjanson, Mathieu Ouedraogo, Philip K. Thornton, Piet Van Asten, Robert ZougmorÃ©, Mariana C. Rufino
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Burkina Faso, Ghana, Senegal (West Africa)
METHODS: The paper examines how different agricultural adaptation strategies (e.g., crop diversity, small ruminants, SWC, vegetables, fertilizers, improved varieties) relate to food security outcomes in different smallholder household types. It uses survey data from the CCAFS benchmark sites, regression modeling, and typology classification to assess impacts on land productivity, income, and food self-sufficiency.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: This is a climate adaptation study, using CCAFS sites in West Africa to evaluate how household types adopt and benefit from different adaptation practices.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Food security is driven by land productivity, income, and market orientation

Adaptation strategies like soil and water conservation, small ruminants, and crop diversity improve land productivityâ€”especially in more market-oriented households

Households fall into 4 types (subsistence, diversified, extensive, intensified), with distinct strategies and vulnerabilities

Not all households achieve food security through current adaptation strategiesâ€”suggesting the need for transformational change in some cases

Off-farm income plays a key role in stabilizing household cash flows and buffering seasonal vulnerabilities
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Household-level analysis excludes community-level adaptations

Limited to energy-based food security indicators (not nutrition or health)

Adaptation practices may have delayed or context-specific effects
NOTES:
Strong relevance to CCAFS and CGIAR climate-smart agriculture frameworks

Suggests targeting of strategies based on household type rather than blanket promotion

TITLE: Sustainable intensification: What is its role in climate smart agriculture?
AUTHORS: Bruce M. Campbell, Philip Thornton, Robert ZougmoreÌ, Piet van Asten, Leslie Lipper
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Global (with examples from East Africa and the Sahel)
METHODS: This review paper synthesizes the complementary roles of Sustainable Intensification (SI) and Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) using conceptual frameworks and illustrative case studies. It provides a high-level overview of the synergies between CSA objectives (productivity, adaptation, mitigation) and SI practices across different agricultural systems, with examples including coffee-banana intercropping, livestock feed strategies, and agroforestry.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Climate mitigation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The paper is explicitly focused on integrating sustainable intensification practices into climate change adaptation and mitigation strategies in agriculture.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
SI and CSA are deeply complementary: CSA helps farmers adapt and builds institutions, while SI boosts productivity and mitigation through efficiency.

Examples include coffeeâ€“banana intercropping in Uganda (IITA-led), livestock feed intensification using Leucaena, and stone bunds and zaÃ¯ pits in the Sahel.

Emphasizes the importance of risk management, ecosystem services, and information systems.

Argues for policies that link adaptation, mitigation, and food system transformation to reduce environmental trade-offs.

SI and CSA are crucial but need to be supported by broader food system reforms, including reducing food waste and promoting better diets.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
CSA and SI often require high upfront costs, which can be barriers to uptake among smallholders.

Risk of intensification leading to land expansion if not accompanied by safeguards

Effectiveness depends on local adaptation and governance systems
NOTES:
The paper features an IITA-led case study on banana-coffee intercropping in East Africa, showing increased income and reduced climate risk.

Strong emphasis on the need for climate justice and support to smallholders who contribute least to climate change but are most affected.

TITLE: Ecological drivers of pteridophyte diversity and distribution in Togo (West Africa)
AUTHORS: Komla Elikplim Abotsi, Ruksan Bose, Kossi Adjossou, Vincent Deblauwe, Germinal Rouhan, Kossi Novinyo Segla, Komina Honam Atsri, Kouami Kokou
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Togo, West Africa
METHODS: Nationwide survey of pteridophyte diversity across 130 plots in 5 ecological zones in Togo. Multivariate analysis, Canonical Correspondence Analysis (CCA), and indicator species analysis were used to assess influences of climate, land cover, and human disturbance on species richness and composition.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Highlights strong climate influence on pteridophyte distribution. Emphasizes precipitation, elevation, and humidity as key drivers of diversity and resilience, and links species presence to climatic water deficit and cloud cover.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Most pteridophyte species are concentrated in submontane humid forests in zone 4 of Togo. 

Climate (especially humidity), land cover, and disturbance significantly affect distribution. 

Agroforests retain ~30% of rainforest diversity. Indicator species vary by land cover and disturbance level.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Plot-based study may not capture full national diversity; ecological indicators based on abundance-dominance may vary across time.
NOTES: Data collected over 5 years; emphasizes need for conserving submontane forests and promoting less destructive agroforestry practices.

TITLE: Farmers, food and climate change: ensuring community-based adaptation is mainstreamed into agricultural programmes
AUTHORS: Helena Wright, Sonja Vermeulen, Gernot Laganda, Max Olupot, Edidah Ampaire, M.L. Jat
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Bangladesh, Mozambique, Uganda, and India
METHODS: Synthesizes insights from four case studies to evaluate the opportunities and barriers for mainstreaming community-based adaptation (CBA) into agricultural programs. Data sources include literature reviews, project evaluations, and participatory research, with an emphasis on extension services and farmer organizations.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs

Gender equality, youth, and social inclusion
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Strong focus on adaptation to climate risks through decentralized governance, participatory development, and policy integration. Climate change is treated as a key cross-sectoral challenge.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Mainstreaming CBA requires enabling environments and institutional innovation. 

Local adaptation knowledge is often not integrated into policy. 

Success depends on bridging top-down and bottom-up processes via extension services, communication, and inclusive governance.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Findings are based on selected case studies; generalizability may be limited. Many adaptation successes remain anecdotal. Policy uptake is often constrained by institutional fragmentation and limited implementation capacity.
NOTES: CBA can mobilize youth and women, as in India, and influence policy reforms. The article argues for procedural justice and long-term institutional embedding of adaptation knowledge rather than one-off projects.

TITLE: Institutional Perspectives of Climate-Smart Agriculture: A Systematic Literature Review
AUTHORS: Edmond Totin, Alcade C. Segnon, Marc Schut, Hippolyte Affognon, Robert B. ZougmorÃ©, Todd Rosenstock, Philip K. Thornton
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Global (systematic review with emphasis on Sub-Saharan Africa, India, Germany, and other regional clusters)
METHODS: This systematic review analyzed 137 peer-reviewed publications from 1991 to 2017 using institutional analysis frameworks. It examined how the CSA pillars (productivity, adaptation, mitigation) are addressed and the extent to which institutional dimensions (e.g., policy, market structure, social norms) are integrated. Thematic and content analysis was conducted using R software and Adobe Acrobat keyword searches based on predefined inclusion/exclusion criteria.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Climate mitigation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs

Gender equality, youth, and social inclusion
NOTES ON CLIMATE: CSA is framed around productivity, adaptation, and mitigation; adaptation and productivity dominate in lower-income countries, mitigation in higher-income contexts.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
CSA adoption is constrained by weak attention to institutional contexts. 

Market incentives, knowledge infrastructure, and multi-stakeholder partnerships are key to scaling CSA.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: CSA literature lacks focus on physical infrastructure, socio-political and historical contexts, and private sector engagement.
NOTES: Significant gap between CSAâ€™s conceptual framework and its operationalization; stresses the need for institutional innovation to complement technical CSA solutions.

TITLE: A framework for priority-setting in climate-smart agriculture research
AUTHORS: Philip K. Thornton, Anthony Whitbread, Tobias Baedeker, Jill Cairns, Lieven Claessens, Walter Baethgen, Christian Bunn, Michael Friedmann, Ken E. Giller, Mario Herrero, Mark Howden, Kevin Kilcline, Vinay Nangia, Julian Ramirez-Villegas, Shalander Kumar, Paul C. West, Brian Keating
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Global, with an emphasis on Sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia
METHODS: The paper develops a conceptual framework for setting research priorities in climate-smart agriculture (CSA). It synthesizes literature, case studies, and expert consultations from a CGIAR-led workshop to define six key elements for CSA priority setting: entry points, scale, research questions, impact estimates for productivity/adaptation/mitigation, social and environmental effects, and likelihood of impact. It reviews tools including cost-benefit analysis, simulation modeling, optimization, and participatory methods.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Climate mitigation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs

Gender equality, youth, and social inclusion
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate change is central to the article. The framework evaluates both adaptation and mitigation as core CSA pillars, explicitly discussing GHG emission reductions and carbon sequestration.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
CSA prioritization is complex due to trade-offs between adaptation, mitigation, and productivity.

A conceptual six-element framework is proposed to structure CSA research planning.

Impact metrics must consider spatial/temporal scale, stakeholder goals, and system variability.

The framework includes tools like simulation modeling, economic surplus, and participatory ranking.

Equity and social inclusion are identified as important dimensions for future CSA research.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Complexity and data-intensity of tools (e.g., simulation models)

Lack of consensus on CSA definitions

Trade-offs and equity issues remain under-researched
NOTES:
Developed under CGIAR-CCAFS with wide institutional authorship (World Bank, IITA, CIAT, CIMMYT, ILRI)

Focused on research-for-development and donor alignment in CSA inv

TITLE: Horizon scanning to assess the bioclimatic potential for the alien species Spodoptera eridania and its parasitoids after pest detection in West and Central Africa
AUTHORS: Ghislain T. Tepa-Yotto, GÃ©rard N. Gouwakinnou, Johannes R. Fagbohoun, Manuele TamÃ², May-Guri SÃ¦thre
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: West and Central Africa, with global modeling (especially Sub-Saharan Africa, Asia, Latin America)
METHODS: Used species distribution modeling (Maxent) to assess the current and future habitat suitability of Spodoptera eridania (southern armyworm) and four of its co-evolved parasitoids. Climate data from WorldClim was combined with pest and parasitoid occurrence records to generate global suitability maps under current climate and two future climate scenarios (RCP6.0 and RCP8.5). Model accuracy was evaluated with AUC and TSS.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The paper extensively integrates climate scenarios (RCP6.0 and RCP8.5) but focuses on adaptation through pest forecasting and biological controlâ€”not mitigation.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
S. eridania is expanding in Africa and could severely affect key crops.

Parasitoids like Telenomus remus and Cotesia marginiventris show promise for biocontrol.

Bioclimatic suitability for parasitoids generally overlaps with that of the pest.

Climate change will shift suitability zones, requiring adaptive pest control planning.

Biological control is promoted as a durable, climate-smart pest management approach.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Models do not account for intraguild competition or biotic interactions

Results rely on data availability and quality (e.g., underreporting)

Lack of field validation for parasitoid performance in African contexts
NOTES:
Acknowledges support from CGIAR-CCAFS and World Bank AICCRA project

Builds on previous IITA modeling work on Spodoptera frugiperda

Calls for complementary pest control methods alongside biocontrol

TITLE: Implementation Outline of Climate-Smart One Health
AUTHORS:
Ghislain T. Tepa-Yotto, Henri E. Z. Tonnang,  Stephen Yeboah,  Michael Yao Osae,
Awudu Amadu Gariba, Mustapha Dalaa, Faustina Obeng Adomaa, Osman Tahidu Damba,
Reginald Kyere, FidÃ¨le T. Moutouama, Cyriaque Agboton, Jeannette K. Winsou, Manuele TamÃ², Robert Zougmore
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Global, with emphasis on regions vulnerable to zoonotic disease and climate risk (e.g., Sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia)
METHODS: This is a policy-oriented conceptual document that outlines an implementation framework for an integrated One Health approach to climate change adaptation and mitigation. It synthesizes existing frameworks and policies without primary data collection.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Climate mitigation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The report explicitly centers climate change as both a driver of zoonotic disease and a key threat to food and health systems, outlining mitigation and adaptation strategies within a One Health approach.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
A Climate-Smart One Health approach can address multiple SDGs.

Systems-level coordination across human, animal, and environmental health is critical.

Investments are needed in integrated data, early warning systems, and transdisciplinary capacity building.

Strong emphasis on co-benefits: reduced GHG emissions, improved ecosystem services, and food security.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Challenges to implementation include fragmented governance, weak cross-sector data integration, and limited local capacity.
NOTES:
Developed by a coalition of experts including WHO, FAO, UNEP, and CGIAR.

Recommends pilot projects and scaling through regional coordination platforms.

Intended as an actionable strategy, not a research paper.

TITLE: Global Habitat Suitability of Spodoptera frugiperda (JE Smith) (Lepidoptera, Noctuidae): Key Parasitoids Considered for Its Biological Control
AUTHORS: Ghislain T. Tepa-Yotto, Henri E. Z. Tonnang, Georg Goergen, Sevgan Subramanian, Emily Kimathi, Elfatih M. Abdel-Rahman, Daniel FlÃ¸, Karl H. Thunes, Komi K. M. Fiaboe, Saliou Niassy, Anani Bruce, Samira A. Mohamed, Manuele TamÃ², Sunday Ekesi, May-Guri SÃ¦thre
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Global, with a special focus on Sub-Saharan Africa, Asia, and Latin America under current and future (RCP8.5) climate conditions
METHODS: The study used species distribution models (SDMs) to predict the global habitat suitability of Spodoptera frugiperda (fall armyworm) and six of its key parasitoids (biocontrol agents). It employed Maxent modeling based on georeferenced presence records and climatic/environmental predictor variables under current and future climate scenarios (RCP8.5 for 2050). The goal was to inform strategic deployment of biological control for managing fall armyworm globally.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The study incorporates climate change (RCP8.5) directly in pest and biocontrol habitat modeling. It focuses on climate adaptation but does not reference mitigation or emissions.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Parasitoids have high habitat overlap with fall armyworm in Africa and parts of Asia and Latin America.

Habitat suitability for both pests and parasitoids may expand or shift under climate change.

Deployment of multiple parasitoids may improve biocontrol robustness.

SDMs offer a cost-effective tool for biocontrol planning across regions.

Fall armyworm poses increasing threat under future climates without effective control.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Lack of native range data for some parasitoids

No field validation of biocontrol efficacy

Habitat suitability does not guarantee field successâ€”ecological interactions are complex
NOTES:
Supported by CGIAR CCAFS and FAW-IPM project

Funded by EU, Royal Norwegian Embassy, and GIZ

IITAâ€™s Biorisk Management Facility (BIMAF) acknowledged as key institutional supportâ€‹

TITLE: How does IPM 3.0 look like (and why do we need it in Africa)?
AUTHORS: Manuele TamÃ², Isabelle Glitho, Ghislain Tepa-Yotto, Rangaswamy Muniappan
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Sub-Saharan Africa (general, with references to Benin, Kenya, Niger, Nigeria, etc.)
METHODS: This is a conceptual review that proposes the next evolution in Integrated Pest Management (IPM 3.0), anchored in three pillars: (1) farmer empowerment through digital decision-making tools, (2) science-based ecological control strategies, and (3) integration of genomics, biopesticides, and habitat management. The article draws on case studies from across Africa to illustrate innovations and policy needs for transitioning away from synthetic pesticide dependency.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs

Gender equality, youth, and social inclusion
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate change is a central context throughout, with IPM 3.0 framed as a tool for resilience and GHG mitigation. Pushâ€“pull, conservation agriculture, and biopesticide use are highlighted as low-emission alternatives.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Farmers need digital, accessible pest ID tools to make informed decisions.

Biopesticides and nature-based pest control can reduce pesticide treadmill.

Pushâ€“pull has evolved to become climate-smart and more drought-resilient.

CRISPR and AI are part of a new generation of ecological pest management.

Policy harmonization and investment in regional diagnostics are essential.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Past IPM approaches failed to reach scale due to complexity and lack of institutional support

Biopesticide uptake remains low due to poor market development and regulation gaps

Gender and literacy barriers still restrict farmer access to digital tools
NOTES:
Promotes a regional early warning system for invasive pests

Funded by USAID, AICCRA/World Bank, and NORAD

Clear linkages to IITA-led innovations like the Farmer Interface Application (FIA)

TITLE: Exploring the floristic diversity of tropical Africa
AUTHORS: Marc S.M. Sosef, Gilles Dauby, Anne Blach-Overgaard, Xander van der Burgt, LuÃ­s Catarino, and 25 others
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Tropical Africa (sub-Saharan Africa excluding southern Africa and Madagascar)
METHODS: This large-scale observational study analyzes the RAINBIO databaseâ€”a curated compilation of over 600,000 herbarium records covering 22,577 vascular plant species in tropical Africa. The study uses species richness estimates, floristic turnover metrics, sampling completeness, and growth form classifications to map biodiversity patterns, identify exploration gaps, and suggest priority sampling areas. Analyses were conducted in R using species richness estimators (Chao1, Nielsen), turnover indices (Î²sim), and geospatial modeling.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The paper emphasizes that up to 90% of species may lose part or all of their suitable climate zones by 2085, underscoring the urgency of conservation under climate change. However, mitigation via carbon or emissions is not addressed.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
RAINBIO contains 609,776 georeferenced herbarium records covering 22,577 species.

Forests in tropical Africa host ~15,387 species, with ~30% endemism in Central Africa.

Sampling remains incomplete; only 21 regions meet high-density standards.

High floristic turnover zones include Cameroon, Gabon, Nimba Mountains, and Eastern Arc.

Strong call for improved digitization and sampling to meet conservation goals.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Many areas lack digitized herbarium data (e.g., Angola, DRC, Republic of Congo)

Data are biased toward regions with higher historical collecting intensity

Some regions remain botanically unexplored despite high conservation importance
NOTES:
Advocates identifying â€œPriority Sampling Unitsâ€ (PSUs) based on high turnover and low sampling

Used to support Objective 1 of the Global Strategy for Plant Conservation 2011â€“2020

Recommends continued support for digitization of African herbaria and database integration

TITLE: Food sovereignty in sub-Saharan Africa: Reality, relevance, and practicality
AUTHORS: Grace Mudombi-Rusinamhodzi and Leonard Rusinamhodzi
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Sub-Saharan Africa (general)
METHODS: This perspective article critically analyzes the food sovereignty discourse within the socio-ecological realities of sub-Saharan Africa. It reviews agroecological practices, organic and ecological intensification, gender roles, soil fertility, land degradation, yield gaps, and marketing barriers, drawing on published literature and policy experiences to assess practical relevance.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs

Gender equality, youth, and social inclusion
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The article frames climate variability as a major constraint in rainfed farming systems and supports ecological intensification as a climate-resilient approach but does not frame any practice as mitigation.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Agroecology alone is insufficient in SSA without integrated inputs and productivity investments.

Food sovereignty is more feasible in high-income settings with supportive markets.

Women face labor-intensive burdens in traditional food systems.

Access to certification, markets, and fair pricing are critical barriers to food system equity.

Food sovereignty must be adapted to local ecological and socio-political contexts.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Food sovereignty is rarely practical in low-yield systems with labor/resource constraints

Organic-only production cannot meet yield requirements in SSA

Certification and premium markets are inaccessible for most smallholders
NOTES:
Advocates integrated soil fertility management (ISFM) and hybrid production models

Suggests that food sovereignty must go beyond activism toward operational policies in SSA

IITA affiliation brings institutional weight to practical policy recommendations

TITLE: Integrating a crop model with a greenhouse gas calculator to identify low carbon agricultural intensification options for smallholder farmers in rural South Africa
AUTHORS: Farirai Rusere, Lynn V. Dicks, Siyabusa Mkuhlani, Olivier Crespo
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: South Africa â€“ specifically Ha Lambani (Limpopo) and Amathole (Eastern Cape)
METHODS: The study integrates two modelsâ€”DSSAT for crop yield simulations and the Cool Farm Tool (CFT) for greenhouse gas calculationsâ€”across various smallholder farm typologies. It evaluates baseline practices and seven conservation agriculture (CA) scenarios to identify those that improve yields while reducing GHG emissions. ANOVA and LSD were used for statistical analysis.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Climate mitigation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate change and low-carbon transitions are central; the study directly links its findings to the Paris Agreement and SDG 2, using emissions modeling and climate-resilient farming practices.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Combining cover crops, residue retention, and no-till boosts yields and reduces emissions.

Single CA practices (e.g., no-till alone) have mixed effectsâ€”best outcomes occur when practices are bundled.

The full CA package (FP + NT + CC + RR) yields the highest benefit in yield and lowest emissions.

Modelling reveals the inefficiency of current practices in both emissions and productivity.

A pathway toward climate-smart intensification is possible even with limited synthetic inputs.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Small sample size of farms used to build typologies

Use of default emission factors may not capture all local variability

Results are more comparative than absolute due to modeling assumptions
NOTES:
Funded by the Water Research Commission and NERC

Links directly to the Paris Agreement and SDG 2

Potentially replicable across other smallholder-dominated regions in SSA

TITLE: Fighting to thrive via plant growth regulators: Green chemical strategies for drought stress tolerance
AUTHORS: Raza A., Iqbal N., Raza Q.A., Zhao X., Wei Y., Jaleel H., et al.
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Global â€“ no single regional focus, but relevance is emphasized for arid and semi-arid agricultural zones
METHODS: This is a comprehensive narrative review synthesizing physiological and biochemical pathways involved in drought tolerance through plant growth regulators (PGRs), including hormonal crosstalk, osmolyte accumulation, antioxidant systems, and gene expression. It discusses green chemical strategies, mainly focusing on salicylic acid, gibberellins, cytokinins, ABA, and ethylene modulators, without conducting original experiments.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The article repeatedly links drought as a primary climate stressor and positions PGRs as tools to manage plant responses under ongoing climate change, particularly for agriculture in arid environments.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Drought stress causes significant physiological and oxidative damage in plants.

PGRs like salicylic acid, abscisic acid, and cytokinins can improve stress responses.

Green chemicals are safer and more sustainable than synthetic agrochemicals.

Molecular crosstalk between signaling hormones is key for enhancing drought resilience.

Integration into climate-smart farming could reduce yield loss from water stress.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Limited in vivo validation of some PGR mechanisms

Inconsistent results across species and application rates

Need for large-scale field studies before widespread adoption
NOTES:
Strong advocacy for transitioning to bio-based and sustainable inputs

Discusses global implications, especially for low-rainfall agricultural zones

Proposes molecular markers for future screening of drought tolerance linked to PGR use

TITLE: The Exploitation of Orphan Legumes for Food, Income, and Nutrition Security in Sub-Saharan Africa
AUTHORS: Popoola JO, Aworunse OS, Ojuederie OB, Adewale BD, Ajani OC, Oyatomi OA, Eruemulor DI, Adegboyega TT, Obembe OO
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Sub-Saharan Africa (West, East, and Southern Africa emphasized)
METHODS: This literature-based review evaluates five underutilized legumes in SSAâ€”African yam bean, Bambara groundnut, Kerstingâ€™s groundnut, Lima bean, and Jack beanâ€”highlighting their agronomic potential, nutritional value, and opportunities for commercialization and genetic improvement. It discusses production constraints, value chains, and the use of modern biotechnological tools like omics and genome editing.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The article frames climate change as a major threat to staple crops and positions orphan legumes as climate-smart alternatives with resilience traits like drought tolerance and soil adaptability.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Orphan legumes offer untapped potential for food security, income, and climate resilience.

Lack of investment, hard-to-cook traits, poor awareness, and absence from trade statistics are barriers.

IITA and partners are conserving genetic resources and promoting advanced breeding tools.

Local markets are growing, but international visibility and formal seed systems remain lacking.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Inadequate policy attention, funding, and research focus on orphan legumes

Lack of production and consumption data

Processing and storage infrastructure are major limitations to scaling
NOTES:
Highlights IITAâ€™s leadership in seed conservation and genomics

Aligns with SDG 2 (Zero Hunger) and 13 (Climate Action)

Strong argument for re-centering neglected legumes in food systems

TITLE: Integrating temperature-dependent life table data into Insect Life Cycle Model for predicting the potential distribution of Scapsipedus icipe Hugel & Tanga
AUTHORS: Magara H.J. Otieno, Monica A. Ayieko, Saliou Niassy, Daisy Salifu, Azrag G.A. Abdelmutalab, Khamis M. Fathiya, Sevgan Subramanian, Komi K.M. Fiaboe, Nana Roos, Sunday Ekesi, Chrysantus M. Tanga
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Africa â€“ with model simulations for Eastern, Western, Central, and parts of Southern Africa
METHODS: The study developed and validated temperature-dependent demographic models for Scapsipedus icipe, an edible cricket species, using life table parameters measured at 9 constant temperatures. The models were integrated into the Insect Life Cycle Modeling (ILCYM) platform to project spatial distribution across Africa under current and future climate scenarios. Statistical analyses included ANOVA, regression modeling, and spatial simulations using WorldClim data and IPCC climate projections.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate change is explicitly integrated into modeling scenarios using 2050 projections, guiding adaptive food system development for insect-based protein.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Optimal growth of S. icipe occurs at 30Â°C; development fails at 10Â°C and 40Â°C.

Highest fecundity and population growth were recorded at moderate temperatures, offering clear insights for production system design.

Spatial simulations predict broad potential for establishment across Sub-Saharan Africa.

Introduces life-cycle-based conservation mapping for new edible species.

Demonstrates the utility of ILCYM in informing insect production and climate adaptation planning.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Based on constant temperature regimes; real-world variability not captured

Focused only on thermal factors, excluding humidity and resource limitations

No socioeconomic data on production feasibility or consumer acceptance
NOTES:
Funded by Danida, IDRC, ACIAR, and BioInnovate through icipe

Provides a replicable modeling approach for other new insect species

Suggests insect farming as part of African climate resilience and nutrition strategies

TITLE: Genomic Analysis of Selected Maize Landraces from Sahel and Coastal West Africa Reveals Their Variability and Potential for Genetic Enhancement
AUTHORS: Charles Nelimor, Baffour Badu-Apraku, Ana LuÃ­sa Garcia-Oliveira, Antonia Tetteh, Agre Paterne, Assanvo Simon-Pierre Nâ€™guetta, Melaku Gedil
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: West Africa (Burkina Faso, Ghana, Togo)
METHODS:
Genotyped 208 maize accessions (190 landraces and 18 reference populations) using 5974 high-quality SNP markers from DArTseq.

Diversity indices (He, Ho, PIC, MAF) were estimated using PowerMarker.

Population structure was inferred using STRUCTURE (K = 1 to 12), supported by DAPC, PCoA, and NJ tree clustering.

AMOVA partitioned genetic variation within and between populations; FST and Nm were calculated for differentiation and gene flow.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The article frames landrace diversity as essential for developing maize that can withstand drought and heat, both of which are increasing due to climate changeâ€”emphasizing adaptation but not mitigation.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Three distinct gene pools identified; Burkinabe gene pool was the most genetically differentiated.

High within-population variation (86%) and moderate among-population variation (14%).

Landraces show strong potential for improving stress tolerance in maize.

Reference populations were genetically similar to Ghanaian and Togolese landraces, but not to Burkinabe.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Possible misclassification of old improved varieties as landraces

Lack of eco-geographical data for many accessions

Genetic admixture due to historical germplasm exchange

Sampling bias due to historical collection methods
NOTES:
Suggests Burkinabe gene pool as a priority source for heat and drought tolerance

Supports future GWAS and marker-assisted selection

Data deposited at IITA repository and supported by the STMA project and WASCAL

TITLE: Exploring farmerâ€™s assessment of soil quality and root yield in cassava-based cropping systems
AUTHORS: Samuel Ayodele Mesele, Paul Sobowale Soremi, James Kehinde Adigun
JOURNAL: Journal of the Saudi Society of Agricultural Sciences
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Southwestern Nigeria (Ogun State, specifically Wasimi village)
METHODS: The study used participatory methods (interviews, group discussions, field observations) with 40 cassava farmers to explore indigenous indicators of soil quality and yield estimation. It compared farmersâ€™ yield predictions with actual harvest data and conducted laboratory soil analyses of four representative farm plots. A linear regression was used to compare predicted vs. measured yield.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Farmers attribute yield decline primarily to reduced rainfall. Adaptive practices include early planting and crop diversification.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Farmers use soil colour, drainage, and depth to assess land suitability

Visual yield indicators include soil cracks and leaf/stem appearance

Cassava yield has declined 10â€“50% over the past decade due to low rainfall and poor soils

Farmersâ€™ yield estimates moderately correlate with actual yields (RÂ² = 0.62)

Soil analyses confirmed low fertility across study sites

Integrating traditional knowledge with scientific metrics can strengthen agricultural interventions
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Weather data were unavailable to validate rainfall-related yield trends

Yield estimation accuracy varied widely among farmers

Soil data were limited to four farm sites
NOTES:
Strong emphasis on farmer inclusion and participatory research

No external funding was received for the research

Study underscores missed opportunities when farmer knowledge is excluded from intervention design

TITLE: Framework for Incorporating Gender Equality and Social Inclusion (GESI) Elements in Climate Information Services (CIS)
AUTHORS: Everisto Mapedza, Sophia Huyer, Nitya Chanana, Alison Rose, Inga Jacobs-Mata, Netsayi Noris Mudege, Sabine Homann-Kee Tui, Sika Gbegbelegbe, Gloriose Nsengiyumva, Munyaradzi Mutenje, Ngowenani Nohayi
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Zambia (with reference to Ethiopia, Ghana, Kenya, Mali, and Senegal under AICCRA)
METHODS: The paper uses a literature review approach to propose a gender equality and social inclusion (GESI) framework for Climate Information Services (CIS), grounded in the ongoing AICCRA project. The framework is structured around five core indicators: gender targeting by design, sex-disaggregated data collection and analysis, dissemination of technology, and continuous gender monitoring and empowerment evaluation.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs

Gender equality, youth, and social inclusion
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate variability and its disproportionate impacts on women are the foundation for the GESI-CIS framework. The goal is to make adaptation tools more accessible and equitable.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Women are systematically excluded from CIS due to socio-cultural, institutional, and systemic barriers

Gendered access to communication technologies is critical for effective CIS delivery

Framework operationalized in Zambia via agricultural data hubs, flood/drought indicators, and training modules

Five indicators proposed to track inclusion: targeting, data, analysis, dissemination, and empowerment evaluation

Emphasizes transformative change beyond inclusionâ€”targeting structural gender norms
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
CIS alone is insufficient to empower womenâ€”norms and structural inequalities must also be addressed

Context-specific challenges in each country require flexible framework adaptation
NOTES:
Funded by the World Bank via AICCRA (P173398)

Builds directly on CGIAR-CCAFS legacy research

Highlights digital inequality and the intersectionality of gender with age, class, literacy, and disability

TITLE: Interactive effects of altitude, microclimate and shading system on coffee leaf rust
AUTHORS: Theresa Liebig, Fabienne Ribeyre, Peter LÃ¤derach, Hans-Michael Poehling, Piet van Asten, Jacques Avelino
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Uganda (Mt. Elgon region: Bulambuli, Kapchorwa, Sironko districts)
METHODS: This study examined how altitude and coffee shading systems influence the microclimate and Coffee Leaf Rust (CLR) incidence in Arabica coffee agroecosystems in Uganda. Field data (e.g., leaf rust incidence, temperature, RH, dew point) were collected over a growing season across 49 plots at three altitudes and with varying shade configurations. The authors used piecewise structural equation modeling (SEM) to assess causal relationships between environmental factors, shading systems, microclimate, and CLR development.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Strong climate-relevance: connects microclimatic variation across altitudes and seasons with Coffee Leaf Rust severity. Climate change implications for disease outbreaks are implied.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
CLR incidence is significantly influenced by altitude, shade system, and microclimate (e.g., dew point, night temperature, RH)

Higher altitudes and certain shade configurations (e.g., coffee-tree systems) are associated with lower CLR incidence

Shade effects on microclimate are complex and vary with altitude

Diurnal temperature range and humidity are key disease drivers

Shade system effects may mitigate or exacerbate CLR depending on environmental context
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Did not assess yield or socio-economic outcomes

Study area-specific results may not generalize across all agroecological zones

Other biotic and abiotic interactions not included in final model
NOTES:
Funded by BMZ and implemented under CCAFS

Collaborators include IITA, CIAT, CIRAD, and local farmers

Avelino and van Asten are long-standing contributors to coffee agroecology and systems disease management

TITLE: How future climatic uncertainty and biotic stressors might influence the sustainability of African vegetable production
AUTHORS: J.D.H. Keatinge, D.R. Ledesma, J.dâ€™A. Hughes, F.J.D. Keatinge, S. Hauser, P.C.S. Traore
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Pan-African (locations include Nigeria, Kenya, Mali, Tunisia, etc.)
METHODS: This review analyzes long-term temperature and rainfall data (1975â€“2014 and up to 100 years where available) across 13 African countries. It assesses climate trends and predicts future warming scenarios. The paper focuses on how increasing temperatures and climate variability will impact biotic stressors (pests, diseases, weeds) in African vegetable production systems, especially for smallholder farmers.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs

Gender equality, youth, and social inclusion
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Temperature trends are projected for 25â€“100 years, and future risks to vegetable production are linked to increased pests, diseases, and weed pressures.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Climate change will exacerbate crop losses via biotic stressors

Rainfall trends remain highly variable and statistically insignificant

Localized responses neededâ€”climate trends vary by site

Temperature rise projected up to +6.87Â°C by 2115 in Tunisia; +1.11Â°C in Ibadan (IITA)

Highlights the need for temperature-adapted breeding and disease monitoring

Policy and funding should prioritize adaptive horticulture and farmer resilience
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Long-term rainfall data were scarce and trends were inconclusive

Highly variable local conditions limit regional generalizability

Did not include field-level pest or crop performance data
NOTES:
Strong integration of historical meteorological analysis

Supports Sustainable Development Goal 2 via improved horticulture

IITA and AVRDC data sources contributed to the climate analysis

TITLE: Habitat range shift and prediction of the potential future distribution of Ricinodendron heudelotii (Baill.) Heckel in Benin (West Africa)
AUTHORS: Guillaume Hounsou-Dindin, Rodrigue Idohou, Paterne Agre, Achille HounkpÃ¨vi, Aristide Cossi Adomou, Achille Ephrem Assogbadjo, Romain GlÃ¨lÃ¨ KakaÃ¯
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Benin (with emphasis on humid and sub-humid zones)
METHODS: Species distribution modeling (SDM) using an ensemble of four algorithms (RF, GBM, GLM, MAXENT) in the biomod2 framework was applied to current and projected climate and soil data (RCP 4.5 and 8.5 scenarios) to assess the present and future distribution of Ricinodendron heudelotii in Benin. Occurrence records were collected from field sampling and GBIF, and a predictive model was developed to identify suitable conservation and domestication zones.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Environmental health and biodiversity

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate change is central. RCP 4.5 and 8.5 scenarios are used to project shifts in suitable habitat zones through 2085.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Currently, 4.43% of Benin is highly suitable for R. heudelotii

Under both RCP scenarios, humid zone habitat suitability declines, while sub-humid zones gain importance

Model performance was very high (AUC = 0.994; TSS = 0.925)

Environmental variables (isothermality, rainfall, and soil sand content) are key predictors

Results inform national planning for climate-smart forest and agroforestry management
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
No modeling of biotic interactions, dispersal, or landscape connectivity

SDM limitations: does not predict actual presence, only suitability

No socioeconomic constraints modeled (e.g., land access, institutional barriers)
NOTES:
Recommends ex situ strategies (seedling propagation, assisted migration)

Important case study for climate-resilient agroforestry planning in West Africa

TITLE: Exâ€ante evaluation of promising soybean innovations for subâ€Saharan Africa
AUTHORS: Sika Gbegbelegbe, Arega Alene, Alpha Kamara, Keith Wiebe, Victor Manyong, Tahirou Abdoulaye, Petros Mkandawire
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Sub-Saharan Africa (including country-level focus on Nigeria, Malawi, Zambia, Zimbabwe, Uganda, Benin, Ethiopia, Cameroon, CÃ´te d'Ivoire, Gabon, etc.)
IITA OFFICE: IITA Malawi, IITA Nigeria, IITA Tanzania
METHODS: The study uses a combination of crop modeling, hydrology modeling, and the IMPACT economic model to assess how soybean innovations (e.g., improved varieties, increased acreage, and expanded processing capacity) may reduce import dependency and enhance production in sub-Saharan Africa under future climate scenarios.
IMPACT AREAS:
Climate adaptation

Nutrition, health, and food security

Poverty reduction, livelihoods, and jobs
ACTION AREAS:
Resilient agrifood systems

Systems transformation

Genetic Innovation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: Climate change is central to the modeling exercise. Yield impacts under RCP 8.5 are used to define risks and adaptation needs. The HadGEM2-ES model is used to simulate future drought-related stress.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
SSAâ€™s soybean import dependency is projected to worsen without intervention

Yield-enhancing tech alone insufficient; acreage and processing must also double

Combined innovations could make SSA self-sufficient in soybean by 2050

High-value recommendations for policy (e.g., subsidies, floor prices, contract farming)
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS:
Does not include biotic stress effects

No income or poverty effects modeled (partial equilibrium model)

Assumes homogeneous technology uptake scenarios
NOTES:
Authors note need for CGE modeling to understand income and poverty outcomes

Modeling includes all major soybean-producing countries in SSA

Supported by CGIAR-PIM and Gates Foundation

TITLE: Rhizobium inoculant, nitrogen, and phosphorus improves photosynthesis and water use efficiency in soybean under drought stress
AUTHORS: Singh, M., Singh, V., Khera, D. et al.
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Nigeria
METHODS: Greenhouse pot experiments were conducted using soybean varieties, applying rhizobium inoculation, nitrogen, and phosphorus treatments. Photosynthesis and water use efficiency were measured under induced drought and well-watered conditions to assess physiological responses.
IMPACT AREAS: Climate adaptation
NOTES ON CLIMATE: The paper investigates physiological mechanisms (photosynthesis, water use) through which soybean can become more drought-resilient, explicitly positioning the research within climate adaptation.
THEMES/FINDINGS:
Inoculation with rhizobium plus nitrogen and phosphorus significantly enhanced photosynthesis and water use efficiency.

The combined treatment improved drought tolerance in soybean, suggesting a viable strategy to sustain productivity under climate stress.
LIMITATIONS FROM AUTHORS: Greenhouse study may not fully replicate field conditions; long-term effects not evaluated.
NOTES: Strong physiological focus with clear implications for adaptation but not broader food system themes.

TITLE: Forest governance and REDD+ in Central Africa: Towards a participatory model to increase stakeholder involvement in carbon markets
AUTHORS: Adrien N. Djomo, J. Andrew Grant, Celestine Fonyikeh-Bomboh Lucha, Julie Tchoko Gagoe, NoÃ«l H. Fonton, Neal Scott, Denis Jean Sonwa
GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS: Central Africa (Congo Basin countries: Cameroon, DRC, Gabon, Republic of Congo, Central African Republic, Equatorial Guinea)
METHODS: The paper provides a literature-based and policy review of REDD+ initiatives in the Congo Basin, analyzing carbon stock monitoring, governance structures, stakeholder participation, and implementation challenges. It includes historical data synthesis, silvicultural system design, and proposed carbon accounting methodologies. The authors propose a participatory model for engaging local communities in carbon markets.
